Verdict  of guilty.

Set  aside  and  new  trial.

10

Burwell  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  laborer,  who  stands
jointly  indicted  with  Lee  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  laborer,
was,  again  led  to  the bar  in  the  eustody  of  the  jailor of the  court,
and  the  jury,  sworn  over  the  first  day  of  the  term  the  truth  of and
in court  in charge of H.  C.
upon  the  premises  to  speak,  appeared
Clark  and  A.  W.  Houchins,  deputy  sheriffs,  pursuant  to  their  adjourn-
ment,  and  were  sent  to  their  room  to  consult  on  a  verdict,  and  after
some  time  came  into  court,  having  found  the  following  verdict,  to  wit,
‘*  We,  the  jary,  find  the  prisoner,  Burwell
Reynolds,  guilty  of  murder
in  the  first  degree,  in  manner  and  form  as  charged  in  the  indietment.”

Whereupon,  on  motion  of  the  said  Burwell  Reynolds,  by counsel,  and
for  reasons  appearing  to  the  court,  it  is  ordered  that  the  said  verdict  be
set  aside  and  that  a  new  trial  be  had  upon  the  said  indictment  at  the
next  term,  and  the  prisoner  is  remanded  to jail.

At  another  day,  to  wit,  at  a  special  term  of  the  circuit  court  for  the
county of Patrick,  held  at  the  court-house  thereot, on the 25th day  of
October,  1373:

Motion  for  mixed  jury,  and  removal  renered  and  overruled,

Burwell  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  laborer,  and  Lee
Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  laborer,  who  stand  indicted  of
murder,  were  led  to  the  bar  in  custody  of  the jailor  of  this  court,  and  the
prisoners,  the  said  Burwell  Reynolds  aad  the  said  Lee  Reynoids,  be-
fore  the  empanneling  of  the  jury  for  their  trial  at  this  term,  charged
with  the  murder  of  Aaron  C.  Shelton,  moved  the  court  to  remove  said
prosecution  to  the  next  term  of  the  United  States  cireuit  court,  to  be
held  in  Danville,  Virginia,  upon  the  ground  set  forth  in  their  petition,
presented  and  Sled  at  the  April  term,  1575,  of  the  court;  which  motion
was  again  overruled  and  the  prayer  of  the  petition  refused.

Burieell’s  second  trial.

The  prisoners  electing  to  be  tried  separately,  Burwell  Reynolds  was
put  upon  his  trial;  whereupon  a  panel  of  sixteen  jurors,  selected  from
the  twenty-four  summoned  by  the  sheriff,  were  examined  by  the  court
and  found  free  trem  all  legal  exceptions,  and  qualified  to  serve  as  jurors
according  to  law,  and  the  prisoner,  Burwell  Reynolds,  having.  erase
trom  the  said  pannel  four  of  said  jurors,  b.  R.  Hall,  Wm.  M.  Frances,
Walker  Carter,  Daniel  G.  Hatcher,  M.  E.  Lewis,  Z.  T.  Turner,  John
Tuggle,  Richard  Cochram,  Wilson  J.  West,  B.  P.  Paee,  E.  P.  Newman,
and  George  RK.  King,  to  whom  there  was  found  no  objeetion,  being
sworn,  the  truth  of  and  upon  the  premises  to  speak,  having  partly  heard

to-morrow  morning,  10  o'clock;  whereupon  the  jury  was  given  in charge

they  unanimously  replied  there  was  none;  whereupon,  by  consent,  it
was  ordered  that  D.  G.  Hatcher,  one  of  the  jurors  aforesaid,  be  with-
drawn,  and  the  rest,  from  rendering  their  verdict,  discharged;  and  the
prisoner  is remanded  to jail.

And  at  another  day,  to  wit,  at  a  circuit  court  continued  and  held  for
the  county  of  Patrick,  at  the  court-house  thereof,  on  the  5th  day  of
April,  1878.

Verdict  of guilty  as  to  Lee  Reynolds.

Lee  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  laborer,  who  stands
jointly  indicted  with  Burwell  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,
laborer,  of  murder,  was  again  led  to  the  bar  in  custody  of  the  jailor  of
this  court,  and  the  jury  sworn  on  yesterday  the  truth  of  and  upon  the
premises  to  speak,  was  brought  into  court  in  charge  of R. M.  McArthur
and  A.  W.  Houchins,  deputy  sheriffs,  pursuant  to  their  adjournment,
and  were  sent  to  their  room;  and  after  some  time  were  brought  into
court  in  charge  of  deputy  sheriff,  having  found  the  following  verdict,  to
wit:  We,  the  jury,  find  the  prisoner,  Lee  Reynolds,  guilty  of  murder  in
the  second  degree,  in  manner.and  form  as  charged  in  the  indictment,
and  ascertain  the  term  of  his confinement  in  the  penitentiary  tg  be  the
period  of  fifteen  years.

Thereupon  the  prisoner,  Lee  Reynolds,  by  counsel,  moved  the  court
to  set  aside  the  verdict  and  grant  him  a  new  trial,  and  the  cvurt  takes
time,  and  the  prisoner,  Lee  Reynolds,  is  remanded  to jail.

And  at  another  day,  to  wit,  at  a  circuit  court  coutinued  and  held  for
the  county  of  Patrick,  at  the  court-house  thereof,  on  the  Sth  day  of
April,  1878.

Lee  Reynolds’  motion  for  a  new  trial  overruled.

Sentenced.

Lee  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  laborer,  who  was  jointly
indicted  for  murder  with  one  Burwell  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of
Patrick,  laborer,  was  led  to  the  bar  in custody  of  the  jailor  of this  court,
and  the  court  having  considered  the  prisoner’s  motion  for  a  new  trial,
the  same,  for  reasons  appearing  to  the  court,  is overruled.

13

And  it  being  demanded  of  the  prisoner  if anything  for  himself  he had
or  knew  to  say  why  the  court  should  not  now  proceed  to  pro-
nounce  judgment  against  him  according  to  law,  and  nothing  be-
ing  offered  in  delay  of judgment,  it  is  considered  by  the  court
that  the  said  Lee  Reynolds  be  imprisoned  in  the  penitentiary  of  the
commonwealth  for  the  term  of  fifteen  years,  to  be  treated  in  the  manner
prescribed  by  law.

And  the  sheriff  of this  county  is ordered,  as  soon  as  possible  after  the
adjournment  of  this  court,  to  remove  and  convey  the  said  Lee  Reynolds
trom  the  jail  of  this  county  to the  penitentiary  of  this  commonwealth,

:

earred  about  two  weeks  before  the  homicide.

ind  hallooed  school-butter.

Mary  Burwell,  one  of  the  witnesses  for  the  defence,  informed  Burwell
and  Lee  Reynolds  that  day  of  the  manner  in  which  Aaron  C,  Shelton

EX  PARTE  COMMONWEALTH  OF  VIRGINIA.

13

had  abused  their  sister;  late  in  the  evening  of  the  same  day  Aaron  C.
Shelton  returning  home  from  his  work  found  Barwell  and  Lee  Reynolds
trying  to  roll  one  of  the  said  logs  eut  by  lis  unele,  which  he  was  going
to  carry  to  the  saw  mill,  out  of  the  road—a  road  had  been  eut  by  the
nneie  of  Aaron  C.  Shelton  by  which  any  one  could  pass  around  the
logs—Shelton  asked  Burwell  and  Lee  Reynolds  what  they  were  doing;
they  said  they  were  going  to  roll  the  logs  out  of  the  road;  he  enursed
them,  and  told  them  if  they  did  he  would  thrash  them;  Burwell  and
Lee  Reynolds  passed  on  around  the  log  after  the  threat  to  the  negro
school-house  before  mentioned,  which  was  on  their  road  home,  and  there
stopped,  and  when  Aaron  C.  Shelton  arrived  at  that  place,  which  was
also‘on  his  road  home,  he  found  them  standing  on  the  roadside  with  a
stick  and  axe;  they  addressed  Shelton,  and  Lee  Reynolds  told  him  they
were  going  back  to  the  log  at  which  he  had  first  found  them  the  next
morning,  and  were  going  to  roll  it  out  of  the  road,  and  if  he,  Shelton,
interfered  they  would  shoot  him,  and  if  he  ran  they  would  make  their
This  threat  was  denied  by  Burwell  Reynolds,  who  was
dogs  catch  him.
Green  Shelton,  the  brother  of  Aaron  C.  Shel-
introduced  as  a  witness.
ton,  was  the  witness  to  this  threat.
Aaron  C.  Shelton  passed  the  school-
house  on  Wednesday  and  Thursday,  tor  the  purpose  of  hauling  the  saw-
logs  which  had  been  cut,  without  any  further  interference  with  the
school.

On  Thursday,  about  three  o’clock,  he  went  to  load  a  log on  his  wagon,
which  was  lying  in  the  road  about  a  quarter  of  a  mile  from  the  log  Bur-
well  and  Lee  were  trying  to  roll  out  of  the  road;  when  he  get  in  sight
of  the  log  he  intended  loading  he  saw  Burwell  and  Lee  Reynolds  stand-
ing  within  ten  or  fifteen  steps  of  the  log;  Burwell  and  Lee  Reynolds
had  been  hauling  corn  from  their  father’s  farm,  and  were  returning  home
by  their  usual  route,  and  had  driven  their  slide,  to  which  was  attached
a  horse  and  oxen,  outside  of  the  road;  as  they  came  up  to  this  point
hey  found  a  log  lying  across  the  road;  they  passed  around  the  log
through  the  space  made  by  Shelton  iv  removing  one  of  the  saw-logs  be-
ow  the  road,  and  stopped  where  they  were  first  seen  by  Shelton;  they
leould  have  seen  Shelton  at  a  distance  of eighty  yards  in  the  road  before
he  reached  them,  and  could  have  heard  him  coming  about  two  hundred
yards;  when  Shelton  first  came  in  sight  with  his  wagon,  his  brother
Green  with  him,  he  came  in  a  fast  walk,  the  track  was  a  single  track
with  a  fence  on  one  side  and  woods  on  the  other,  and  two  such  vehicles
Burwell  Rey-
could  not  pass  each  other  without  one  giving  the  road.
nolds,  who  was  driving  the  slide  with  some  little  loading  in  it,  left  the
main  track  and  drove  his  slide  out  of  the  road  down  below,  and  was
tound  with  the  heads  of  the  ox  and  horse  turned  in  a  direction  nearly
diagonally  with  the  road;  the  wagon  was  found  in  the  road,  exactly
opposite  the  rear  of  the  slide;  between  the  wagon  and:slide,  directly
below  the  fore  and  hind  wheels  of  the  wagon,  Lee  Reynolds  was  found
stunding  with  a  gun  and  stick.
Shelton  stopped  his  wagon  about
fourteen  paces  from  the  log  which  he  intended  loading;  Shelton
had  hauled  one  log  the  day  before,  and  stopped  his  wagon  at  a
position  further  from  the  log  than  he  did  on  this  day.
There  was  a  log
across  the  road;  when  Shelton  hauled  the  first  log  he  could  have
stopped  at  that  point  and  loaded,  or  could  have  gone  further  and  turned
around  and  then  loaded.
When  Shelton  stopped  his  wagon  he  asked
Lee
what  he  was  doing  with  that  stick  —there  was  a  conflict  of  testimony
as  to  whether  he  said  he  had  a  right  to  carry  his  stick,  or  whether  he
said  “af  you  will  get  down,  God  damn  you,  |  will  show  you.”
Shelton
The  testimony
ordered  hits  to  divop  the  stick,  and  got  out  of  the  wagon.

WM.  M.  TREDWAY.  [SEAL.!

In  the  clerk’s  office  of  the  cireuit  court  for  the  county  of  Patrick,  at
the  court-house  of  said  county,  in  the  vacation  of said  court,  on  the  Lith
day  of  October,  1878,  the  following  mandate  was  received  and  entered
in  the  words  and  figures  following:

VIRGINIA:

At  a  supreme  court  of  appeals  beld  at  the  court  house  of  Wythe  County,
in  the  town  of  Wytheville,  the  30th  day  of  July,  1878.

Judgment  of supreme  court  of appeals.

E  REYNOLDS,  PLAINTIFF  IN  ERROR,

against

)
>

THE  COMMONWEALTH,  DEFENDANT  IN  ERROR.  \

Upon  a  writ  of  error  toa  judgment  of  the  circuit  court  of  Patriek
County,  rendered  on  the  5th  day of April,  1878,  on  au  indictment  of  the
said  plaintiff  in  error  for  murder.

16

This  day  came  again  as  well  the  said  plaintiff  in error,  by his  counsel,
as  the  Commonwealth,  by  the  attorney-general,  and  the  court  having
maturely  considered  the  transcript  of  the  record  of  the judgment
aforesaid,  and  the  arguments  of counsel,  is of opinion,  for  reasons
stated  in writing and  filed  with  the  record,  that  the  facts  certified
as  proved  on  the  trial  did  not  warrant  the  jury  in  finding  the  plaintitf
in  error,  Lee  Reynolds,  guilty  of the  homicide  charged  in the  indictment,
and,  therefore,  the court  erred  in  overruling  the  motion  of said  plaintiff
in  error  to  set  aside  the  verdict  and  grant  him  a  new  trial,  because  the
verdict  was  against  the  evidence.

Therefore  it is considered,  ordered,  and  adjudged  that  the  said  jadg-
ment  of the  said  circuit  court  be  reversed  and  annulled,  the  said  verdic
set  aside,  and  the  cause  be  remanded  to  the  said  circuit  court  for  a  new
trial  to  be  had  therein  of the  said  plaintiff  in  error,  Lee  Reynolds,  upon
the  said  indictment.

Clerk  of Patrick  Cireuit  Court.

At  another  day,  to  wit,  at  a  special  term  of  the  cireuit  court  for  the
county  of  Patrick,  held  at  the  court-house  thereof,  on'the  28th  day  of
October,  1878:

Burwell  Reynolds,  late

of  the  county  of  Patrick,  laborer,  and
Lee  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  laborer,  who  stand  in-
dicted  of  murder,  were  led  to  the  bar  in  custody  of  the  jailor  of  this
court;  and  the  said  Burwell  Reynolds  and  the  said  Lee  Reynolds,  be-
fore  the  empannelling  of the  jury  for  their  trial  at  this  term,  charged
with  the  murder  of  Aaron  C.  Shelton,  moved  the  court  to  remove  the
said  prosecution  to  the  next  term  of  the  United  States  circuit  court,  to
be  held  in  Danville,  Virginia,  upon  the  ground  set  forth  in  their  petition
presented  and  filed  at  the  April  term,  1878,  of this  court;  which  motion
was  again  overruled  and  the  prayer  of  the  petitioners  refused.

The  prisoners  having  elected  to  be  tried  separately,  Burwell  Reynolds

was  put  upon  his  trial,  \c.,  and  the  prisoner  remanded  to  jail.

And  at  another  day,  to  wit,  at  a  special  term  of  the  circuit  court  of
Patrick  County.  continued  and  held  for  the  county  of  Patrick,  at  the
court-house  thereof,  on  the  30th  day  of  October,  1873.

Lee  Reynolds’  second  trial.

Lee  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  laborer,  who  was  jointly
indicted  of  murder  with  one  Burwell  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of
Patrick,  laborer,  was  again  led  to  the  bar  in  custody  of  the  jailor  of  this
court.
Whereupon  a  pannel  of  sixteen  jurors,  selected  from  those  sum-
moned  by the  sheriff,  were  examined  by  the  court  and  found  free  from
all  legal  exceptions  and  qualified  to  serve  as  jurors  according  to  law  ;
and  the  prisoner  having  erased  from  the  panel  four  of  said  jurors,  L.
G.  Joyce,  W.  W.  Stoops,  C.  J.  Dalton,  W.  H.  Wimbish,  W.  C.
Lackey,  Sparrel  Wood,  C.  W.  Plasters,  J.  P.  McGhee,  A.  J.  Dal-
ton,  Isham  Barnard,  McD.  Ross,  and  James  H.  Rangeley,  being
sworn  the  truth  of  and  upon  the  premises  to  speak,  and  having  partly
heard  the  evidence,  the  turther  prosecution  of  the  cause  is  concluded
until  to-morrow  morning,  9 o’clock.

17

|

Thereupon  the  jury  were  given  in  charge  of A.  W.  Houchins  and  D.
E. Conner,  deputy  sherifts, to whom  was  administered  the  usual  oath,  and
directed  to  take  the  said  jury  out  of  court  to  some  convenient  room,
there  to  be  kept  together  without  communication  with  any  one  and  to
be  brought  into  court  at  the  time  aforesaid;  and  the  prisoner  is remanded
to  jail.

And  now  at  this  day,  to  wit,  at  a  special  term  of  the  cireuit  court  of
Patrick  County,  continued  and  held  at  the  court-house  thereof,  on  the
3dlst  day  of  October,  1873.

Second  verdict  of guilty  of Lee,  and  sentence.

Lee  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  laborer,  who  with

16

EX  PARTE  COMMONWEALTH  OF  VIRGINIA,

Burwell  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  laborer,  is  jointly  in-
dicted  for  murder,  Was  again  led  to  the  bar  in  custody  of  the  tailor
of  this  court;  and  the  jury  sworn  on  yesterday  thestrath  of  and  apon
the  premises  to  speak,  and  adjourned  over  until  this  morning,  9  o’clock,
came  into  court  in  charge  of  A,  W.  Tlouchins  and  D.  KE.  Conner,  deputy
sheriffs,  pursuant  to  their  adjournment,  and  having  fully  heard  the  evi
dence  and  arguments  of  counsel,  were  sent  to  their  room  to  consult  of
their  verdict,  and  after  some  time  was  brought  into  court,  having  found
the  following  verdict,  to  wit:  **  We,  the  jury,  find  the  prisoner,  Lee
Reynolds,  guilty  of  murder  in  the  second  degree,  and  ascertaiv  the  term
of  his  continement  in  the  penitentiary  to  be  eighteen  years.”

Whereupon  the  prisoner  moved  the  court  to  set  aside  the  verdict  and
grant  him  a  new  trial;  which  motion  being  heard  the  same  is overruled  ;
and  it  being  asked  of  the  prisoner  if anything  for  himself  he  had  or  knew
to  say  why  this  court  should  not  now  proceed  to  pronounce  judgment
against  him  according  to  law,  and  nothing  being  offered  or  alleged  in
delay  of judgment,  it  is  considered  by  the  court  that  the  said  Lee  Rey-
nolds  be  imprisoned  in  the  penitentiary  of  this  commonwealth  for  the
term  of eighteen  years;  and  the  sheriff  of  this  county  is  ordered  as  soon
as  possible  after  the  adjournment  of  this  court  to  remove  and  convey  the
said  Lee  Reynolds  from  the  jail  of  this  county  to  the  penitentiary  of  this
commonwealth,  therein  to  be  kept  imprisoned  and  treated  in  the  man-
ner  directed  by  law  for  the  term  aforesaid.

Memorandum.

The  prisoner,  by  counsel,  excepted  to  the  opinion  of  the  court  over-
ruling  his  motion  for  a  new  trial,  and  tendered  his  bill,  which  was  signed
by  the  court  and  ordered  to  be  made  a  part  of  the  record  iu  this  cause.

Bill  of exceptions  and  facts.

Be  it  remembered  that  at  the  trial  of  this  cause,  after  the  verdict  of
the  jury,  the  defendant  moved  the  court  to  set  aside  the  verdiet  and
award  a  new  trial,  because  it  was  against  the  law  and  evidence;  which
motion  was  overruled;  to  which  opinion  of  the  court  the  prisoner,  by
counsel,  excepted,  and  prays  the  court  to  certify  the  facts  proven;  and
the  court  doth  certify  the  following  as  all  the  facts  proven:

18

That  A.  C.  Shelton  had  become  provoked  some  time  preceding  the
homicide  because  a  younger  brother  of  his  had  been  ducked  by
the  children  of a  negro  school  for  hallooing  school  butter,  of  which
His  brother,  Burwell  Rey-
schoo!  Lee  Reynolds  was  a  member.
nolds,  who  was  jointly  indicted  with  him,  took  an  active  part  iu  the  duck-
ing;  this  took  place  about  three  weeks  before  the  homicide.

On  Tuesday  morning  before  the  homicide  Aaron  C.  Shelton  passed
the  school - house  in  which  the  school  was  then  in  session,  hallooed  school-
butter,  aud  passed  on  to  his  work  without  any  disturbance,  about  four
hundred  yards  to  where  he  was  going  to  load  his  wagon  with  saw  logs,  in
Which  business  he  was  at  that  time  engaged.
At  recess of the  scool  on
the  same  day,  the  teacher  having  left  his  school  in  charge  of  one  of  the
grown  aud  advane2d  scholars,  another  brother  of  Aaron  ©.  Shelton,
about  thirteen  years  of  age,  approached  the  school  house  at  a  distance
of  150  yards,  and  hallooed  school-butter;  the  members  of  the  schoo!
then  pursued  him  until  they  arrived  at  where  Aaron  C.  Shelton  was
standing  in  the  road  with  astick  in  his  hand,  his  uncle,  Asa  Tugele,  be-
ing  present;  this  place  was  about  four  hundred  yards  trom  the  plaes

17

where  his  saw-logs  were,  in  the  direction  of the  schoolt-house.
AaronC.
Shelton  having  understood  some  one  to  call  him  a  rogne,  approached
the  children  and  asked  who  called  him  a  rogue,  and  he  was  told  by one
of  the  school  children  that  it  was  Puss Reynolds,  the  sister  of  Lee  and
Burwell  Reynolds;  whereupon  some  altercation  took  place  between
Shelton  and  the  school,  in  which  he  abused  Puss  Reynolds;  the  school
children  then  turned  back  to  the  school-house,  Aaron  C.  Shelton  follow-
ing  them  with  a  rock  and  stick,  stating  to  them  that  bis  brother  had
been  ducked  a  short  time  before,  and  he  might  pass  there  when  he
damned  pleased  and  halloo  school-butter,  and if  he  was  ducked  again
he  would  shoot  their  heart-strings  out,  and  if  the  teacher  interfered  he
would  shoot  him,  if  he  had  to  follow  him  into  the  school  house;  neither
Lee  nor  Burwell  Reynolds  were  present  on  this  oceasion,  but  Aaron  C,
Shelton  shook  a  stick  over  the  head  of  Puss  Reynolds,  a  sister  of  Lee
and  Burwell  Reynolds,  and  asked  her  who  it  was  that  called  him  a
rogue;  she  said  no  one,  and  asked  Shelton  what  all  this  meant;  he  re-
plied,  ‘‘one  of  your  brothers  ducked  my  little  brother  some  time  ago  ;”
she  said,  her  brother  *  did  not  duck  his  brother,  but  only  patted  a  little
water  on  his  head  ;”  Shelton  then  said  he  would  pat  for  him,  aud  used
abusive  language  to  her,  which  the  witness  declined  to  repeat  in  court.
Mary  Burwell,  one  of  the  witnesses  for  the  defence,  informed  Burwell
and  Lee  Reynolds  that  evening  of  the  manner  iu  which  Aaron  C.  Shel-
ton  had  abused  their  sister;  late  in  the  evening  of  the  same  day  Shel-
ton,  returning  home  from  his  work,  found  Burwell  and  *Lee  Reynolds
trying  to  roll  one  of  the  saw-logs  that  had  been  cut  by his’uncle,  which
heintended  carrying  to  the  saw-mill,  out  of  a  road  down  a  hill;  aroad  had
been  cut  by the  uncle  of  Shelton  by which  any  one  could  pass  around  the
log  without  any  inconvenience.
Shelton  asked  Lee  Reynolds  what  he
was  doing ; he said  they  were  going  to  roll  that  log out  of the  road.  Shel-
ton  cursed  them,  and  told  them  if  they  did  he  would  thrash  them.
Lee
and  Burwell  then  passed  on  around  the  log  in  the  direction  of  the  school-
house,  and  Shelton  stopped  at  the  log  about  a  quarter  of  an  hour,  and
When  he  got  to
then  went  on  in  the  same  direction,  on  his  way  home.
the  school-huuse  he  found  Lee  and  Burwell  standing  on  the  side  of  the
road,  from  which  they  had  tarned  to  allow  Shelton  to  pass,  with  an  axe
which  they  used  at  the  sehool-house.
Lee  addressed  Shelton,  and  told
him  that  he  and  Burwell  were  going  back  to  the  log  at  which  he  had
just  found  them  the  next  morning,  aud  were  going  to  roll  that  log  out,
and  if he  interfered  with  him  they  would  shoot  him,  and  if  he ran
they  would  make  their  dogs  cateh  him.

19

This  threat  was  denied by  Burwell  Reynolds,  who  was  iutro-
duced  as  a  witness,  and  stated  that  Shelton  stopped  in  the  road  and
said  he  had  not  forgotten  him  for  ducking  my  brother.
Green  Shelton,
the  brother  of  Aaron  C.  Shelton,  was  the  witness  to  this  threat.

‘hauling  his  logs  without  interfering  with  the  school.

Aarou  C.  Shelton  passed  the  school-house  on  Wednesday  and  Thursday
Ou  Wednesday
Shelton  received  a  message,  purporting  to  come  from  Lee  Reynolds,  say-
ing  that  heintended  to  move  that  log, and  that  he  bad  his  gun  with
him,  and  if  he,  Shelton,  interfered  with him  he  would  shoot  him.  On
Wednesday  morning,  following  the  past  difficulty  on  the  Tuesday  be-
fore,  the  prisoner,  w ith his  brother,  Burwell  Reynolds,  went  back  to  their
farm;  on  their  way  they  were  informed  by  Asa  Tuggle,  uncle  of the  de-
ceased,  that  Aaron  C.  Shelton  told  him  to  tell  them  that  if  he  caught

Tt  was  cut

their  farm,  finished  their  work.  and  about  o>  o'clock  p.m.  were  return.
ing  the  same  road,  Burwell  driving  the  slide  with  some  little  loag-
Ing  on  it;  the  prisoner  was  walking  in  front  with  agun  and  stick,  They
eame  toa  tree  across  the  road  which  had  been  cut  by  Tuggle  since  they
went  down  in  the  morning,
into  saw-logs;  the  second  ent
had  been  hauled  away;  there  was  one  aeross  the  road  ;  they  moved  the
log  out  of  the  road  to  pass  with  the  slide,  and  while  in  the  act  of  mov-
ing,  Asa  Tuggle,  who  had  eut  the  log  and  who  was  engaged  for  that
purpose  by  Shelton,  said:  *  Now  boys,  you  have  moved  that  log,  and
you  must  take  the  result.”
Burwell  drove  on  up  the  road,  which  was  a
single  track,  about  fifteen  paces,  turned  short  around  a  tree  into  the
brush  out  of  the  road.
The  deceased  drove  his  wagon  up  opposite  the
rear  of  the  slide  in  the  only  track  a  wagon  could  have  passed,  and
stopped  at  least  five  or  ten  paces  before  it was  necessary  to  have  stopped
in  order  to  load  his  log,  but  had  stopped  further  from  the  log  the  day
before,  and  the  slide  could  have  passed  out  behind  the  wagon  after  it
stopped.
There  was  a  conflict  of  testimony  at  this  point  as  to  how  the
difticulty  occurred,  whether  Lee  Reynolds  drew  the  stick  upon  the  de-
ceased,  or  whether  the  deceased  ordered  him  to  drop  it;  but  that  the
deceased  did  get  the  stick  into  his  possession,  and  that  Lee  Reynolds
backed  and  the  deceased  followed  him  in  a  scuttle  over  the  gun  for  fif-
teen  paces,  until  the  prisoner  got  to  the  log;  there  Shelton  struck  him
with  the  stick  and  knocked  him  over  the  log;  at  this  moment  Burwell
Reynolds,  who  was  with  the  slide,  came  up  from  the  rear  and  stabbed
him,  Shelton,  in  the  back  with  alarge  tobacco  Knife.  It was  proven  that
it was  eighty  yards  from  the  slide  to  where  Shelton  was  first  seen  with
his  wagon  by  the  prisoner,  and  that  the  slide  could  have  passed  the
wagon  at  other  and  better  places  in  that  distance,  which  is  aceordingly
certitied.

I,  L.  G.  Racker,  clerk  of  the  circuit  court.  for  said  county  of  Patrick,
in  the  State  of  Virginia,  do  hereby  certify  that  the  foregoing  ts  a  true
transcript  of  the  record  and  proceedings  in  a  certain  prosecurion  pend-
ing in  said  court  against  Burwell  and  Lee  Reynolds,  on  an  indictment  for
murder,  with  all  things  touching  the  same,  as  fully  and  wholly  as  they
now  exist  among  the  records  of  my  oflice.

court  this  ¥Yth  day  of  December,  1578.

On  consideration  of  the  petition  and  motion  filed  in  this  cause  by  Mr.
Jawes  G.  Field,  of  counsel  for  the  petitioner,  praying  tor  a  rule  requir-

Ansicer  of  Judge  Rives.

In  obedience  to  the  foregoing  copy  of  a  citation  of  the  Supreme  Conrt,
I beg  leave  to  vouch  the  record  of  the  cases  as  now  pending  in  the  eir-
cuit  court  of  the  United  States  at  Danville.
It  has  been  hastily  and
unartificially  made  up,  and  includes  applications  for  habeas  corpus,
which  were  never  acted  upon.
It  is exclusively  a case  of removal  under
sect.  641  of  the  Revised  Statutes.

rom  this  record  may  be  gathered  the  following  jurisdictional  facts:
In  April,  1878,  the  petitioners  being  denied,  as  they  allege,  a  legal
and  impartial  jury  tor  their  trial,  at  once  filed  their  petitions  in  the  cir-
cuit  court  of  Patrick  for  removal  to  the  circuit  court  of  the  U.  S.,
next  ensuing,  at  Danville,  which  was  to  occur  on  the  15th  Nov’r,  1878.
These  petitions  were  denied  and  disregarded  by  the  State  court,  and
the  defendants  forced  into  trial.  Thereupon  Burwell  Reynolds  was
found  gnilty  of  murder  in  the  first  degree,  and  a  new  trial  given  him ;
Lee  Reynolds,  of  murder  in  the  second  degree,  and  sentenced  to  15
years  in  the  penitentiary,  &  denied  a  new  trial,  to  which  he  ex-
cepted,  appealed  to  the  supreme  court  of  appeals,  and  was
awarded,  on  30th  July.a  new  trial,on  the  ground  *  that  the  facets
certified,  as  proved  on  the  trial,  did  not  warrant  the  jury  in  finding  the
plaintiff  in  error  guilty.”

23

In  Oct’r,  1878,  these  parties  were  again  arraigned  for  trial  before  the
circuit  court,  of  Patrick,  renewed  their  challenges  to  the  venire,  and
upon  their  being  overruled  a  second  time,  pressed  their  applications  for
removal  to  the  Federal  court,  and  were  a  second  time  refused  and  forced
into  trial.
Lee
feynolds  was  again  convicted  for  the  longer  term  of  18  years,  refused
a  new  trial,  and  sentenced  for  the  same.

In  the  case  of  Burwell  Reynolds  there  was  a  mistrial.

On  the  first  day  of  the  U.S.  circuit  court  at  Danville,  namely,  on  the
15th  Nov’r,  1878,  which  was  the  first  term  after  the  filing  of  their  peti-
tions  in  the  State  court,  the  said  parties,  by their  counsel,  filed  their  peti-
tions  in  this  court,  all  which  resulted  in  the  order  of  15th  Nov’r,  1878.

proval  and  compelled  my  assent  and  action.

28

Its  validity  is ‘derived  from  the  XLV  amendment.

Approved  in  Vanuevar  vs.  Bryant  (2L  W.,  41).
Lil.  Section  641  is  strictly  analogous  to  the acts  thus  pronounced  con-
stitutional.
I  beg
leave  in  this  connection  to  refer  to  the  Slaughter-house  cases  (16  Wall.,
36),  for  the  object  of  this  amendment,  namely,  ‘the  freedom  of  the  slave
race”;  the  security  and  firm  establishment  of  that  freedom  and
the  protection  of  the  newly-made  freeman  and  citizen  from  the
oppressions  of  those  who  had  formerly  exercised  unlimited  domin-
ion  over  him.”
The  court  places  under  the  guarantees  of  the  XIV
amendment  the  rights  accruing  under  its  concluding  clause,  namely,
‘nor  shall  any  State  deny  to  any  person  within  its  jurisdiction  the
equal  protection  of  the  laws” : and  I  humbly  submit,  it  matters  not
whether  it  be  done  by  the  State  by commission  or  omission,  by  law  or
want  of  law,  by  administration  of  its  executive  or  jadicial  departments,
the  mischief  is equal  and  the  remedy  as  much  needed.

29

IV.  That  a  Federal  question  arising  under  this  amendment  was  in-
volved  in,  and  a  Federal  right  invaded  by the  action  of the  circuit  court
of  Patrick,  results  from  the  right  of  the  accused  to  trial  by  an  impar-  |
tial  jury  selected  without  discrimination  of race  or  color.
The  challenge
to  the  array  was  well  taken  and.  should  have  been  allowed;  and  its
denial  deprived  the  prisoners  of  *“*the  equal  protection  of  the  laws,”
and  exposed  them  without  reason  or  excuse  to the  fatal  effects  of
race  and  traditionary  prejudices.
It  cannot  be  that  these  people
shall  be  entitled  to  the  equal  privileges  and  accommodations  of
public  inns,  &e.,  but  may  not  enjoy  the  ordinary  muniments  of personal
security  in trial by juty,  but  shall  lose  the  benefit  of ‘all  the  common-
law  challenges  for  “unindiffereney’ ’——-as  Ld.  Coke  expresses it:  “*So  it is,
if the  sheritt  return  any  one  that  he  be  more  favouralie  to  the  one  than
to  the  other,  all  the  array  should  be  quashed.”

(3 Thomas’  Coke,  457.)
before  adverting  to  the  language  of  the  law  by  means  of ascertaining
how  far  it  is  warranted  by  the  amendment,  I  beg  leave  to  quote  the
jangnage  of  Ch.  J.  Waite  in  delivering  the  opinion  of  the  court  in
U.S.  vs. Reese  et  al.  (92  U.S. Rep’ts,  p.217):
“  Rights  and  immunities
created  by or  dependent  upon  the  Constitution  of  the United  States,  can
be  protected  by Congress.
The  form  and  mauner  of the  protection  may
be  such  as  Congress  in  the  legitimate  exercise  of  its  legislative  discre-
tion  shall  provide.
These  may  be  varied  to  meet  the  necessities  of  the
particular  right  to  be protected.”

By  this  test,  I submit  the  remedy  of removal  is justified  for  **  any  per-
son  who  is  denied  or  cannot  enforce  in  the  judicial  tribunals  of
30,  31  the  State,  or  in  the  part  of the  State  where  such  suit  is prosecuted
any  right  secured  to him  by any  law  providing  for  the  equal  rights
of  all  citizens,  or  of  all  persons  within  the  jurisdiction  of  the  Uuited
States.”

The  record  attests  that  these  parties  were  denied  trial  by  a  jury,
legal  under  the  laws  of  the  State,  and  without  discrimination  of  race;

that  their  challenge  to  the  array  was  improperly  overruled,  &  that  the
results  of  two  trials,  had  in  defiance  of the  act  of Congress,  and  one  end-
ing  in  a  verdict,  against  the  opinion  of  the  supreme  court  of  the  State  on
the  facts,  shewed  that  they  could  expect  no  relief  save  before  a  Federal
court.

With  these  convictions  and  these  reasons  and  these  authorities,  while
holding  the  cireuit  court  at  Danville  in  the  absence  of  the  circuit  judge
and  the  Chief  Justice,  who  is  allotted  to  this  circuit,  | rendered  the
judgment  complained  of.

The  question  whether  a  mandamus  lies  in  such  a  ease,  is  respectfully
submitted  to  the  court  under  its  own  well-known  and  reinterated  decis-
ions.

Respectfully  submitted.

Pleas  before  the  judges  of  the  circuit  court  of  the  United
States  of America  for  the  fourth  circuit  and  the  western  district
of  Virginia,  at  Danville,  on  the  25th  day  of  February,  in  the  year  of
our  Lord  one  thousand  eight  hundred  and  seventy-nine.

Be  it  remembered  that  heretofore,  to  wit,  at  a  circuit  court  of  the
United  States  of America  for  the  fourth  circuit  and  the  western  district
of Virginia,  continued  and  held  at  Danville,  on  the  20th  day of Novem.
ber,  in  the  year  of our  Lord  one  thousand  eight  hundred  and  seventy-

3o

At  a  circuit  court  held  for  the  connty  of  Patrick  at  the  court-house
thereof,  on  the  first  day  of  April,  1878,  Burwell  Reynolds  and  Lee  Rey-
nolds,  late  of  the  county  of  «Patrick,  labourers,  who  stand  jointly  in-
dicted  for  murder,  was  ied  to  the  bar  in.  custody  of  the  jailor  of  this
court ; whereupon  the  accused,  by counsel,  moves  the court  the  venire  in
‘the  cause,  composed  entirely  intirely  of  the  white race,  be  so  modi-
fied  as  to  allow  one-third  of  the  venire  to  be  composed  of  their
race,  they  being  colored,  which  motion  was  overruled,  upon  the
ground  that  the  court  had  no  authority  to  change  the  venire,  it  appear-
ing  that  the  said  venire  had  been  regularly  drawn  from  the  jury-box
according  to  law.
The  accused,  by counsel,  then  appealed  to  the  attor-
ney  for  the  Commonwealth  and  to  the  counsel  assisting  in  the.  prosecu-
tion  that,  as  the  ballots  in  the  jury-box  were  entirely  of  the  white  race,
they,  the  said  attorney  for  the  Commonwealth  and  the  feed  counsel  for
the prosecution,  to  allow  them,  the  accused,  a  venire  to  be  composed  of
one-third  of  their  own  race,  they,  the  accused,  waiving  all  objections  to
the  illegality  of  so  constituting  the  same,  which  the  attorney  for  the
Commonwealth  and  the  feed  counsel  assisting  in  the  prosecution  retused.
Thereupon  the  accused,  by  counsel,  filed  their  petition,  verified  by
their  aflidavits,  praying  that  this  prosecution  may  be  removed  to  the
next  cireuit  court  to  be  held  for  the  United  States  for  the  western  Cistrict
of  Virginia,  in  the  town  of  Danville,  which  prayer  was  denied  by  the
court.

Whereupon  the  accused,  in  the  county  court,  having  elected  to  be
tried  separately,  plead  not  guilty  to  the  indictment,  and  were  remanded
for  triai  before  this  court.
On  their  demand  Burwell  Reynolds  was  put
upon  his  trial.  Thereupon  a  panel  of  sixteen  jurors,  selected  from  the

Oia

24

EX  PARTE  COMMONWEALTH  OF  VIRGINIA.

36

thirty-six  drawn  by the  clerk  and  summoned  by the  sheriff,  were  exam-
ined  by  the  court  and  found  free  from  all  legal  exceptions  and  qualified
to  serve  as  jurors  according  to  law,  and  the  accused  having  erased  from
said  panel  four  of said  jurors,  W.T.  Thomas,  D.  J. Morrison,  C.  J.

Whereupon,  on  motion  of the  said  Burwell  Reynolds,  by  counsel,  and
for  reasons  appearing.  to  the  court,  it  is ordered  that  the  said  verdict  be
set  aside  and  that  a  new  trial  be  had  upon  said  indictment,  and  the
prisoner  is  remanded  to jail.

The  petition  filed  in  this  cause  is  in  the  words  and  figures  following,

to  wit:

To  the judge  of  the  circuit  court  of  Patrick:

:

Your  petitioners,  Lee  Reynolds  and  Burwell  Reynolds,  represent  that
there  is  now  pending  against  them  a  criminal  prosecution  ip  the  circuit
court  of  Patrick,  in  which  they  are  charged  with  the  murder  of  one
Aaron  Shelton.

Your  petitioners  are  negroes,  aged  respectively  17  and  19;  that  the
man  whom  they  are  charged  with  having  murdered  was  a  white  man.
Your  petitioners  allege  that  the  right  secured  to  them  by  the  law  pro-
viding  tor  the  equal  civil  rights  of  citizens  of  the  United  States.
is denied  to  them  in  the  judicial  tribunal  of the county  of Patriek,
of  which  county  they  are  natives  and  citizens.

38

EX  PARTE  COMMONWEALTH  OF  VIRGINIA.

25

They  allege  that  by the  law  of ,Virginia,  that  all  mail  citizens,  twenty-
one  years  of  age,  and  not  over  sixty,  who  are  entitled  to  vote  and  hold
oftice  under  the  constitution  and  laws  of  this  State,  shall  be  liable  to
serye  as  jurors;  this  law  allows  the  right  as  well  as  requires  the  duty of
the  race  to  which  they  belong  to  serve  as  jurors;  yet  the  grand  jury
who  has  made  the  indictment  against  them,  as  well  as  the  veftfe  sum-
moned  to  try  them,  are  exclusively  compose@of  the  white  race.

They  have  applied  through  their  counsel  to  your  honor,  the  judge  of
said  court,  and  also  the  prosecuting  attorney  of  said  county,  as  well  as
the  fee’d  counsel  employed  to  assist  in  the  prosecution,  that  a  portion  of
the  venire  by  which  they  are  to  be  tried  in said  circuit  court  of  Patrick
should  be,  at  least  in  part,  composed  of  competent  jurors  of  their  own
race  and  color;  this  right  has  been  refused  them.

They  allege  that  a  strong  prejudice  exist?  in  the  community  of  said
county  against  them  independant  of  the  merits  of  the  case,  and  based
solely  upon  the  fact  that  your  petitioners,  the  accused,  are  negroes,  and
that  the  man  whom  they  are  charged  with  having  murdered  was  a white
man;  from  this  fact  alone  they  are  satisfied  that  they  cannot  obtain  an
impartial  trial  before  a  jury  composed  of the  white  race.
They  further
State  that  their  race  have  never  been  allowed  the  right  to serve  as jurors,
either  in  civil  or  criminal  cases  in  the  county  of  Patrick,  in  any  case
civil  or  criminal  in  which  their  race  have  ever  been  in  any  way  inter-
ested  up  to  the  present  time.

39

Your  petitioners  therefore  pray  that  said  prosecution  may  be
removed  to  the  next  circuit  court  to  be  held  for  the  United  States
tor  the  western  district  of  Virginia  in  the  town  of  Danville.

On  the  power  andl  right  of  removal  of  crim.  prosecutions  for  State
oTences,  under  sections  641-2  Revw’d  Stat’s,  where  the  prisoners,

their  trial.

,

[I  am  aware  that  this  application  presents  questions  of  novelty,
gravity,  and  delicacy.
The  law  on  which  it  is fouuded is not  familiar  to
Hence,  any
the  bar  generally,  and  far  less  so  to  the  public  at  large.
action  under it is  liable  to  be  misunderstood  and  misrepresented;  and
thus  to  give  rise  to  undue  excitement,  disquiet,  and  popular  disturb- |
anee.
This  particular  enactment  has  not  beea  author’atively  construed,
though  some  light  is  thrown  upon  it  by decisions  of  the  Supreme  Court
upon  kindred  parts  of  the  same  general  legislation  tor  the  enforcement
Amy-
of  civil  rights  under  the  late  amendments  of  the  Constitution.
thing  like  a  conflict  of jurisdiction  between  the  State  and  Federal  courts
ought  to  be  avoided  whenever  it  is  possible;  and  it  is  to  be  presumed
that  each  respective  set  of  tribunals  will  be  animated  by  an  equal  and
common  desire  to  obviate  all  such  interference.
Both  judicatories  are
alike  subjected  by  Article  VI  of  the  Constitution  of  the  United  States
to  that  Constitution  and  laws  of  Congress  made  in  pursuance  thereof,
and  it  is expressly  added  that  ‘the  judges  in  every  State  shall  be  bound
thereby,  anything  in  the  constitution  or  laws  of  the  State  to  the  con-
trary  notwithstanding.”
Kven  where  unavoidable,  such  conflict  is  apt
to  disturb  the  harmony  and  interrupt  the  peacetul  action  of  the  two
governments;  to  shock  the  just  sensibility  and  excite  unduly  the  ap-
prebeusions  of  the  public.

47

In  the  consideration,  therefore  ot  this  case,  I felt  | would  best
consult  my  Own  peace  and  popular  repose  if  I could  find  the
means  thereby  of  reconciling  my  duty  witha  denial  of  this  peti-
tion.
Bat,  of  course,  paramount  to  such  considerations  was  my  wish
and  determination  alike  to  execute  the  laws  of  Congress  in  behalf  even

29

of  the  humblest,  so  as  to  ensure  the  equal  protection  of  all  citizens,  as
guaranteed  by  the  XIV  amendment  of  the  Constitution.

It  is  only  necessary  to  state  such  facts  of  this  application  as  are
necessary  to  the  presentation  and  clear  understanding  of  the  question  I
am  to  decide.
The  immediate  and  last  petition  here  is  for  a  habeas
corpus.

fore  a  trial  of  their  cases  for  a  removal  of  them  to  this  court.

petitioners,  on  the  mere  statement  thereof  it  would  seem  the  period  had
passed  for  removal.

The  law  on  which  it  is fouuded is not  familiar  to
gravity,  and  delicacy.
the  bar  generally,  and  far  less  so  to  the  publie  at  large.
Hence,  any
action  under  it  is liable  to  be  misunderstood  and  misrepresented;  and
thus  to  give  rise  to  undue  excitement,  disquiet,  and  popular  disturb-
anee.
This  particular  enactment  has  not  beea  author’atively  construed,
though  some  light  is  thrown  upon  it  by decisions  of  the  Supreme  Court
upon  kindred  parts  of  the  same  general  legislation  for  the  enforcement
of  civil  rights  under  the  late  amendments  of  the  Constitution.
Any-
thing  like  a  conflict  of jurisdiction  between  the  State  and  Federal  courts
ought  to  be  avoided  whenever  it  is  possible;  and  it  is  to  be  presumed
that  each  respective  set  of  tribunals  will  be  auimated  by  an  equal  and
common  desire  to  obviate  all  such  interference.
Both  judicatories  are
alike  subjected  by Article  VI  of  the  Constitution  of  the  United  States
to  that  Constitution  and  Jaws  of  Congress  made  in  pursuance  thereof,
and  it is expressly  added  that  **the  judges  in  every  State  shall  be  bound
thereby,  anything  in  the  constitution  or  laws  of  the  State  to  the  con-
trary  notwithstanding.”
Even  where  unavoidable,  such  couflict  is  apt
to  disturb  the  harmony  and  interrupt  the  peaceful  action  of  the  two
governments;  to  shock  the  just  sensibility  and  excite  unduly  the  ap-
prebensions  of  the  public.

‘  When  all  the  acts  necessary  for  the  removal

within  the  United  States,  and  subject  to  the  jurisdiction  thereof,  are

It is conceded  that  thisinhibition  applies exclusively  tothe  State
A  State  1s  a  sort  of  trinity  ;  it
But  that  term  presents  a  compiex  idea.
exists,  acts,  and  speaks  in  three  capacities—legislative,  executive,  and

Hence,  it seems
to  me  it  is  in  strict  pursuance  of  this  article  to  base  the  intervention  of
the  Federal  courts  on  the  inability  to  enforce  in  the  judicial  tribunals  of
the  State  or  in  some  parts  thereof  the  equal  civil  rights  secured  by  this
article.  The  mischief is the  same,  whether  the  deprivation  proceeds  from
the  law,  the  courts,  or  the  executive.  It is equally  attributable  to  the
State;  the  laws  of  the  State  may  be  all  conformable  to  the  require-
ments  of  this  article,  but  its  infraction  may  rest  with  the  courts,  ur  ex-
ecutive  authorities  of  the  counties.

cation  of this  law  of  Federal  intervention  under  the  theory  of  this  arti-

forcement  of these  equal  civil  rights.

The  State  law,  as  well  as  this  amendment,  guarantees  to  this  lately  en-
franchised  class  the  same  sort  of juries  for  their  trial  as  should  reasonably
follow  from  the  deliberate  omission  in  the  law  of  any  discrimination  on
account  of color  or  race.
The  demand,  therefore,  to  reconstruct  the  ve-
nire  under  the  direction  of  the  presiding  judge,  so  as  to  allow  some  rep-
resentation  of  the  prisoner’s  race  upon  it,  was,  as  it seems  to  me,  in  con-
formity  with  the  law  of  the  State,  and  the  just  concession  to  the  spirit
of this  amendment.
This  demand  is  to  be  taken  as  a  challenge  to  the
array  on  the  ground  that  whereas  the  State  law  ordains  what  is  called
mixed  juries,  that  is,  of all  persons  qualified  as  voters,  without  distine-
tion  of  race  or  color,  in  this  case  the  selection  has  been  so  made,  whether
by design  or  accident  it  matters  not,  as  to  include  only  whites,  to  the
manifest  hazard  of  the  prisoner.
The  converse  of  the  proposition,  as  I
have  suggested,  namely,  the  rightfulness  of  overruling  the  challenge  of
[am  sure,  be  contended
a  white  person  to  a pauel  of negroes,  would  not,
for  in  any  quarter.

his  race  or  color,  the  privilege  of  being  tried  by such  jurors  as  the  State
in  every  view  I can  take  of  it,  the  denial  of this  challenge

deprived  the  prisoners  of the  equal  protection  of  laws.

ing  for  them  any  new  right,  but  only  the  protection  of  the  State  laws
for  their  trial.

venire  awarded  on  the  terms  of  the  law,  without  distinction  of

contrary,  ample  discretion  to  that  end  is  reposed  with  the  judge  by the

53d

The  question  recurs,  was  not  its  denial  under  the  circumstances  a
denial  to  the  prisoner  of the  equal  protection  of the  laws?  It  is  greaily
to  be  deplored  that  no  uniform  practice  obtains  in  this  regard  through-
out  the  commonwealth,  though  the  law  is  the  same  everywhere.
I am
informed  that  the  judge  of this  county  invariably  gives  the  accused,  when
colored,  the  benetit of a  mixed  jury,  and  that  it  is  believed  that  no  harm
bas  ever  resulted  from  it, or  any  failure  of  punitive  justice.  The  same,  I
um  told,  is  the  practice  in  other  counties  of  the  commonwealth,  and  |
trust,  one  of  the  effects  of  the  agitation  of  this  question  will  be  to  ex-
tend  it.
If this  should  become  general  or  be  required  by  law,  all  pre-
tence  for  Federal  interference  would  be  removed,  and  the  law  and  prac-
tice  would  conform  in  leading  to  the  just  enforcement  of  equal  civil
rights  to  all  colors  and  races.
If  negroes  were  to  be  tried  by  former
slaveholders,  once  allied  to  them  by  interest,  affection,  and  sympathy,
the  danger  might  not  be  great,  but  if  these  people  are  to  depend  for
their  lives,  liberty,  and  property  on  the  white  men  who  never  knew  or
felt  these  ties,  | regret  to  say  they  are  without  the  guar’tees  enjoyed  by
others  from  the  jury  trial  secured  by  Article  VI  of  the  amendments  to

the  Constitution.

UNITED  STATES  MARSHAL’S  OFFICE,

Danville,  Virginia,  February  25th,  1879.

This  writ  was  executed  by Deputy  Marshal  Grief  Lamkin,  on  the  24th
day  of  November,  18373,  by  taking  the  bodies  of  the  within  named  Lee
feynolds  and  Burwell  Reynolds  from  the  jail  of  Patrick  County,  Vir-
ginia,  and  conveying  them  to  the  jail  of  Pittsylvania  County,  Virginia,
where  they  were  confined  in  my  custody  until  the  19th  day  of  December,
1578,  when  they  were  conveyed  by  Deputy  Marshal  D.  S.  Lewis  to  the
jail  of  Rockingham  County,  Virginia,  where  they  were  confined  for  safe
keeping  until  the  23d  of  February,  1879,  when  they  were  conveyed
to  Danville,  Virginia,  and  are  this  day  delivered  before  the  U.S.  cireuit
court,  according  to  the  within  writ.

The  day  and  cause  of  their  detention  in  the  jail  of  Patrick  County,
Virginia,  are  fully  set  forth  in  the  record  exhibited  with  the  petition  in
re  Lee  Reynolds  and  Burweil  Reynolds,  filed  in  the  U.S.  circuit  court  at
Danville,  Virginia.

United  States  Marshal,  Western  District  of  Virginia.

JOHN  F.  LEWIS,

Filed  25th  February,  1879.

exander  Thompson,  A.  J.  Willis,  Claiborne  [Edons,  J.  H.  Cruise,  8S.  J.

true  bill;  which  indictment,  with  the  endorsement  thereon  by  the  fore-
nan,  is  in  the  words  following,  to  wit:

STATE  OF  VIRGINIA.

County  of Patrick,  to  wit:

The  jurors  of  the  commonwealth  of  Virginia,  in  and  for  the  body  of
the  county  of  Patrick,  and  now  attending  the  said  court,  upon  their
oaths  present  that  Burwell  Reynolds,  late  of  the  said  county,  labourer,
and  Lee  Reynolds,  late  of  the  said  county,  labourer,  on  the  29th  day  of
November,  in  the  year  of  our  Lord  1877,  with  force  and  arms,  at
aforesaid,  in  the  county  aforesaid,  in  and  upon  one  Aaron  C.  Shelton,
in  the  peace  of  God,  and  of  their  malice  aforethought,  did  make  an
assault,and  that  they,  the  said  Burwell  Reynolds  and  the  said  Lee  Rey-
nolds,  with  a  certain  knife  of the  value  of  fifty  cents,  in the  hands
of  the  said  Burwell  Reynolds,  then  and  there  being  had  and
held,  the  said  Aaror  C.  Shelton  in  and  upon  the  left  side  of  the
body,  on  the  left  side  of  the  backbone,  and  between  the  ribs  of  him  the
said  Aaron  C. Shelton,  then  and  there  feloniously,  willfully,  and  of their

A  true  bill.

E.  B.  TURNER,  Foreman.

BURWELL  AND  LEE  REYNOLDS.  \

Us,

The  deposition  of Aaron  C.  Shelton,  taken  before  me,  Wm.  W.  Moir,  a
commissioner  in  chancery  of  Patrick  County  court,  on  the  first  day  of
December,  1877,  at  the  residence  of  Joshua  Craddocks,  in  Patrick
County,  Virginia,  upon  what.  his  physician  supposes  to  be  his  death
bed,  to  be  read  as  evidence  in  any  prosecution  of  the  commonwealth
against  the  said  parties.
The  said  Aaron  C.  Shelton,  first  being  duly  sworn,  deposeth  as  fol-

lows:

On  the  29th  November  I was  hauling  stocks  on  the  hill  this  side  of
T.  S.  Camphell’s  mill,  and  Asa  Tuggle  was  cutting ; Lee  Reynolds  came
up,  and  I said  to  him, I  suppose  you  have  threatened  to  take  my  life
With  that  stick  and  gun,  and  he  said  that  he  would  kill  meif  I did  not
run,  and  if I ran,  that  he  would  make  bis  two  dogs,  then  with  him,  catch
me.  I said  to him,  ‘* Look  here,  boy, do you  know  who you  are  talking  to?”
I was  setting  on  the  wagon  at  this  time,  and  then  got  off  the  wagon  to
load  my  log,  and just  as  I got  off the  wagon,  he  cocked  his  gun,  and  I
step’ed  up  to  him  as  quick  as  I could,  and  jerked  the  stick  from
him,  and  knocked  him  over  the  log  that we  were  going  to  load  on
the  wagon,  and  just as he  fell  Burwell  Reynolds  ran  up  and  stab-
The  stick  now  shown  me  is the

bed  me  with  a  large  knife  in  my  back.

me  I fell,  and  they  both  ran  off  and  left  me.

rick,  at  the  court-house  thereof,  on  the  first  day  of  April,  1878,  Burwell

of  the  county  of  Patrick,  labourer,  who  stands  jointly  indicted  for  mur-
Whereupon
der,  was  led  to  the  bar  in  custody  of  the  jailor  of  this  court.
the  accused  by  counsel  moved  the  court  that  the  venire  in  this  ease,
composed  entirely  of  the  white  race,  be  so  modified  as  to  allow  one-third
of  the  venire  to  be  composed  of  their  race,  they  being  colored,  which
motion  was  overruled  upon  the  ground  that  the  court  had  no  authority
to  change  the  venire,  it  appearing  to  the  satisfaction  of  the  court  that
the  said  venire  had  been  reguiarly  drawn  from  the  jury  box  according
to  law.
The  accused  by  counsel  then  appealed  to  the  attorney  tor  the
commonwealth,  and  the  counsel  assisting  in  the  prosecution,  that  as  the
ballots  in  the  jury  box  were  entirely  of  the  white  race,  they,  the
,

69
ing  in  the  prosecution,  allow  them,  the  accused,  a  venire  com-
posed  of  one-third  of  their  own  race,  they,  the  accused,  waiving  all  ob-
jections  to  the  illegality  of so  constructing  the  same,  which  the  attorney
for  the  commonwealth  and  the  feed  counsel  for  the  prosecution  refused.
Thereupon  the  prisoners,  by counsel,  filed  their  petition,  verified  by their
affidavits,  praying  that  this  prosecution  may  be  removed  to  the  next
cirenit  court  to  be  held  for  the  United  States  for  the  western  district  of

court.

'

Which  said  petition  is  in  the  words  and  figures  following,  to  wit:

To  the  judge  of  the  circuit  court  of  Patrick:

Your  petitioners,  Lee  and  Burwell  Reynolds,  represent  that  there  is
now  pending  against  them  a  criminal  prosecution  in  the  eircuit  court  of
Patrick,  in  which  they  are  charged  with  the  murder  of  one  Aaron  ©.
Shelton.
Your  petitioners  are  negroes,  aged  respectively  17  and  19.
That  the  man  whom  they  are  charged  with  having  murdered  was  a white
man.
Your  petitioners  allege  that  the  right  secured  to  them  by the  law,
providing  for  the  equal  civil  rights  of  all  the  citizens  of the  United  States,
is denied  tothem  in the  judicial  tribunals  of  the county  of Patrick, of which
county  they  are  natives  and  citizens.  They allege  that  by the  laws  of Vir-
ginia,  all  male  citizens  twenty-one  years  of age,and  not  over  sixty,
who  are  entitled  to  vote  and  hold  office  under  the constitution  and
laws  of this  State,  shall  be liable  to  serve  asjurors.  This  law  allows
the  right  as  well  as  requires  the  duty  of the race  to  which  they  belong  to
Yet  the  grand  jury  who  has  made  indictment  against
serve  asjurors.
them  as  well  as  the  venire  summoned  to  try  them  are  exclusively  com-
posed  of  the  white  race.  They  have  applied  through  their  counsel  to
your  honor,  the  judge  of  said  court,  and  also  to  the  prosecuting  attor-
ney  of said  county,  as  well  as  to  the  feed  counsel  employed  to  assist  in

70

EX  PARTE  COMMONWEALTH  OF  VIRGINIA.

39

the  prosecution,  that  a  portion  of  the  venire  by  which  they  are  to  be
tried  in  said  circuit  court  of Patrick  should  be  at  least  in  part  composed
of  competent  jurors  of  their  own  race  and  color;  this  right  has  been  re-
fused.  They  allege  that  a  strong  prejudice  exists  in  the  community
against  them  independent  of  the  merits  of  the  case,  and  based  solely
upon  the  fact  that  your  petitioners,  the  accused,  are  negroes,  and  that
the  man  whom  they  are  charged  with  having  murdered  was  a  white
man.  . Irom  this  fact  alone  they  are  satisfied  that  they  cannot  obtain
an  impartial  trial  before  a  jury  composed  entirely  of  the  white  race.
They  allege  that  their  race  have  never  been  allowed  the  right  to  serve
as  jurors  either  in  civil  or  criminal  cases  in  the  county  of  Patrick,  in
any  case  civil  or  criminal  in  which  their  race  have  ever  been  in  any  way
interested  up  to  the  present  time.

Your  petitioners  therefore  pray  that  said  prosecution  may  be  removed
to  the  next  circuit  court  to  be  held  for  the  United  States  for  the
western  district  of  Virginia,  in  the  town  of  Danville.

Aud  at  another  day,  to  wit,  at  a  circuit  court  continued  and  held  for
the  county  of  Patrick,  at  the  court  house  thereof,  on  the  5th  day  of
April,  1878,  Lee  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  labourer,  who
stands  jointly  indicted  with  Burwell  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of
Patrick,  labourer,  of  murder,  was  again  led  to  the  bar,  in custody  of  the
jailor  of  this  court,  and  the  jury  sworn  on  yesterday  the  truth  of  and
upon  the  premises  to  speak  was  brought  into  court  in  charge  of  R.  M.
McArther  and  A.  W.  Honuchins,  deputy  sheriffs,  pursuant  to  their  ad-
journment,  and  were  sent  to  their  room  to  consult,  and  after  some  time
were  brought  into  court  in  charge  of  said  deputy  sheriffs,  having  found
the  following  verdict,  to  wit:  We,  the  jury,  find  the  prisoner,  Lee  Rey-
nolds,  guilty  of  murder  in  the  second  degree,  in  manner  and  form  as
charged  in  the  indictment,  and  ascertain  the  term  of  his  confinement  in
the  penitentiary  to  be  the  period  of  fifteen  years.
Thereupon  the  pris-
oner,  Lee  Reynélds,  by  counsel  moved  the  court  to  set  aside  the  verdict
and  grant  bim  a  new  trial,  and  the  court  takestime.
And  the  prisoner,
Lee  Reynolds,  is  remanded  to  jail.

i

And  at  another  day,  to wit,  at  acireuit  court  continued  and  held
for  the  county  of  Patrick,  at  the  court  house  thereof,  on  the  5th
day  of  April,  1878,  Lee  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  labourer,
who  stands  jointly  indicted  with  Burwell  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of
Patrick,  labourer,  for  murder,  was  again  led  to  the  bar,  in  custody  of
the  jailor  of  this  court,  and  the  court  being  advised  of its judgment,  the
prisoner’s  motion  for  a  new  trial,  overrules  the  said  motion.

|

Thereupon  it  was  demanded  of  the  prisoner  if  anything  for  himself
he  had  or  kuew  to  say  why  the  court  should  not  now  proceed  to  pro-
bounce  judgment  against  him  according to law, and  nothing  being  offered
in  delay  of  judgment,  it  is  considered  by  the  court  that  the  said  Lee
ceynolds  be  imprisoned  in  the  penitentiary  of  this  commonwealth  for
the  term  of  fifteen  years,  to  be  treated  in  the  manner  prescribed  by law,
and  the  sheriff  of  this  county  is ordered  as  soon  as  possible  after  the
adjournment  of  this  court  to  remove  and  convey  the  said  prisoner,  Lee
teynolds,  from  the  jail  of  this  county  to  the  penitentiary  of  the  com-
monwealth,  therein  to  be  kept  imprisoned  and  treated  in  the  manner

directed  by law  for  the  term  aforesaid.

SO

On  the  trial  of  this  prosecution  the  prisoner,  by  counsel,  ex-
cepted  to  the  opinion  of  the  court  overruling  bis  motion  for  a
new  trial  and  tendered  his  bill  of  exceptions,  which  was  received,  signed,
and  ordered  to  be  made  a  part  of  the  record.
And  the  prisoner  is  re-
manded  to  jail.

In  the  clerk’s  office  of  the  circuit  court  for  the  county  of  Patrick,  at
the  court-hbouse  of  said  county,  in  the  vacation  of  said  court,  on  the  17th
day  of  October,  1875,  the  following  mandate  was  received  aud  entered,
in  the  words  aud  figures  following,  to  wit:

VIRGINIA:

Ata  supreme  court  of  appeals,  held  at  the  court-house  of  Wythe

County,  in  the  town  of  Wytheville,  on  the  50th  day  of  July,  1573.

LEE  REYNOLDS,  PLAINTIFF  IN  ERROR,
against

)

THE  COMMONWEALTH,  DEFENDEN'  IN  ERROR.  9

Upon  a  writ  of  error  to  a  judgment  of  the  circuit  court  of  Patrick

EX  PARTE  COMMONWEALTH  OF  VIRGINIA.

43

County,  render’d  on  the  3d  day of  April,  1878,  on  an  indictment  of  the
said  plaintiff  in  error  for  murder.

81

This  day  came  again  as  well  the  said  plaintiff in  error,  by his  counsel,
as  the  Commonwealth,  by the  attorney-general,  and  the  court,  having
maturely  considered  the  transcript  of  the  record  of  the judg-
ment  aforesaid  and  the  argument  of  counsel,  is of  opinion,  for
reasons  stated  in  writing  and  filed  with  the  record,  that  the  facts
certified  as  proved  on  the  trial  did  not  warrant  the  jury  in  finding  the
plaintiff  in  error,  Lee  Reynolds,  guilty  of the  homicide  charged  in  the
indictment,  and  therefore  the  court  erred  in  overruling  the  motion  of
the  plaintiff  in  error  to  sit  aside  the  verdict  and  grant  him  a new  trial,
because  the  verdict  was  against  the  evidence.

Therefore  it is considered,  ordered,  and  adjudged  that  the  said  judg-
ment  of  the  said  circuit  court  be reversed  and  annulled,  the  said  verdict
set  aside,  and  the  cause  be  remanded  to  the  said  circuit  court  for  a  new
trial  to  be  had  therein  of the  said  plaintiff  in  error,  Lee  Reynolds,  upon
the  said  indictment.
Which  is  ordered  to be certified  to the  said  circuit
court  of  the  county  of  Patrick.

And  at  another  day,  to  wit,  at  a  special  term  of  the  circuit  court  for
the  county  of  Patrick,  held  at  the  court-house  thereof  on  the  28th  day
of  October,  1878,  Burwell  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,
labourer,  and  Lee  Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  labourer,
who  stands  indicted  of  murder,  were  led  to  the  bar  in  custody  of
the  jailor  of  this  court;  and  the  said  Burwell  Reynolds  and  the
said  Lee  Reynolds,  before  the  empaneling  of  the  jury  for  their
trial  at  this  term,  charged  with  the  murder  of Aaron  C. Shelton,  moved
the  court  to remov e the ‘said prosecution  to the  next  term  of of the  United

set  forth  in  their  petition,  presented  and  filed  at  the  April  term,  1878,
of  this  court,  which  motion  was  again  overruled  and  the  prayer  of  the
petitioners  refused.  The  prisoners  having  elected  to  be tried  separately,
Lee  Keynolds  was  remanded  to  jail.

And  at  another  day,  to  wit,  at  a  special  term  of  the  circuit  court  of

examined  by  the  court,  and  fuund  free  from  all  legal  exceptions  and
qualitied  to  serve  as  jurors  according  to  law,  and  the  prisoner  having

the  county  of  Patrick,  held  at  the  court-house  thereof  on  the  31st  day

and  upon  the  premises  to  speak,  and  adjourned  over  until  9 o’clock  this

“We,  the  jury,  find  the  prisoner,  Lee  Reynolds,  guilty  of  murder  in  the
second  degree,  and  ascertain  the  time  of  his  confinement  in  the  peni-

84

nolds  be  imprisoned  in  the  penitentiary  of  this  commonwealth  for  the
term  of eighteen  years,  and  the  sheriff  of this  county  is ordered,  as  soon

manner  directed  by  law  for  the  term  aforesaid.

show  no

said  western  district  of  Virginia,  to  the  jailor  of  Patrick  County,  in  said

Reynolds,  to  be  dealt  with  according  to  the  laws  of  the  said  Common-
wealth.

orable  court  to  issue  its  writ  of  mandamus  to  the  said  Alexander  Rives,
district  judge  as  aforesaid,  commanding  to  do  so.

Burwell
Reynolds,  late  of  the  county  of  Patrick,  was  jointly  indicted  for  murder,

administered  the  usual  oath,  came  into  court  pursu: unt to  adjourn-
ment,  and  having  fully  beard  the  evidence  and  arguments  of
counsel  were  sent  to  their  room  to  consult  of  a  verdict,  and  after  some

wit:

89

“We,  the  jary,  find  the  prisoner  Lee  Reynolds  guilty  of  murder  in  the
second  degree,  and  ascertain  the  term  of  his  confinement  in  the  peni-
tentiary  to  be  eighteen  years.”

Whereupon  the  prisoner  moved  the  court  to  set  aside  the  verdict  and
grant  him  a  new  trial,  which  motion  being  heard  the  same  for  reasous
appearing  to  the  court  is  overruled.

And  it  being  asked  of  the  prisoner  if  anything  for  hisnaelt  he  had  or

ment  against  him  according  to  law,  and  nothing  being  offered  or  alleged
in  delay  of  judgment,  it  is  considered  by  the  court  that  the  said  Lee
teynolds  be  imprisoned  in  the  penitentiary  of  this  Commonwealth for
the  term  of  eighteen  years.

And  the  sheriff of this  county  is  ordered  as  soon  as  possible  after  the
adjournment  of  this  court,  to  remove  and  convey  the  said  Lee  Reynolds
from  the  jail  of  this  court  to  the  penitentiary  of  this  Common-
wealth,  therein  to  be  kept  imprisoned  and  treated  in  the  manner
directed  by  law  for  the  term  aforesaid.

90)

The  prisoner  stating  that  he  desired  to  apply  to  the  supreme
court  of  appeals  for  a  writ  of error  to  the  said  judgment,  the  court  sus-
pends  the  said  judgment  for  sixty  days  from  this  time,  to  enable  him
to  apply  for  such  writ.

On  the  trial  of  this  cause  the  prisoner  excepted  to  the  opinion  of  the
court,  overruling  his  motion  for  a  vew  trial,  and  tendered  his  bill  of
exceptions,  which  was  signed  by  the  court  and  ordered  to  be  made  a
part  of  the  record.

And  the  prisoner  was  remanded  to jail.

STATEMENT  OF  THE  CASE.

Burwell  Revnolds  and  Lee  Reynolds  were  jointly  indicted  for  mur-
der  in  the  county  court  for  the  county  of  Patrick,  in  the  State  of  Vir-
ginia,  at  its  January  term,  1878.

On  being  arraigned  they  pleaded  not  guilty,  and  demanding  to  be
tried  in  the  cireuit  court  of  said  county,  were  remanded  fcr  trial  in

a

that  court.

\

‘

When  brought  up  for  trial  at  the  April  term,  1878,  of  said  circuit
court,  they  made  the  motion  and  application  for  a  jury  composed  in
part  of  their  own  race  (they  being  colored  persons),  which  will  .be

found  on  pages  6 and  7 of  the  record.

Said  motion  and  application  being  overruled  and  refused,  they  then
filed  the  petition,  which  will  be  found  on  pages  7 and  8  of the  record,
praying  for  the  removal  of  the  prosecution  against  them  to  the  next
cireuit  court  to  be  held  for  the  United  States  tor  the  western  district
The  prayer  of said  petition  was
of  Virginia,  in  the  town  of  Danville.

denied  by the  court.

2

As  explanatory  of  the  reason  given  by the  court  for overruling  said
motion,  it is proper  to  state  that  the law  under  which  the  venire  had  been
summoned  was  the  act  approved  on  the  27th  March,  1876,  a  copy  of
which,  as  well  as  of the  statutes  now  in  force  in  reference  to  the  quali-
fications  and  mode  of  summoning  juries  in  Virginia,  is filed  with  this
brief,

The  prisoners  having  elected  to  be  tried  separately,  Burwell  Rey-
nolds  was  then  put  upon  his  trial,  and  being  found  guilty  of  murder
in  the  first  degree,  on  his  motion  the  verdict  was  set  aside  by the
eourt  and  a  new  trial  awarded  him.

Lee  Reynolds  having,  at  the  same  term,  been  put  upon  hns  trial,
was  found  guilty  of  murder  in  the  second  degree,  and  the  period  of
fifteen  years  was  fixed  by the  jury  as  the  teri  of  his  confinement  in
the  penitentiary;  whereupon  he  moved  the  court  to  set  aside  the  ver-
dict  and  grant  him  a  new  trial,  on  the  ground  that  the  verdict  was
against  the  law  and  the  evidence;  which  motion  being  overruled  by
the  court,  he  excepted  to  the  opinion  of  the  court  overruling  the  same,
and  the  facts  proved  on  the  trial  having  been  set  forth  in  the  bill  of
exceptions,  a  writ  of  error  was  sued  out  by him  from  the  supreme
court  of  appeals  of  Virginia,  on  the  hearing  of which  on  the  30th  day
of  July,  1878,  the  judgment  of  the  circuit  court  was  reversed,  the
verdict  of  the  jury  set  aside,  and  the  cause  remanded  to  the  circuit
court  for  a  new  trial  to  be  had  therein.

At  ihe  October  term,  1878,  of  said  circuit  court  Burwell  Reynolds
and  Lee  Reynolds  were  again  brought  up  for  trial,  and  before  the
empanelling  of  the  jury  for  their  trial,  they  moved  the  court
to
remove  the  prosecution  against  them  to  the  next  term  of  the  United
States  circuit  court  to  be  held  in  Danville,  Virginia,  upon  the  ground
set  forth  in  their  petition  presented  and  filed  at  the  April  term,  1878;
which  motion  was  again  overruled  and  the  prayer  of  the  petitioners
refused,  as  will  appear  on  page  15  of  the  record.
The  prisoners  were  then  again  tried  separately.
In  the  case  of  Burwell  Reynolds,  the  jury  being  unable  to  agree
upon  a  verdict,  a  Juror  was  withdrawn,  by  consent,  and  he  was  re-

manded  to  yal  to  be  thereafter  tried.

Lee  Reynolds  was  again  convicted  of  murder  in  the  second  degree,
and  eighteen  years  fixed  by the  jury  as  the  term  of  his  confinement

in  the  penitentiary.

etree

Hle  moved  for  a  new  trial,  but  his  motion  was  overruled  by the
court  and  sentence  pronounced  in  accordance  with  the  verdict,  he
being  remanded  to  jail  until  he  should  be  removed  ‘to  the  peniten-
tiary.
Ile  excepted  to  the  opinion  of  the  court  overruling  his  motion
for  a  new  trial,  and  his  bill  of  exceptions  was  duly  signed  and  sealed
and  made  part  of  the  record  of  the  case.

While  the  said  Burwell  and  Lee  Reynolds  were  thus  imprisoned  in
the  jail  of  Patrick  county,  the  former  until  he  should  be  again  tried
for  the  offence  with  which  he  was  charged,  the  latter  until  he  should
he  removed  to  the  penitentiary  under  the  sentence  aforesaid,  they  pro-
cured  from  the  clerk  of  the  circuit  court  of  Patrick  county  a  copy  of
the  record  of  ‘said  proceedings  against  them,  which  they  offered  to  file
in  the  cireuit  court  of  the  United  States  for  the  western  district  of

them  presented  as  aforesaid  to  the  circuit  court  for  the  county  of  Pat-
rick  for  the  removal  of the  prosecutions  against  them  into  said  United
States  circuit  court,  and  prayed  of  said  last  mentioned  court  that  said
prosecutions  shonld  be  there  docketed  and  proceeded  with;  which
prayer  was  granted  by the  Hon.  Alexander  Rives,  who,  as  district  judge
of the  United  States  for  the  western  district  of  Virginia,  was  then
holding  said  circuit  court,  and  an  order  was  entered  by said  court,  on
the  15th  day  of  November,  1878,  directing  said  causes  to  be  docketed

in  said  court  for  trial,  and  authorizing  the  clerk  to  issue  forthwith  a

tody  to  be  dealt  with  according  to  law,  and  the  orders  of  said  court,

The  petition  in  this  case  has  been  filed  in conformity  with  joint  reso-
lutions  of  the  general  assembly  of  Virginia,  adopted  on  the  sth  day
of  February,  1879  (a copy whereof  is  filed  with  this  brief),  in  order

The  Hfon.  Alexander  Rives,  judge  as  aforesaid,  in  his  answer  to  the
rule  awarded  against  him  to  show  cause  why  a  mandamus  should  not  be
issued  requiring  him  to  cause  the  bodies  of  the  said  parties  to  be  re-
delivered  by the  marshal  of  his  district  to  the  jailor  of  Patrick  county,
admits  the  facts  as  hereinbefore  stated,  and  justifies  his  action  upon
the  ground  that  the  refusal of the  circuit  court  of  the  State  of  Virgina,
to  set  aside  the  venire  which  had  been  summoned  for  their  tral,  and
give  them  a  jury  composed  in  part  of  their  own  race  and  color,  was  a
denial  to  them  of  “the  equal  protection  of  the  laws,”  and  rendered  it
proper  that  their  cases  should  be  removed,  under  sections  641  and  642

of  the  Revised  Statutes.  into  his  court  for  trial.

PRIEF  OF  ARGUMENT.

The  petitioning  Commonwealth  insists  that  the  action  of  Judge
Rives  is wholly  without  authority,  and  that  his  court  has  not,  and  can-
not  have  under  the  Constitution  of  the  United  States,  any  jurisdiction
whatever  of  the  cases  over  which  he  has  assumed  jurisdiction.  - The
prosecutions  against  Lee  and  Burwell  Reynolds  are  in  the  name  and
on  behalf of the  Commonwealth  of  Virginia,  one  of  the  States  of  the
Federal  Union;  for  the  crime  cf  murder  committed  within  the  terri-
torial  limits  of  said  State.

sy the  Constitution  of  the  United  States  it  is  expressly  provided  in
paragraph  2,  article  3,  that  “in  all  cases  affecting  ambassadors,  other
public  ministers  and  consuls,  and  those  in which  a  Stale  shall  be  a  party,
the  supreme  court  shall  have  original  jurisdiction.”
The  reference
here  to  the  State  relates  to  her  in  her  sovereignty,  as  one  of  the

original  sovereignties  which  called  into  existence  the  United  States
under  a  written  constitution,  in  which  certain  powers  and  parts  of

her  sovereignty  were  delegated,  and  all  others  reserved.

In  the  constitution  conferring  the  delegated  powers,  a  judiciary  sys-

a)

tem  was  provided  ror, to  consist  of one  supreme  court  and  such  inferior
courts  as  Congress  may  from  time  to  time  ordain  and  establish.
(See
section  1, article  3 of  United  States  Constitution.)
Ilaving  provided
for  the  supreme  court  eo  nomine,  the  second  paragraph  of  section  2
of  the  same  article,  provided  that  in  al/  cases  in  which  a  State  (one
of  the constituent  sovereignties)  should  be  a  party,  the  supreme  court
shall  have  original  jurisdiction.

We  insist  that  this  means  an  erelusive  original  jurisdiction  ; and  under
an  inferior  court,  called  into  existence  by
no  circumstances  can
act  of  Congress,  ever  be  clothed  with  the  power  to  deal  with  a  State
or  to  decide  any  question  of controversy  with  her  respecting  or  arising
out  of her  sovereignty.
If Congress  has  ever  attempted  to  clothe  any
such  inferior  tribunal  with  such  a  power  or  jurisdiction,  then  such
attempt  is nugatory,  as  being  wltra  vires.
But  in  the  further  progress
of  this  argument  we  propose  to  try  to  show  that  Congress  has  never

niade  any  such  attempt.

Not  only,  therefore,  can  there  be  no  loss  of  separate  and  independent
