care  of  the  constitution

In  delivering  the  opinion  of  this  court  in  the  ‘ease  of  The  Collector
vs.  Day,  Mr.  Justice  Nelson  said:  “The  general  government  and  the
States,  although  both  exist  within  the  same  territorial  limits,  are  sepa-
rate  and  distinct  sovereignties,  acting  separately  and  independently  of
‘The  former  in  its  appro-
each  other  within  their  respective  spheres.
priate  sphere  is  supreme,  but  the  States  within  the  limits  of  their
powers  not  granted,  or  in  the  language  of  the  tenth  amendment,  ‘re-
served,’  are  as  independent  of  the  general  government  as  that  govern-
ment  within  its  sphere  is  independent  of  the  States.’—11  Wallace,
124.
Mr.  Justice  Bradley,  in  dissenting  from  the  opinion  of  Mr.  Jus-
tice  Nelson  in the  above  case,  said  :  “  I am  as  much  opposed  as  any  one
ean-be  to  any  interference  by  the  general  government  with  the Just
powers  of  the  State  governments.”  —Jbid,  129.

We  submit,  if  there  are  any  “just  powers”. of the  State  with  which

the  general  goverment  cannot  interfere,  the  power  to  try  and  pun-
ish  the  violations  of  her  criminal  laws  occurring  within  her  terri-
Here,  as  Mr.  Justice  Nel-
torial  limits  and  jurisdiction  is one  of them.

son  says,  the  State  is  a  “  sepurate  and  distinet  sovereignty.”

The  State  acting  here  as  a  “separate  and  distinct  sovereignty,”  it
is  not  within  the  power  of  Congress,  by any  law  which  it  may  enact,
to  subject  her  to  the  jurisdiction  of  any  tribunal  ordained  by  it.
If
amenable  to  any  judicial  tribunal  under  the  constitution  of the  Umited
States,  that  tribunal  is  the  supreme  court.

The  next  proposition  we  submit  is,  that  the  Constitution  of  the
United  States  does  not  invest  the  supreme  court  with  any:  jurisdiction

or  control  over  the  State  or  its  courts  in  the  administration  of  its
criminal  laws,  except  so  far  as  by  writ  of  error,  when  a  federal  ques-
tion  arises,  the  accused  is  entitled  to  take  the  ease  from  the  court  of
last  resort  in  the  state  to  the  supreme  court  of  the  United  States.

Murder  is  a  common-law  offence.

The  United  States  have  no

8

common-law  jurisdiction  in  criminal  eases;  therefore  the  trial  for
murder  must  be  in  the  courts  of  the  State  within  the  territorial  juris-
diction  of  which  the  crime  was  committed.
In  the  first  proposition
we  tried  to  maintain  that  as  the  State  in  the  prosecution  of  the  indict-
ment  against  Reynolds  was  acting  in  her  character  of  sovereign,  that
no  United  States  court,  under  any  circumstances,  could  take  Jurisdic-
tion  of  the  case  but  the  supreme  court.

The  State  courts  must  then  remain  the  sole  and  exclusive  tribunals
of  trial,  subject  under  the  limitation  above  mentioned,  to  the  right  of
the  accused  to  a  writ  of  error  to  the  supreme  court.

That  the  State  courts,  and  not  the  United  States  courts,  are  the
exclusive  tribunals  for  the  trial  of  crime  committed  within  the  State
and  against  the  laws  of the  State,  we  refer  to  the  following  cases:  Cor-
field  vs.  Coryell,  4 Washington’s  Circuit  Court  Reports,  371;  Ward
vs.  State  of  Maryland,  12  Wall.  430;  The  Slaughter-house  Cases,  16
Wall.  36,  180;  United  States  rs.  Cruikshank,  2 Otto,  542.

From  what  has  preceded,  we  claim,  with  submission,  that  Congress
has  not  conferred  upon  the  United  States  court  for  the  western  district
of  Virginia,  held  by Judge  Rives,  the  jurisdiction  he  has  assumed  and.
is  now  exercising.
Nor  do  we  think  it has  attempted  to  confer  the  ju-
risdiction.
Judge  Rives  claims  to  act  by virtue  of  section  641  of  the
Revised  Statutes  of  the  United  States,  edition  of  1878,  page  110.
Does  this statute  confer  the  jurisdiction?  We  claim  that  it does  not,  nor
was  it intended  to  do  so.
So  much  of said  section  as  is deemed  neces-
sary  to  the  proper  understanding  of  the  question  now  being  consid-
ered  is as  follows:  “*  When  any  civil  suit  or  criminal  prosecution  is
commenced  in  any  State  court,  for  any  cause  whatsoever,  against  any
person  who  is  denied  or  cannot  enforce  in  the  judicial  tribunals  of the
State,  or  in  the  part  of  the  State  where  such  suit  or  prosecution  is
pending,  any  right  secured  to  him  by any  law  providing  for  the  equal
eivil  rights  of  citizens  of  the  United  States,  or  of  all  persons  within
the  jurisdiction  of  the  United  States,  or  against  any  officer,  civil  or
military,  or  other  person,  for  any  arrest,  imprisonment  or  other  tres-
pass  or  wrongs,  made  or  committed  by virtue  of  or  under  color  of au-
thority  derived  from  any  law  providing  for  equal  rights  as  aforesaid,
or  for  refusing  to  do  any  act  on  the  ground  that  it would  be.  incon-
sistent  with  such  law,  such  suit  or  prosecution  may,  upon  the  petition
of  such  defendant,  filed  in  said  State  court  at  any  time  before  the

9

trial  or  final  hearing  of  the  cause,  stating  the  facts  and  verified  by
oath,  be  removed  for  trial  into  the  next  circuit  court  to  be  held  in  the
district  where  it is pending.”
This  section  relates  to  the  removal  of
causes  having  their  origin  in  the  denial  of  some  civil  right,  and  is  so
Betore  a  cause  can  be
declared  to  be  on  the  margin  of  the  statute.

Ist.  ‘The  denial  of  rights  secured  by the  first  section  of  the  act.
2d.  Inability  to  enforce  in  the  court  any  of  said  rights.
The  rights  here  referred  to  are  the  rights  secured  by sections  1977
The  sections  are  as  follows:
«  All  persons  within  the  jurisdiction  of the  United  States  shall  have
the  same  right  in  every  State  and  territory  to  make  and  enforce  con-
tracts,  to  sue,  be  parties,  give  evidence,  and  to  the  full  and  equal  bene-
fit  of  all  laws  and  proceedings  for  the  security  of  persons  and  pro-
pesty,  as  is  enjoyed  by white  citizens,  and  shall  be  subject  to  like  pun-
ishment,  pains,  penalties,  taxes,  licenses,  and  exactions  of  every  kind,

and  1978  of  Revised  Statutes,  page  347.

and  to  no  other.”

|

‘«‘  All  citizens  of the  United  States  shall  have  the  same  right in  every
State  and  territory,  as  is  enjoyed  by white  citizens  thereof,  to  inherit,
purchase,  lease,  sell,  hold  and  convey  real  and  personal  property.”

These  two  sections  confer  the  civil  rights  referred  to.
The  civil  rights  conferred  upon  the  colored  race  are  the  rights  en-
joyed  by white  citizens  in  the  States  and  Territories  at  the  time  the
law  was  enacted.
It  merely
extended  to  the  colored  race  rights  existing  and  enjoyed  by  white
citizens,  and  provided  that  these  rights  should  not  be  denied  to  the
colored  citizens.

Congress  did  not  create  any  new  rights.

|

All  that  the  colored  citizen  can  claim  is that  his  rights  shall  be  the
same  as  that  of  the  white  citizen,  and  that  he  shall  be  permitted  to
enforee  these  rights  in  the  courts.

The  right  to  have  a  jury  of  a  particular  race  or  color,  in  whole  or
in  part,  to  try  rights  of  property  or  rights  of  person,  is  not  one  of

«Under  the  American  system  it is still,  however,  regarded  as  an  ex-

forms  of  remedy  are  powerless  to  afford  redress  to the party  aggrieved,

Now  this  want  of  jurisdiction  of  the  inferior  court  in
a  summary  proceeding  to  remove  an  officer  of  the  court,  or  disbar  an
attorney  or  counsellor,  is one  of  the  specitie  eases  in  which  this  writ  is
the  appropriate  remedy.  We  have  already  seen  from  the  definition
and  office  of  it,  that  it  is  issued  to  the  inferior  courts  *‘  to  enforce  the
due  exercise  of  those  jadicial  or  ministerial  powers  with  which  the
crown  or  legislature
have  invested  them,  and  this,  not  only  by  restrain-
ing  their  excesses,  but  also  by  quickening  their  neghgence  and  ob-
viating  their  denial  of  justice.’”

Again,  at  page  579,  the  learned  justice  further  remarks:  “  But  the
proceeding  (that  1s  by mandamus)-is  admitted  to  be  the  recognized
remedy  when  the  case  is  outside  of  the  exercise  of  this  diseretion,  and
is  one  of  irregularity,  or  against  law,  or  of flagrant  injustice,  or  without

Mr.  Justice  Miller  in  delivering  a  dissenting  opinion  in  the  above
case,  at  page  380-1,  seemis  to  base  his  dissent  upon  the  ground  that  the
court  below  had  jurisdiction  of  the  person  of  Mr.  Bradley,  and  of
the  offence  charged,  and  that  the  action  of  the  court  was  but  the
exercise  of  a  discretion  clearly  within  the  jurisdiction  of  the  court.

In  the  ease  now  at  bar,  the  cirenit  court  of  the  United  States  for  the
western  district  of  Virginia  had  no  jurisdiction  of  the  State  of  Vir-
ginia,  and  under  the  United  States  constitution  coul.l  have  none,  and

it  had  no  jurisdiction  of  the  offence  charged,  to-wit:  the  murder  of
Shelton  by  Reynolds,  an  offence  against  the  State  law.

Dudley  Field  remarked  in  the  very  able  argument  he  made  in  Crunik-

It  the  United  States  courts  can  intervene  and  take  from  the  State
courts  their  criminal  jurisdiction,  would  it  not  be  well  for  congress
to  provide  their  jails,  their  penitentiaries  and  their  gallows  in  every

State

It  is  worthy  of  observation  that  section  641  gives  to  any  person

whatsoever  who  will  allege  on  oath  that  he  is denied  or  cannot  enforce
in  the  judicial  tribunals  of  a  State  any  right  seeured  to  him  by any
law  providing  for  the  equal  civil  rights  of  citizens  of  the  United
States,  the  absolute  right  to  remove  his  case  into  a  cireuit  court  of  the  -
United  States,  section  642  making  it  the  duty  of  the  clerk  of  the  cir-
cuit  court  to  issue  the  writ  of  habeas  corpus  eum  causa,  without  the
order  of  the  Judge.

As  was  said  by  Mr.  Justice  Bradley, in Texas  vs.  Gaines,  2 Woods,
344:  «Tf  every  citizen  who  is  prosceuted  in  a  State  court  ean,  on  his
own  allegation,  remove  his  case  to  the  United  States  courts,  it  will
present  a  powerful  temptation  to  litigants,  especially  of  the  criminal
class,  and  the  United  States  courts  will  be  flooded  with  eases  in  which

one  of  the  parties  lMagines,  Or  Says,  that  he  cannot  have  a  fair  trial
in  the  State  courts.  We  cannot  think  that  this  is  the-true  construc-

tion  of  the  statute.”

,

It,  however,  it  shall  be  held  that  it  must  be  so  construed,  then  we
of
ot

is  unconstitutional  and  void.

it
10  is  unecoNnstliuUaonal

It  deprives

the  States
states

insist  that

aeprives  the

and

4

their  right  to  try  and  punish,  in  their  own  courts,  offenders  against
their  laws;  and  coniers  upon  the  Federal  courts  power  to  try  and

16

punish  offenders  for  crimes  which  are  not,  and,  under  our  system  of
government,  cannot  be  within  their  jurisdiction.

__

The  judicial  power  of  the  United  States  is defined  and  limited  by
the  second  section  of  Article  III  of  the  constitution.
By no  latitude
of  construction  can  the  power  thereby  conferred  be  extended  to  the
punishment  of  offences  against  the  States,  and  not  against  the  United

States.

|

The  inhibitions  upon  the  power  of  the  States  contained  in  the  14th
amendment,  do  not  any  more  than  those  of the  10th  section  of  Article
I of  the  constitution,  confer  upon  congress  the  right  to  take  into  their
hands  the  administration  of  the  civil  or  criminal  laws  of  the  States  as
a  means  of  preventing  them  from  violating  those  inhibitions.

The  only  way  in  which  the  constitutional  restraints  upon  the  power
of  the  states  can  be  made  effectual  by the  action  of  congress,  is by
providing  that  acts  done  in  violation  of  them  shall  be  treated  as  null,
and  providing  the  means  of  having  them  so  declared,  and  their  opera-

tion  staved  by  the  federal  judiciary.

This  has  been  effectually  provided  for  by the  law  granting  writs  of
error  from  the  supreme  court  of  the  United  States  to  the  State  court
In  cases  in  which  federal  questions  and  rights  are  involved.

In  the  cases  now  under  consideration,  if  the  accused  were  entitled
to  require  that  the  juries  by  which  they  were  to  be  tried  should  be
composed  in  whole  or  in  part  of  persons  of  their  own  race  and  color,

nothing  was  easier  than  for  them  to  take  their  cases  to  the  supreme
court  of  appeals  of  Virginia  on  that  point,  and  upon  an  adverse  deci-

sion  by that  tribunal,  to  take  them  to  the  supreme  court  of  the  United

States  and  have  the  question  there  decided,

In  this  way  all  the  rights  of  the  accused  would  have  been  eompletely
secured,  and  the  State  and  her  officers  held  to  a  due  observance  of  the

eonstitution  of  the  United  States  and  laws  made  pursuant  thereto,
without  impairing  her  sovereign  rights  by depriving  her  courts  of  juris-
diction  ever  offences  against  her  laws.
The  State  and  Federal  courts
would  thus  have  moved  cireumspectly  in  their  appropriate  orbits,  and

the  State  and  Union  saved  the  excitement  incident  ‘to  the  conitlict  be-
tween  State  and  Federal  judicial  authorities.

It  is  not  pretended  that  the  State of Virginia  has  made  or  attempted
to  enforce  any  Jaw  by wiich  the  privileges  and  immunities  of  citizens

of  the  United  States  are  abridged;  or  that  there  is  any  law  of  the

State  pursuant  to  which  any.  person  is  denied  “the  eqnal  protection  of
the  laws.”

On  the  contrary,  Judge  Nives,  in  the  opinion  delivered  by him  when
he  directed  the  removal  of  the  causes  (tiled  with  his  answer  to  the  rnle),
says:

“The  State  law  is  not  in  fault  here.

Under  it  all  voters  are  compe-
tent  jurors,  the  selection  devolving  on  the  county  Judge, so that  no  dis-
crimination  Is  made  by it  on  aeeount  of  color  or  race.
I  lave  en-
deavored  to  follow,  as  closely  as  practicable,  this  State  law  in the  selee-
tion  of  my  juries  at  this  bar.”

[f  any  provision  of  the  fourteenth  amendment  has  been  vielated  in

tiis  Case,  the  State  did  not  authorize  it.
If the  act  complaine  lL of  was
in  conflict  with  any  provision  of  that  amendment,  it  was  also  an  in-

fraction  of  the  State  law,  and  it  is  not  to  be  presumed  that  the  error
would  not  be  corrected  by  her  own  tribunals.

It  was  therefore  manifestly  premature  to  Invoke  the  intervention  of
the  Federal  courts  until  the  party  supposing  limself  to  be  aggrieved
had  attempted  and  failed  to  have  the  alleged  error  corrected  by  the
State  courts  of  last  resort,  when,  as  lhereinbefore  indicated,  a  writ  of
error  might  be  sued  out  from  the  supreme  court  of  the  United  States.
The  fourteenth  anendment  applies  to  the  States  in  their  corporate
capacity;  and  it  is  difficult  to  understan:|  how  the  act  of  a  judge  of
an  inferior  State  court,  in  contravention  of  the  State  law,  which  con-
forms  to  that  amendment,  can  be  held  to  be  the  act  of  the  State.
But  conceding  for  arguinent  suke  that  Judge  Rives  is  right  in  holding
that  the  act  of  such  judge  must  be  taken  to  be  the  act  of  the  State,
how  does  it appear  that  *  the  equal  protection  of  the  laws”  has  in this
cause  been  denied  to  the  acensea  7

All  they  could  ask  was  to  be  put  on  the  same  footing  with  white
persons—that  the  jury  to  try  them  should  be  constituted  in  the  same
This  they  had  a  right  to
manner  that  it  would  be  for  white  persons.
demand—no  more,  and  no  less.
There  is  no  law,  State  or  Federal,
which  gives  to  persons  of  either  race  or  color,  the  right  to  insist  that
the  juries  by which  they  are  to  be  tried  shall  be  e»myosel,  either  in

whole  or  in  part,  ot  their  Owl  Face  or  colo’.  A  haw  SO  providing

would  be  contrary  to  the  whole  spint  of  the  const.tutional  amend-
ments,  which  were  designed  to  put  both  races  on  a  footing  of  entire
equality  unde  the  law.  Jndge  Rives  in  requiring  that  persons  of color

The  subject
matter  of  litigation  in  these  suits  had  no  relation  to  the  14th  amend-
ment  or  the  civil  rights  bill.
We  therefore  insist  that  these  cases  are
not  authorities  sustaining  the  action  of  Judge  Hives.

The  third  proposition  of  Judge  Rives,  at  page  21  of  printed  record,
is  that  section  641  is  strictly  analogous  to  the  acts  thus  pronounced
constitutional,  and  that  the  14th  amendment  gives  it  validity.
Upon
this  proposition  depends  the  question  at  issue.  We  deny  that  the  14th
amendment  confers  the  mght  to  a  jury  of  a  particular  race  or  color,
and  the  amendment  not  conferring  such  a  rigit,  section  641  does  not
authorize  a  removal  merely  becanse  such  right  is  claimed  and  denied.

RESOLULIONS

GENERAL  ASSEMBLY

ADOPTED  BY  THE

IN  REGARD  TO  THE

USURPATION  OF  POWER  BY  THE  FEDERAL  JUDICIARY

IN  VIRGINIA.

Your  committee,  therefore,  report  the  following  resolutions,  an‘|  re-

commend  their  passage:

RESOLUTIONS.

The  general  assembly  of  Virginia,  relying  with  a  firm  trust  on  the
virtue,  intelligence  and  patriotism  of the  people  as  the  only  sure  foun-
dation  of republican  government,  and  on  which  the  ultimate  guarantee
of  liberty  must  always  rest,  invoke  the  calm  and  serious  consideration
of all  the  States  and  ali  the  people  thereof,  to  the  statements  and  declar-
ations  hereinafter  set  forth  ;  and  do  appeal  to  them  for  their  co-opera-
tion  and  assistance  in  maintaining  the  principles  of  tree  government,
and  in  enforcing  remedies  under  the  constitution  for  the  wrongs  and:
injuries  herein  shown  to  have  been  done  to  the  rights  of  the  people,
and  in  preventing  a  repitition  of the  same.

They  do  declare  and  affirm:
1.  That  the  constitution  of  ‘the  United  States  is in  as  full  force  and

vigor  as  when  adopted  and  ratified.

2.  That  Virginia  in  re-entering  the  Union  after  the  civil  war,  re-
sumed  all  her  relations  to  the
the  United  States  on  per-
fect  equality  with  every  other  State;  and  is entitled  to the  same  rights
and  powers  and  bound  by  the  same  duties  and  obligations  as  each
other  state;  and  that  her  citizens  are  equals  in  every  respect  under  the
constitution  with  all  other  citizens  of the  United  States.

government  —

o.  That  the  United  States  ean  only  exercise  the  powers.  delegated

2

to  them  in  the  constitution.
Within  the  scope  of  these  powers  their
All  powers
government  issupreme;  beyond  them  it bas  no  existence.
not  delegated  to  them  are  reserved  to  the  States  or  to  the  people,  and

in  the  exercise  of  them  the  States  are  sovereign,  the  two  governments
together  forming  one  complete  whole,  and  neither  can  or.  ought  to  in-
terfere  with  the  rights  or  duties  of  the  other.

4.  That  the  preservation  of the  States  and  the  maintenance  of  their
governments  are  as  much  within  the  design  and  care  of  the  constitu-
tion  as  the  preservation  of  the  Umon  and  the  maintenance  of  the
Federal  government;  and  the  separate  and  independent  autonomy  of
the  State  is necessary  to  the  Union  under  the  constitution.

®.  That  the  essential  and  fundamental  right  reserved  by the  people
of  the  States  is  to  organize  each
a  separate:  and  dis-
tinct  political  society,  to  constitute  such  government  therein  -as_  best
suits  ther  will  and  pleasure,  and  to  change  the  same  whenever  their
convenience  requires  it, subject  only  to  the  constitution  of  the  United
States,  and  the  laws  and  treaties  made  in  pursuance  thereof.

State  as

6.  That  the  necessary  and  inherent  condition  of  this  reserved  right
of  local  self-government  is the  right  and  duty  of  the  State  to  protect
life,  liberty  and  property  within  her  jurisdiction,  by laws  of  her  own
ereation  and  by officers  of  her  own  selection.

7.  That  the  constitution  of  the  United  States  grants  certain  powers
and  imposes  consequent  duties  upon  the  United  States  which  they  are
bound  to  execute  and  perform;  and  that  the  rights  reserved  to  the
States  impose  on  them  certain  other  functions  and  duties  which  they
are  equally  bound  to  execute  and  perform;  and  the  United  States  can
no  more  rightfully  interfere  with  and  attempt  to  execute  the  powers
and  perform  the  functions  of  the  State,  than  the  States  can  those  of
the  United  States;  and  any  attempt  by either  to  interfere  with  the
rights  of  the  other  will  be  usurpation,  unconstitutional  and  void.

8.  That  the  United  States  have  only  the  right  to  prevent  the  exer-
«ise  by the  States  of powers  prohibited  to  them  by the  constitution,  by
pronouncing  such  forbidden  action  void,  through  the  judicial  power,
but  in  no  case  can  the  United  States  undertake,  in  any  manner,  or
for  any  reason  whatsoever,  to  perform  any  duty  which  it is incumbent
on  a  State  to  perform.

9.  That,  therefore,  all  acts  of  the  congress,  and  particularly  those
known  as  the  civil  rights  bill,  and  the  enforcement  act, which  attempt,

2°

i EBA tiers:

or  profess  to  attempt,  to  provide  for  the  performance  by the  United
States  of  duties  and  obligations  belonging  to  States,  are  uneonstitu-
tional  and  void.

10.  That,  therefore,  the  action  of  the  judge  of  the  district  court  of
tie  United  States  for  the  western  district  of  Virginia,  in  assuming  ju-
risdiction  of  the  cases  cf  Lee  Reynolds  and  Burwell  Reynolds,  parties
charged  with  crime  against  the  laws  of  this  commonwealth,  and  held
to  answer  therefor  by the  cireuit  court  of  Patrick  county,  one  of  the
courts  of  this  commonwealth  of  competent  jurisdiction,  and  his  re-
moval  of  those  parties  from  the  custody  of  the  sheriff  of  Patrick
county,  who  is the  legal  custodian  of  the  same,  to  that  of  the  marshal
of  the  United  States,  and  his  proceeding  to  try  the  same  in  the  cireuit
court  of  the  United  States  for  said  western  district,  is  an  attempt  on
his  part  to  execute  the  laws  of  this  commonwealth,  to  regulate  her  in-
ternal  police,  in  the  courts  and  by the  officers  of  the  United  States,  is
unwarranted  by  the  constitution,  is  destructive  of  the  mght  of  the
people  of  each  State  to  protect  life,  liberty  and  property  in  their  own
way,  by their  own  courts  and  officers, and  ought  at once  to be remedied
by proper  judicial  action,  and  any  recurrence  of  the  same  prevented
by appropriate  legislation.

11.  That  proper  provision  by law  ought  to  be  oinite for  the  prompt
an  effective  supervision  by the  supreme  court.of  the  United  States  of
all  the  proceedings  of  the  judges  of  the  inferior  courts  of  the  same,
when  they  deny  any  right  claimed,  in  any  matter  before  them,  under
the  rights  reserved  to  the  States  or  the  people,  or  the  rights  guaranteed
by the  constitution  of  the  United  States;  and  this  supervision  ought  to
be  exercised  by interference  before  a  wrong  is  done,  as  well  as  by ap-
peal  after  one  has  been  committed.

12.  That  the  Governor  is  hereby  requested  to  direct  the  Attorney-
General  to  institute  proceedings  in  the  name  ot  this  Commonwealth
before  the  supreme  court  of  the  United  States,  and  to  demand  in  her
name  such  protection  and  redress  as  that  tribunal  can  afford  in  the
premises,  to  the  end:  that  the  parties  now  wrongfully  held  under  the
mandate  of  the  judge  of  the  district  court  of the  United  States  for  the
western  district  of  Virginia,  may  be  returned  to  the  custody  of  the
officers  of  this  Commonwealth,  to  be  dealt  with  as  her  laws  direct.

13.  That  the  Governor is  hereby  requested  to transmit  copies  of these

and  he may  discharge  any panel  whenever, in  his  opinion, |  thev  have
If  a  person  summoned  under  a  writ  for
served  a  reasonable  time.

and  representatives  in  congress,  in  order  that  they  may  be  laid  before
their  respective  houses;  and  also  to  the  Governor  of  each  State  in
this  Union,  with  the  respectful  request  that  he  bring  the  same  to  the
attention  of  the  general  assembly  of  his  State,  and  the  earnest  hope
that  all  the  States  will  co-operate  and  assist  us  in  maintaining  our  com-
mon  rights  and  protecting  our  common  interests.

All  of  which  is  respectfully  submitted,

a ‘HAP.  167.—AN  ACT  to  amend  and  re-enact  sections  four,  five  and  nine  of  chap-
ter  two  hundred  and  two  of  the  Code  of  Virginia,  edition  of  eighteen  hundred

and  seventy-three,  as  amended  by  an  act  approved  January  fifteenth,  eighteen

hundred  and  seventy-five.  in  regard  to  juries  in  criminal  cases.

Approved  March  27,  1876.

1.  Be  it  enacted  by  the  general  assembly  of Virginia,  That  sections
four  and  nine  of  chapter  two  hundred  and  two  of  the  Code  of  Vir-
ginia,  and  section  five  of  chapter  two  hundred  and  two  of  the  Code  of
of  Virginia,  as  amended  by  an  act  approved  January  the  fifteenth,
eighteen  hundred  and  seventy-five,  be  amended  and  re-enacted  so  as
to  read  as  follows:

§4.  The  clerk  of  the  court  wherein  the  trial  of  persons  accused  of
felony  is  to  be  had,  shall,  as  soon  as  may  be,  issue  a  venire  facias,  di-
rected  to  the  proper  officer  of  the  court,  requiriug  him  to  summon
jurors  therefor.

§5.  The  writ  shall  command  the  officer  charged  with  its  execution
to  summon  thirty-six  persons of his  county  or  corporation,  to  be drawn
from  the  lists  provided  for  in  section  three  of  chapter  one  hundred
and  fitty-eight  of  the  Code  of  Virginia  (edition  of  eighteen  hundred
and  seventy-three),  to  attend  the  court  wherein  any  such  trial  is to  be
had,  on  the  first  day  of  the  next  term  of  said  court,  or  at  such  other
time  as  the  court  may  direct;  and  from  such  list  of  jurors  so  sum-
moned  and  in  attendance,  a  jury  shall  be  empanelled  for  the  trial  of

any  and  every  case  ready  for  trial  at  that  term  of  the  court:  provided,
‘that  if  there  be  only  one  felony  case  on  the  docket  at  the  time  of
issuing  the  writ,  the  writ  shall  command  the  officer  to  summon  only
twenty-four  persons:  and  provided  further,  that  the  judge  of any  court
may,  if  he  deem  it expedient,  direct  the  clerk  of  his  court  to  issue  a
separate  venire  facias  for  jurors  as  hereinbefore  provided  for,  each

week  during  which  the  term  of  his  court  will,  in  his opinion,  continue ;
and  he  may  discharge  any  panel  whenever,  in  his  opinion,  they  have
If  a  person  summoned  under  a  writ  for
served  a  reasonable  time.

ae  ae

RP  IPs  %

4

3h
"ee

the  trial  of  any  criminal  case,  fail  to  attend  as  required,  without  -suf-
ficient  cause,  ke  shall  be  fined  by the  court  not  less  than  five  dollars
nor  more  than  twenty  dollars.

§9.  When  any  jurors  summoned  under  a  writ  of  venire  facias  fail
to  attend,  or  are  challenged  for  cause,  the  court  shall  cause  other jurors
ot  like  qualifications  to  be  sammoned  in  the  county  or  corporation,
from  a  list  to  be  furnished  by the  judge  presiding  at  the  time;  but  if
the  whole  array  be  challenged,  the  court  shall  cause  to  be  summoned
thirty-six  other  jurors  of  like  qualifications,  to  be  drawn  from  the  list
mentioned  in  the  fifth  section  of this  chapter,  until  a  panel  of sixteen
jurors,  free  from  exception,  be  completed.
The  accused  shall  have  a
peremptory  challenge  as
to  four  of  the  panel,  and  the  remaining
twelve  shall  constitute  the  jury  for  the  trial  of  the  case;  but  should

the  accused  fail  to  strike  from  the  panel  any,  or  less  than  four  of  the
names  thereon,  then  the  jury  for  the  trial  shall  be composed  of twelve
of  those  not  so  stricken  off,  to  be  selected  by  lot.
2.  This  act  shall  be  in  force  from  its  passage.

This  case  shows  the  following  facts:

Upon  being  arraigned
for  murder  in  the  county  of  Patrick,  Virginia.

county,  they  asked
that  they  be  allowed a trial  by  a  jury  constituted
In  part  of  their  own  race  and  color;  the  jury  then  summoned  for  their
trial  being  composed  exclusively  of  white  men.

them,  whereupon  they  filed  in  said  court  defore  their  trial  a  petition

But,  if  these  objections  are  not  considered  valid,  let  us  pass  to  the
consideration  of  the  merits  of  the  case.
And  first,  I again  recall  the
attention  of  the  court  to  the  fact  that  no  motion  was  made  in  the  Court
below  to  remand  the  cause,  as  is provided  in  the  act  of  March  3d,
1875,  section  5,  18  U.S.  Statutes-at-Large,  which  provides  for  such
motion  in.all  cases  of  removal  from  State  to  Federal  courts,  and  no
attempt  was  made  there  to  controvert  the  facts.
So  that  the  matters
set  forth  in  the  petition  for  removal,  and  the  accompanying  record  are
here  to  be  taken
They  could  not  be  contradicted  here,  es-
pecially  as  the  parties  whose  direét  interests  are  involved,  are  not  par-
ties  to  this  proceeding,  and  have  had  no  opportunity  to  meet  any  such
contradiétion  either  there  or  here.
If  the  jurisdiction  of  the  Circuit
Court  depended  upon  any  question  of  faét,  surely  a mandamus  would
not  lie  to  require  any  particular  decision  upon  such  question.

as  true.

The  precise  point  of  complaint,  as  shown  by  the  petition  and  the
accompanying  record,  is  that  the  petitioners  were  denied  a  jury  consti-
tuted  without  discrimination  against  the  race  and  color  to  which  they

belonged.

;

The  petition  is  drawn,  it  will  be  observed,  in  as  nearly  as  could  be,
the  literal  language  of  the  statute,  section  641  Revised  Statutes  of
the  United  States.
It  sets  forth  the  grounds  of  the  required  action
in  the  substantial  language  of  the  statute  which  provides:

‘When  any  civil  suit  or  erzminal  prosecution  is  commenced  in  any
State  court  for  any  cause  whatsoever  against  any  person  who  is denied,
‘or  cannot  enforce,  in  the  judicial  tribunals  of  the  State,  or  in  the  part
of  the  State  where  such  suit  or  prosecution  is  pending,  any  right  se-

cured  to  him  by any  law  providing  for  the  equal  civil  rights  of citizens

Sa RN a Py ser

*

of  the  United  States  or  of  all  persons  within  the  jurisdiction  of  the
United  States
such  suit  or  prosecution  may,  upon  the  peti-
tion  of  such  defendant,  filed  in  said  State  court  at  any  time  before  the
trial  or  final  hearing  of  the  cause,  stating  the  facts,  aud  verified  by
oath,  be  removed  for  trial  into  the  next  Circuit  Court  to  be  held  in

*

*

10

the  district  where  it  is  pending.
Upon  the  filing  of  snch  petition,  all
further  proceedings  in  the  State  courts  shall  cease,  and  shall  not  be
resumed  except  as  hereinafter  provided.’’

Section  642  provides  for  the  issuing  of  a  writ  of habeas  corpus  cuit
causa,  to  be  issued  by  the  Circuit  Court  upon  the  above  state  of  facts,
where  the  defendant  isin  the  custody  of  the  State  court  under  its

COS Oe Eero ee

process.

< --  R

Articles  four  and  five  of  the  fourteenth  amendment  to  the  Constitu-

tion  provide  :—

‘¢Nor  shall  any  State  deny  to  any  person  within  its  jurisdiction  the

equal  protection  of  the  laws.’’

‘‘’The  Congress  shall  have  power  to  enforce  by appropriate  legisla-

tion  the provisions  of  this  article.’’

An  act  of  Congress  of  March  3,  1875,  18  Statutes-at-Large,  p.  335,

provides  :—

‘*That  no  citizen  possessing  all  uther  qualifications  which  are  or
may  be  prescribed  by  law  shall  be  disqualified  for  service  as  grand  or
petit  juror  in  any  court  of  the  United  States  or  of any  State  on  account
of  race,  color,  or  previous  condition  of  servitude;  and  any  officer  or
other  person  charged  with  any  duty  in  the  selection  or  summoning  of
jurors  who  shall  exclude  or  fail  to  summon  any  citizen  for  the  cause
aforesaid,  shall,  on  conviction  thereof,  be  deemed  guilty  of  a  misde-

meanor,  and  be  fined  not  more  than  five  thousand  dollars.’”’

Section  722  of  the  Revised  Statutes  of  the  United  States  provides  :—

‘The  jurisdiction  in  civil  and  criminal  matters  conferred  on  the
District  and  Circuit  Courts  by  the  provisions  of  this  title,  and  of  title
‘Civil  Rights,’’  and  of  title  ‘‘Crimes,’’  for  the  protection  of  all  per-
sons  in  their  civil  rights,  ana  for  their  vindication,  shall  be  exercised
and  enforced  in  conformity  with  the  laws  of  the  United  States,  so  far
as  such  laws
are  suitable  to  carry  the  same  into  effect;  but  in  all  cases
where  they  are  not  adapted  to  the  object,  or  are  deficient  in  the  pro-
Visions  necessary  to  furnish
suitable  remedies  and  punish  offenses
against  law,  the  common  law,  as  modified  and  changed  by  the  consti-
tution  and  statutes  of  the  State  wherein  the  court  having  jurisdiction
of  such  civil  or  criminal  cause  is  held,  so  far  as  the  same  is  not  incon-

sistent  with  the  Constitution  and  laws  of  the  United  States,  shall  be
extended  to  and  govern  the  said  conrts  in  the  trial  and  disposition  of
the  said  cause,  and,  if it  is  of  a  criminal  nature,  in  the  infliction  of
punishment  on  the  party  found  guilty.

,?

‘

It  would  seem,  therefore,  from  the  petition  and  the  accompanying

.*

record,  and  these  acts  of  Congress,  that  przma  facie,  at  least,  the  Cir-

cuit  Court  of  the  United  States  had  jurisdiction  of  these  cases,  and

that  the  course  and  manner  of  their  trial  is  prescribed.

11

This  prima  facie  case  is,  however,  sought  to  be  overthrown  by the
following  objections  which  it  is  proper  for  us  to  consider.
I  do  not
know  what  will  be  relied  upon  here,  as  I have  not  in  the  preparation  of
this  brief,  seen  any  briefs«f  counsel  for  the  petitioner,  but  I have  seen
the  objections  made  by  the  report  of  the  Attorney  General  of  Virginia
to  the  Governor,  and  by  the  special  committee  of  the  Senate  and  the
Committee  on  Federal  Relations  of  the House  of  Delegates  of  Vir-
ginia.

It  is  said  that  a  negro  jury,  either  in  whole  or  in  part,  is  not  one  of
those  rights  enumerated  in  the  civil  rights  bill,  as  found  in  seétion
1977  Of  Revised  Statutes.
‘True,  but  we  are  not  confined  to  that.  We
rely  upon  the  Constitution,  the  highest  ‘‘law’’  and  the  aéts  I have
quoted.

;

Certainly  the  act  of  March  2,  1875,  above  quoted,  requires  that  there
5
3
for  service  as  a  juror,  on  the  ground  of
shall  be  no  disqualification
color  or  race,
Every  person  has  a  right  to a trial  by  @ jury  constetuted
wtthout  discrimination  against  his  own  race  or  color  under  this  amend-
It  is  not  for  a  negro  jury  that  we  are  con-
ment,  and  under  this  act.
tending,  nor  is  our  specific  claim  that  negroes  shall  constitute  a  part
of  the  jury.
It  is  simply  that  the  jurv  should  be  empannelled  without
discrimination  against  the  race  of  the  accused.

a ene ame

Again,  the  objection  is  made  that  negroes  have  the  same  juries  as
white  men,  and  that  they  therefore  have  equal  civil  rights  with  them.

ted  by  the  State,  such  a  thing  could  not  occur  in  a  case  of a white  man

to  be  reversed,  that  a  white  man  is charged  with

white  race.
an  illustration  show  that  a  discrimination  in  the  selection  of  juries  on
account  of  race  or  color,  is  a  denial  of  the  ‘‘  equal  protection  of  the

Would  this  be  considered  equal  and  fair?

laws?’’
One  has  a  jury  of  his  own  race;  the  other  does  not.

By  this  system,  white  men  and  negroes  are  not  equally  treated.
One  cannot  be

12

subject  to  the  prejudice  against  him  on  account  of  his  race  or  color ;
the  other  may  be.
amendment  was  designed  to  prevent.

It  was  just  this  discrimination  which  the  fourtecnth

But  it  is  argued  that  a  State  may  prescribe  the  qualifications  of  its
own  jurors,  as  for  example,  that  only  men  or  adults  may  be  allowed  upon
ajury,  or  only  freeholders,  &c.,  and  that  in  such  cases,  a woman  might
as  well  complain  that  a  discrimination  is  made  against  her  sex,  or  an
infant,  that  a  discrimination  is  made  against  infants,  or  one  without
property,  that  none  of  his  class  could  sit  upon  the  jury  for  the  trial  of
his  cause.

But  this  argument  ignores  the  principles  upon  which  the  four-
teenth  amendment  was  based.
‘That  was  directed  to  discriminations
on  account  of  race  and  color,  and  not  to  discriminations  based  upon
such  distinétions  as  had  no  reference  to  race  or  color,  or  perhaps  to
citizens  of  the  United  States  not  citizens  of  the  State.
This  ts
clearly  shown  in  the  decision  of  the  court  in  the  Slaughter  House
Cases.

‘The  court  say:

16  Wall.

‘¢  An  examination  of the  history  of  the  causes  which  led  tothe  adop-
tion  of  the  13th,  and  14th,  amendments,  and  of  the  amendments  them-
selves,  demonstrates  that  the  main  purpose  of  all  the  three  last  amend-
ments,  was  the  freedom  of  the  African  race,  the  security  and  perpetu-
ation  of  that  freedom,  and  their  protection  from  the  oppressions of the
white  men  who  had  formerly  held  them  in  slavery.
In  giving  con-
struction  to  any  of  these  articles,  it  is  necessary  to  keep  this  main  pur-
pose  steadily  in  view.”’

At  page  81  the  Court  say:  ‘‘  Norshall  any  State  deny  to  any  person

within  its  jurisdiction  the  equal  protection  of  the  laws.

‘‘In  the  light  of  the  history  of  these  amendments  and  the  pervading
purpose  of  them,  which  we  have  already  discussed,  it  is  not  difficult
to  give  a  meaning  to  this  clause.

‘The  existence  of  laws  in  the  States  where  the  newly  emancipated
negroes  resided  which  discriminated  with  gross  tnjustice  and  hardship

against  them  as  a  class  was  the  evil  to  be  remedied  by  this  clause,  and
by  it  such  laws  are  forbidden.

‘““If,  however,  the  States  did  not  conformtheir  lawsto  its  require-
ments,  then,  by the  fifth  section  of  the  article  of amendment,  Congress
was  authorized  to  enforce  it  by  suitable  legislation.
We  doubt  very
much  whether  any  action  of  a  State  not  directed  by  way  of  discrimi-
nation  against  the  negroes  as  a  class,  or  on  account  of  their  race,  will
ever  be  held  to  come  within  the  purview  of  this  provision.
It  is  so
clearly  a  provision  for  that  race  and  that  emergency  that  a  strong  case

13

would  be  necessary  for  its  application  to  any  other.
But  as  it  is  a
State  that  is  to  be  dealt  with,  and  not  alone  the  validity  of  its  laws,  we
may  safely  leave  that  matter  until  Congress  shall  have  exercised  its
power,  or  some  «ease  of State  oppression,  dy dental  of equal  justice  in  its
courts,  shall  have  claimed  a  decision  at  our  hands.’’

In  the  Slaughter  House  cases  it  was  attempted  to  extend  the  protec-
tion  of  the  fourteenth  amendment  to  discriminations  made,  or  charged
to  have  been  made,  between  the  citizens  of  the  State,  as  such,  upon
other  grounds  than  those  of  race,  color,  or  previous  condition  of servi-
tude.  But  the  Court  say  that  the  ordinary  fundamental  rights  of citizens,
as  citizens  of  the  State,  are  alone  under  the  protection  of  the  State.
Sovereignty,  for  this  purpose,  rests  alone  with  the  States.
But  where
some  right  pertains  to  a  person  as  a  citizen  of  the  United  States—
making  a  distinction  between  a  citizen  of  the  State  and  a  citizen  of
the  United  States—or  where  the  rights  of any  Jerson  depends  upon  the
Constitution  and  upon  this  amendment,  then  that  right  can  be  en-
forced  and  protected  by appropriate  legislation.

Justice  Field,  in  his  dissenting  opinion,  and  as  restated  by  him  in
Bartemeyer  vs.  lowa,  (18  Wall.,  129,)  thought  that  the  protection  of
the  fourteenth  amendment  extended  much  farther,  and  was  designed
to  protect  from  partial  discrimination  in  regard  to,  and  from  the
abridgment  of  those  fundamental  rights  which  belong  to  the  citizens
of all  free  governments,  growing  out  of  not  only  a  jealousy  and  dislike
towards  the  colored  race,  but  towards  citizens  of  the  North  settling  in
the  Southern  States,  and  towards  those  who  had.been  loyal  to  the
Government  during  the  late  rebellion.

jut  this  view  was  not  adopted  by  the  Court,  and  the  distinction
was  taken  upon  which  we  rely,  and  which  it  seems  to  me _ pre-
serves
to  the  full  extent  the  sovereignty  of  the  States,  except  in
those  cases  in  which  they  refuse  to  comply  with  the  requirements  of
the  Constitution  and  this  amendment,  or  permit  a  refusal  to  be  made
by  persons  charged  with  the  execution  of  its  laws,  and  it  is  in  just  such
a  case,  and  only  such,  that  the  power  of  the  United  States  interposes
and  supplies  the  remedy  for  the  failure  of  the  citizen  to  enjoy  the  full
protection  of  the  right  conferred  ‘or  which.  is  dependent  upon  the
Constitution.

|

This  doctrine  is clearly  stated  in  the  case  of  United  States  ws.  Reese,

92  U.S.

At  page  217  the  Chief  Justice  says:

‘Rights  and  immunities  created  by,  or  dependent  upon  the  Con-
stitution  of  the  United  States  can  be  protected  by  Congress.
The
form  and  the  manner  of  the  protection  may  be  such  as  Congress,  in
‘These
the  legitimate  exercise  of  its  legislative  discretion,  may  provide.
may  be  varied  to  meet  the  necessities  of the  particular  right  to  be  pro-
tected.’’

.

14

The  coystitutional  right  conferred  by  the  fifteenth  amendment  is,
‘exemption  from  discrimination  in  the  exercise  of  the  elective  fran-
chise  on  account  of  race,  color, or previous  condition  of  servitude.
This,  under  the  express  provisions  of  the  second  section  of  the  amend-
ment,  may  be  enforced  by appropriate  legislation.”’

In  this  case  the  Court  say  that  it  would  have  been  within  the  consti-
tutional  power  of Congress  to  legislate  for  the  protection  of  that  right
even  at  State  elections,  but  the  acts  not  being  confined  to  such  unlaw-
ful  discriminations,  though,  perhaps,  its  general  terms  were  broad
enough  to  cover  such  a  case,  and  being  a  penal  act,  it  was  held  that
Congress  had  not  provided  the  appropriate  legislation  for  such  a  case.
The  Court  say,  at  page  218:

‘*Tt  is  only  when  the  wrongful  refusal  at  the  State  election  1s  decause
of race,  color,  or  previous  condition  of  servitude,  that  Congress  can
interpose  and  provide  for  its  punishment.’°

Fa TA!

Again,  inthe  United  States  vs.  Cruikshank,  92  United  States,  at  page

NN AER SO

554,  the  court  say:

‘¢The  fourteenth  amendment  prohibits  a  State  from  denying  to  any
person  within  its  jurisdiction,  the  equal  protection  of  its  laws,  but  this
provision  does  not  add  anything  to  the  rights  which  one  citizen  has
under  the  constitution,  against  another.

The  equality  of the  rights  of citizens  is a  principle  of  republicanism.
Every  republican  government  is  in  duty  bound  to  protect  all  its  citi-
zens.  in  the  enjoyment  of this  principle,  if within  its  power.  That  duty
‘The
was  originally  assumed  by  the  States,  and  it  still  remainsthere.
only  obligation  resting  upon  the  United  States  is  to  see  that  the  States
do  not  deny  this  right.
This  the  amendment  guarantees,  but  no  more.
The  power  of the  government  is  limited  to  the  enforcement  of this  guar-
anty.’’

While,  therefore,  the  duty  of  extending  to  all  persons  within  the
State,  the  egva/  protection  of  its  laws  was  originally  assumed  by  the
States  and  still  remains  there,  yet,  if  it does  deny  or  permit  a  denial
of  this  r7ght  of equality,  if such  tneguality  ts  based  upon  the  ground  of
color  or  race,  then  the  power  of  the  United  States  may  enforce  this
guaranty  of equality  to  the  the  extent  that  it  is  denied.

Surely  the  doctrine  we  contend  for  with  the  limitations  put  upon  it
by  the  majority  of  the  court,  in  the  Slaughter  House  cases,  is  not  so
very  dangerous  or  alarming  as  has  been  urged.
‘There  is  nothing  in
this  toso  greatly  excite  the  executive  and  legislative  authorities  of  the
State.
This  does  not  threaten  to  bring  about  the  universal  destruction  ot
the  sovereignty  of  the  States,  to  establish  the  despotism  of  a  central-
ized  power  upon  their  ruins,  to  result  in  the  existence  of  the  States  at
the  mere  will  and  pleasure  of  the  United  States,  arid  to  demolish  and

15

annihilate  the  independent  autonomy  of the  States.
This  is not  a  sur-
render,  of  the  whole  police  power  of the  State  to  the  ‘‘arbitrary  power
and  fanatical  or  partizan  views  of a  federal  judge.’’  No  such  fearful  con-
sequences  need  be  apprehended.
So  far  as  our  claim  under  this  amend-
ment  is  concerned,  sovereignty  for  all  purposes,  except  in  regard  to
the  egual  protection  of  the  laws,  which  the  court  says  1s a  principle  of
every  republican  government,  is  fully  preserved  to  the  States.
Let  the
State  protect  this  right  as
all  admit  it  is  in  duty  bound  todo,  and
there
can  be  no  interference  whatever ; if it  does  not,  then  the  inter-
ference  can  be  only  so  far  as  to  protect  this  right  by appropriate  legis-
The  sovereignty  of  the  State  for  this  purpose,  and  for  this
lation.
alone,  was  under  this  clause  surrendered  to  the  General  Government
and  however  distasteful  it  may  be,  it  is an  obligation  of  the  State  as-
sumed  by  it,  and  should  be  fulfilled  with  the  most  scrupulous  regard
to  the  dictates  of  honor  and  good  faith,  and  all  will  be  well.

The  objection  is  made  that  if  there  was  any  discrimination  on  the
ground  of  race  or  color  it  was  not  the  act  of  the  State;  it  was  not  a
denial  of  equality  of  rights  by any  law  of  the  State,  and  that  the  pro-
hibition  extends  only  to  the  State.

But  as  this  case  shows,  the  discrimination  was  made  by an  officer
charged  with  the  execution  of  a  law  of  the  State.
The  State  court
decided  that  this  action  of  the  officer  was  authorized  by the  law  of  the
State,  and  therefore  that  such  action  could  not  invalidate  the  zvevzzre.
In  other  words,  the  judiciary  of  the  State  decided  that  this  discrimina-
tion  could  be  made,  and  that  it  could  not  under  the  law  furnish  any
remedy,  and  ¢herefore  denied  the  request  for  a  jury  made  up  without
discrimination  on  account  of  race  or  color.
The  motion  was  over-
ruled,  as  stated  by  the  court,  (p.  7,)  ‘‘upon  the  ground  that  the  court
had  no  authority  to  change  the  venire,  it  appearing  to  the  satisfaction
of  the  court  that  the  said  venire  had  been  regularly  drawn  trom  the
jury-box  according  to  law.’’

The  discrimination  was  made.

It  was  done  under  the  authority  of
a  law  of  the  State,  as  the  State  court  itself.  decided.

The  judicial  power  of  the  United  States  is,  by the  constitution,  ex-
tended  to  a//  cases  at  law  or  in  equity,  arising  under  the  Constitution
or  the  laws  passed  in  pursuance  thereof.

It  is  not  simply  to  cases,  but  to  @//  cases  which  in  the  case  of Martin
that  in
vs.  Hunter,  is  referred  to  as  showing  a  design  ‘‘ex  zadustria’’
this  class  of cases  enumerated  by  the  Constitution  a//  cases  whatsoever
should  be  included.

This,  of course,  includes  criminal  cases,  which  2re  cases  at  law,  where
a  Federal  question  arises.
In  the  case  of  Cohens  vs.  Virginia,  6th  Wh.,
this  Court  expressly  decided  that  the  judicial  power  of  the  United  States
extended  to  a  criminal  cause  when  a  State  was  a  party,  and  took  juris-
diction  of  it  under  its  appellate  powers,  and  asserted  its  power  to  re-
verse  the  action  of  the  State  court.

The  judicial  sewer,  therefore,  of the  United  States,  even  in  a  criminal
cause  in  certain  cases  does  therefore  so  far  trench  upon  the  sovereignty
it  may  seize  hold  of  the  controversy  even  after  the
of  a  State,  that
State  has  fully  considered  the  matter,  and  rendered  its  final  judgment
and  may  reverse  and  annul  the  deliberate  and  perfected  action  of  the
State  court.

This  cannot  be  denied  or  controverted.

The  question  can  arise  then  only  as  to  the  mode  of  exercising  that
power,  and  the  right  of  Congress  in  the  distribution  of  this  judicial
power  to  lodge  it  in  an  inferior  court,  to  wit:  The  Circuit  Court  of
the  United  States.

The  objection  to  it  cannot  be  that  a  State  isa  party.

At  about

the  time  of  the  decision  of  Martin  vs.  Hunter,  was  passed
the  act  of  1845,  in  relation  to  revenue  cases,  which  provided  for  th
-emoval  of  criminal  cases  from  State  to  Federal  Circuit  Courts.

This  act.  which  grew  out  of  the  opposition  of  the  New  England

States  to  the  war  with  Great  Britain,  provided  :—

‘‘Tf  any  suit  or  prosecution  be  commenced  in  any  State  court  against

“

sl

pearance,

anv  collector.  &c.,  under  color  of  this  act
for  any thing  done
by  virtue  of  thisact.  and  t he  defendant  shall,  at  the  time  of entering  his
file  his  petition,  &c.,  [as  in  this  case,]  it  shall  then  be  the
duty  of  the  State  court  to  proceed  no  further  in  the  cause,’’  &c.
It
then  provides  that  the  suit  shall  proceed  in  the  United  States  court,  as
if  brought  there  by  original  process.

The  act  of  1833  provides  for  the  removal  of  criminal  causes.
See  also  Vannevar vs. Bryant,  21  Wall.,  41  ; Hodgson  as.  Millard,  3
Grant,  Pa.,  416—Strong,  J.;  Com.  vs.  Artman,  3 Grant,  Pa.,  436—
Grier,  J.;  Case  of  Madison  J. Wells,  Alb.  Law  Journal  Feby.  9,  1878—
Bradley,  J.;  Story’s  Com.  on  the  Constitution,  sec.  1701;  Federalist
No.  82—approved  by  Story  in  above  section ; State  vs.  Hoskins,  77
North  Carolina,  530.

,

In  the  case  of  Blyew  vs.  The  United  States,  13  Wall.,  581,  the
United  States  Circuit  Court  took  jurisdiction  of  a  case  of  murder  in
Kentucky,  of  a  colored  person  by a  white  person,  on  the  ground  that
by the  law  of  Kentucky,  colored  persons  could  not  be  witnesses  against
white  persons,  under  the  act  of  Congress  of  April  9,  1866,  which  gave
to  the  Circuit  Courts  of  the  United  States,  jurisdiction  of  all  cases,
civil  and  criminal,  affecting  persons  who  are  denied  or  cannot  enforce
in  the  State  courts  any  of  the  rights  secured  to  them  by the  first  sec-
tion  of the  act.

The  court  decided  that  a  mere  wrfness  in  a  cause,  was  not  a party
As  to  juris-
‘*  affected’  by the  denial  of  his  right  to  give  testimony.
diction,  the  court,  Strong,  J.,  say:
‘‘  Was  then  the  prosecution  or
indictment  against  these  defendants  a  cause  affecting  any  such  persons  ?
If  it  was,  then  by the  provisions  of  the  act,  it  was  within  the  jurisdic-
tion  of  the  court,  and  if it  was  not,  that  court  had  no  jurisdiction.’’

This,  it  will  be  observed,  was  a  case  in  which  the  United  States
court  took  erzgina/  jurisdiction,  an  exercise  of  power  far  beyond  that
of removal  from  the  state  court.

Is  it not  manifest  that  Congress  in  the  exercise  of  its  power  to  en-
force  the  equal  protection  of  the  laws  by appropriate  legislation,  has
in  adopting  a  remedy  which  has  had  the  sanction  of  the  government
from  the  beginning  of  its  judicial  history,  used  those  means  which,
consistent  with  any  efficiency,  are  the  least  offensive  possible to the  dig-
nity  and  sovereignty  of  the  States?
‘To
have  held  out  to  the  class  the  amendments  were  designed  to  protect
the  remedy  only  of appeal  to  the  Supreme  court  of  the  United  States,
from  the  final  judgment  of  the  State,  would  have  been  simple  mockery,
an  evasion  of  the  obligations  of  the  amendments,  and  a  practical  aban-
donment  of  the  colored  race  to  the  tender  mercies  of  their  former
owners.  under  just  such  laws  as  this  court  has  said  led  to  the  adoption
of  the  last  three  amendments,  for  if  the  remedy  of  removal  is  denied
in  this  instance  upon  the  ground  that  it  is  unconstitutional  to  remove
all  criminal  causes
acriminal  cause,  the  remedy  must  be  denied  to

Could  it  have  done  less?

23

which  can  arise  under  the  amendments,  or  any  laws  passed  in  pur-
suance  thereof.

It  is all  along  to  be  borne  in  mind,  that  we  claim  no  right  of general
supervision  over  the  criminal  law  of the  State.
It  is  only  when  a  Fed-
eral  ingredient  is  involved,  and  for  the  purposes  of this  case  only  when
the  equal  protection  of the  laws  is denied  by  the  State.

This  Court  has  laid  down  a  much  broader  field  of Federal  jurisdic-
tion  than  it  is  necessary  for  us
It  has  held  that  Congress
may  confer  jurisdiction  upon  the  United  States  courts,  where  the  least
Federal  ingredient  is  involved.

Mayor  vs.  Cooper,  6  Wall.,  252.

to  invoke.

is  not

One  important  thing  1s apparently  overlooked  in the  resolutions  of  the
Assembly,  the  discussions  thereon,  and  in  the  position  of  counsel,  and
that  isthat  the  Government  of  the  United  States  is also  the  government
The  people  of  Virginia  had
belonging  to  the  people  of  Virgiuia.
their  full  share  in  creating  the  Government  of  the  United  States.
In
the  highest  sense  it  is  not  strictly  correct  to  speak  of  the  sover-
The  people  are  sover-
eignty  of  the  State  or  of  the  United  States.
The  United
eign,  and  they  have  established  both  governments.
States
It  is  owr  Government  as
a  foreigner  in  Virginia.
Virginians.
The  United  States  does  not  as  a  foreign  sovereign  pro-
pose  to  *‘invade’’  the  sovereignty  of  Virginia,  and  take  the  trial  of
criminals  out  of  its  hands  and  administer  the  law  according  to  its  own
ideas,  or  its  own  laws.
The  Federal  judges  are  citizens  of  Virginia.
The
The  jurors  ina  Federal  court  would  be  citizens  of  Virginia.
law  of  Virginia  is  to  be  observed  and  carried  out  to  its  fullest  ex-
tent  according  to  the  practice  of  hercourts.
The  only  practical  dif-
ference  between  the  State  and  Federal  court  is,  that  in  one  case  Judge
Tredway  would  preside,  and  in  the  other  Judge  Rives.
Both  are  citi-
zens  of  Virginia,  both  citizens  of  the  United  States,  both  bound  by the
law  of  Virginia,  both  equally  interested  in  preserving  the  honor  and
dignity  of  the  State  of  Virginia,  as  well  as  of  the  United  States.

The  interference  proposed,  however,  limited  as  it  is, can  be  entirely
avoided  by  the  State  by a  faithful  and  scrupulous  observance  on  her
part  of  the  obligations  of  the  Constitution  in  all  its  parts,  which  she,
with  the  other  States,  has  assumed,  and  by which  she  has  covenanted  to
abide,  and  thus  will  her  honor  and  dignity  be  best  preserved.

W.  WILLOUGHBY,

Of  Counsel.

EX  PARTE:  COMMONWEALTH  OF  VIRGINIA  ET  AL.

1

To  the  honorable  the  Supreme  Court  of  the  United  States:

Your  petitioner,  the  Commonwealth  of  Virginia,  respectfally
represents  that  J.  D.  Coles  has  been  for  the  last  five  years  and  still  is
the  county  court  judge  of  the  county  of  Pittsylvania,  in  said  common-
wealth,  duly  elected,  qualified,  and  acting  as  such  under  and  according
to  the  constitution  and  laws  of  the  said  commonwealtb.

2

day  of

That  at  a  stated  term  of  the  district  court  of  the  United  States  for
the  western  district  of  Virginia.  in  the  fourth  circuit,  begun  and  held
at  Danville,  within  and  for  said  district  and  circuit,  ou  the  25th  day  of
February,  A.  D.  1879,  and  continued  by  adjournment  to  and  includ-
ing the  27th  day  of  February,  1879,  an  indictment  was  found  in  said
United  States  district  court,  by the  grand jury  then  and  there  attending
said  court,  against  the  said  J.  D.  Coles,  alleging  and  charging  that  he,
,  in  the  year  of  our  Lord,  1878,
the  said  Coles,ontke
at  the  said  western  district  of  Virginia,  and  within  the  jurisdic.
tion  of said  district  court,  being  then  and  there  the  judge  of  the
eounty  court  of  Pittsylvania  County  aforesaid,  and  an  officer  charged
by law  with  the  selection  of  jurors  to  serve  in  the  cireuit  and  county
courts  of  said  county,  did  then  and  there  exclude  and  fail  to select  as
grand  and  petit  jurors  certain  citizens  of  said  county  of  Pittsylvania,
of  African  race  and  black  color,  said  citizens  possessing  all  other  quali-
fications  prescribed  by law,  and  being  by  him  the  said  J. D.  Coles,  judge
as  aforesaid,  excluded  from  the  jury  lists  made  out  by  him, as such
judge,  on  account  of  their  race,  color,  and  previous  condition  of  servi-
tude,  and  for  no  other  reason,  against  the  peace  of  the  United  States
and  their  dignity,  aud  against  the  form  of  the  statute  of  the  United
States  in  such  case  made  and  provided,  as  will  more  fully  appear  from
a  copy  of  said  indictment  herewith  filed.

3

Your  petitioner  shows  that  since  the  finding  of  said  indictment,  to
wit,  on  the  8th  day  of  March,  A.  D.  15879,  process  was  issued
thereon,  from  the  clerk’s  office  of said  district  court,  which  in the
endorsement  of the  United  States  district  attorney  upon  said  pro-
cess  and  in  the  return  endorsed  thereon  by  the  deputy  marshal  is styled
a  **  bench  warrant,”  directed  to  the  marshal of the  United  States  for
the  western  district  of  Virginia,  and  to  his  deputies,  or  any  or  either  of
them,  commanding  them,  in  the  name  of  the  President  of the  United
States  of  America,  to  apprehend  the  said  J.  D.  Coles  and  bring  him
before  the  said  district  court  of  the  United  States  in  Danville  to  an-
swer  the  indictment  aforesaid  on  the  first  day  of  the  next  term  there-
after.

Your  petitioner  farther  shows  that  said  process  was  delivered  to
James  C.  Luck,  a  deputy  of John  F. Lewis,  the  United  States  marshal  for
said  district,  by  bim  to  be  executed,  and  that  in  obedience  thereto,  and
under  theauthority  thereby  given, the  said  James  C: Luck,  assuch  deputy
marshal,  afterwards,  to  wit,  on  the  13th  day  of  March,  A.  D.  1879,
arrested  the  said  J.  D.  Coles  and  took  him  into  custody  and  continues
still  to  hold  the  said  J.  D.  Coles  in  his  custody  under  and  by
the  authority  of  the  said  process,  a copy  of  which  process  and
of  the  return  thereon,  endorsed  by  said  deputy  marshal,  is  here-

4

with  filed.

|

Your  petitioner  is advised  that  the  said  district  court  of  the  United
States  bad  and  has  no  jurisdiction  of  the  matters  charged  against  the
said  J.  D.  Coles  in  the  said  indictment;  that  they  constitute  no  offence
punishable  in  said  district  court,  and  that  the  finding  of  the  said  in-
dictment,  and  the  consequent  arrest  and  imprisoument  of  the  said  J.  D.

OriGc.  4—1

2

EX  PARTE:  COMMONWEALTH  OF  VIRGINIA  ET  AL.

Coles,  under  the  process  issued  thereon,  are  unwarranted  by  the  Consti-
tution  of  the  United  States,  or  any  law  made  in  pursuance  thereof;  and
are  not  only  in  violation  of  the  rights  of  the  said  J.  D.  Coles,  but  are  in
violation  also  of  the  sovereign  rights  of  your  petitioner,  whose  judicial
ofiicer  he  is,  and  to  whom  alone  be  can  be  held  accountable  for  his  acts
or  omissions  as  such  officer.

The  term  of  office  for  which  the  said  Coles  was  elected  as  county
court  judge  as  aforesaid  will  uot  expire  for  a  considerable  time
yet  to  come,  to  wit,  on  the  Ist  day  of  January,  1880,  nor  there-—
after  until  his  successor  shall  have  qualified.

5

To  the  end,  therefore,  that  the  laws  of  your  petitioner  may  be  duly
adininistered  by  the  said  J.  D.  Coles,  as  county  court  judge  of  the
county  of Pittsylvania,  without  let  or  hindrance,  and  with  his  functions
unimpaired,  it is proper  and  necessary  that  he  shall  be  discharged  from
the  illegal  custody  in  which  he  is  now  held  for  trial  under  said  indict-
ment  by color  of the  authority  of the  United  States  and  left  free  to  per-
form  his  official  duties  as  such  jadge  without  interference  on  the  part  of
the  district  court  of  the  United  States  for  the  western  district  of  Vir-
ginia,  or  of any  one  acting  under  its  authority.

6

Your  petitioner  therefore  prays  that  the  record  of  the  said proceedings
of  said  district  court  tor  the  western  district  of  Virginia  be
brought  by certiorari  into  this  court,  and  that  a  writ  of  habeas
corpus  be  awarded  by this  court,  directed  to  the  said  James  C.,
Luck,  deputy  marshal  for  the  said  John  I.  Lewis,  marshal  for  the  west-
ern  district  of Virginia,  commanding. bim  to  have  before  your  honors,  ou
aay  to  be  named  therein,  the  body  of  the  said  J.  D.  Coles,  together
with  the  cause  of his capture  and  detention,  that  he  may  undergo  and  re-
ceive  Whatsoever  this  court  shall  then  cousider  of  him  in  that  behalf.

And  your  petitioner  willever  pray,  &c.

FRED.  W.  M.  HOLLIDAY,

Governor  of  Virginia.

JAS.  G.  FIELD,

Att’y  Gen’l  of  Va.

I, Jas.  G.  Field,  attorney-geueral  of  Virginia,  do certify  that  the state-
ments  contained  in  the  foregoing  petition  are  true  so  far  as  they  are
within  my  personal  knowledge,  and  so  far  as  they  are  derived
from  the from  information,  I believe  they  are  true.

7

‘i further  certify  that  said  petition  is filed  in  pursuance  of pre-
amble  and  resolutions  of  the  general  assembly  of  Virginia,  approved
March  3d,  1879,  a  duly  authenticated  copy  whereof  is  herewith  filed.

Given  under  my  hand  this  7th  day  of  April,  1879.

JAS.  G.  FIELD,

Atty  Gen'l  of  Va.

Subscribed  &  sworn  to  before  me  this  7th  day of April,  A.  D.  1879.

D.  W.  MIDDLETON,

8

To  the  honorable  the  Supreme  Court  of the  Unite.  States:
Your  petitioner,  J.  D.  Coles,  respectfully  represents  that  he  is  a  citi-

EX  PARTE:  COMMONWEALTH  OF  VIRGINIA  ET  AL.

3

zen  of  the  United  States  and  of  the  State  of  Virginia,  residing  in  the
county  of  Pittsylvania,  in  said  State,  and  that  he  is  the  county  court
judge  of said  county  of Pittsylvania,  duly  qualified  and  acting  as  such.
Your  petitioner  further  shows  that  he  has  been  indicted  in  the  district
court  of  the  United  States  for  the  western  district  of  Virginia,  in  the
fourth  circuit,  held  at  Danville,  for  an  alleged  offence  against  the  laws
of the  United  States,  said  indictment  alleging  that  your  petitioner  being,
as  such  judge  and  officer,  charged  by  law  with  the  selection  of jurors  to
serve  in  the  circuit  and  county  courts  of  said  county  of  Pittsylvania,
did  exciude  and  fail  to  select  as  grand  and  petit  jurors  certain  citizens
of  said  county,  of African  race  and  black  color ; and  alleging  further  that
said  citizens  possessing  all  other  qualifications  prescribed  by  law  were
excluded  from  the  said  jury  lists  by your  petitioner,  as  judge  as  afore-
said,  on  account  of their  race,  color,  and  previous  condition  of servitude,
as  will  more  fully  appear  by  a  copy  of  said  indictment  herewith

filed.

|

Your  petitioner  further  shows  that  since  the  finding  of  said  in-
dictment,  to  wit, on  the  8th  day  of  March,  A.  D.  1879,  process  was
issued  thereon  from  the  clerk’s  office  of said  district  court,  which,  in  the
endorsement  of the  United  State  district  attorney  upon  said  process  and
the  return  endorsed  thereon  by  the  deputy  marshall,  is styled  a  **  bench
warrant,”  directed  to  the  marshal  of  the  United  States  for  the  western
district  of Virginia,  and  to  his  deputies,  or  any  or  either  of  them,  com-
manding  them,  in  the  name  of  the  President  of  the  United  States  of
America,  to  apprebend  your  petitioner  and  him  bring  before  the  said  dis-
trict  court  of  the  United  States  in  Danville  to  answer  the  indictment
aforesaid  on  the  first  day of the  next  term.

Your  petitioner  further  shows  that  said  process  was  delivered  to  James
C.  Luck,  a  deputy  of John  F. Lewis,  the  United  States  marshal  for  said
district,  by  him  to  be  executed,  and  that,  in  obedience  thereto,  and
under  the  authority  thereby  given,  the  said  James  C.  Luck,  as  such
deputy  marshall,  afterwards,  to  wit,  on  the  13th  day  of  March,  A.  D.
1879,  arrested  your  petitioner  and  took  him  into custody,  and  continues
still  to  hold  your  petitioner  in  his  custody,  under  and  by the  au-
thority  of  said  process,  a  copy  of  which,  and  of  the  return  en-
dorsed  thereon  by  said  deputy  marshal,  is herewith  filed.

10

Your  petitioner  further  shows  that,  as  he  is advised,  the  said  district
court  had  and  has  no jurisdiction  of the  matters  charged  against  him  in
said  indictment;  that  they  constitute  no  offence  punishable  in  said  dis-
trict  court;  and  that  the  finding  of  the  said  indictment  and  the  conse-
quent  arrest  and  imprisonment  of your  petitioner  are  unwarranted  by
the  Constitution  of the  United  States,  or  by any  law  made  in pursuance
thereof;  and  are  in  violation  of  the  rights  of  your  petitioner  and  of
the  State  of Virginia,  whose  judicial  officer  he  is.

In  order  that  he  may  be  relieved  from  the  illegal  custody  in which  he
is  thus  beld  for  trial  under  said  indictment  and  under  and  by color  of
the  authority  of  the  United  States,  vour  petitioner  prays  that.  the  rec-
ord  of  said  proceedings  of  said  district  court  be  brought  by certiorari
into  this  court;  and  that  a  writ  of  habeas  corpus  be  awarded  by  this
court  directed  to  the  said  James  C.  Luck,  deputy  marshal  for  the  said
John  F.  Lewis,  marshal  of  the  western  district  of  Virginia,  com-
manding  him  to  have  before  your  honors,  on  a  day  to  be  named
therein,  the  body  of  your  petitioner,  together  with  the  cause  of
bis  caption  and  detention,  that  he  may  undergo  and  receive  whatsoever
this  court  shall  then  consider  of  him  in  that  behalf.

11

And  as  in  duty  bound  your  petitioner  will  ever  pray,  &c.

J.  D.  COLES.

STATE  OF  VIRGINIA,

County  of Pittsylvania,  set:

In  the  western  district  of  Virginia.

On  this  the  22ud  day  of  March,  1879,  before  me,  a  notary  public  in
and  for  the  county  and  State  aforesaid,  duly  commissioned  and  quali-
fied,  personally  appeared  in  said  county  J.  D.  Coles,  the  petitioner
named  in  the  above  petition,  and  made  oath  that  he  had  duly  signed
the  same,  and  acknowledged  it  in  my  presence,  and  said  affiant  further
made  oath,  in  due  form  of  law,  that  the  facts  set  forth  in the  above  peti-
tion  are  true.

Given  under  my  hand  and  official  seal  this  the  22nd  of  March,  1879.

District  court  of  the  United  States  of  America  for  the  western

At  a  stated  term  of the  district  court  of the  United  States  of America
for  the  western  district  of  Virginia  in  the  fourth  cireuit,  begun  and  held
at  Danville,  within  and  for  the  district  and  circuit  aforesaid,  on  the  25
of  February,  in  the  year  of our  Lord  one  thousand  eight  hundred  and
seventy-nine,  and  covtinued  by adjournmeut  to and  including  the  27 day
of February,  in  the  year  of  our  Lord  one  thousand  eight  hundred  and
seventy-nine.

dav  of

The  jurors  of the  United  States  of America  within  and  for  the  district
and  circuit  aforesaid.  on  their  oath,  present  that  J.  D.  Coles,  late  of
Pittsylvania  County,  in  the  district  and  circuit  aforesaid,  heretofore,  to
wit,  on  the
,  in  the  year  of  our  Lord  one  thousand  eight
hundred  and  seventy-eight,  at  the  said  western  district  of Virginia,  and
within  the jurisdiction  of  this  court,  being  then  and  there  the  judge  of
the  county  court  of  Pittsylvania  County,  and  an  officer  charged  by law
with  the  selection  of jurors  to  serve  in circuit  & county  courts  of the  said
county  of Pittsylvania,  did  then  & there  exclude  & fail  to  select  as  jurors,
on  account  of  race,  color,  and  previous  condition  of  servitude,  certain
citizens  of the  county  aforesaid,  possessing  all  other  qualifications  pre-
scribed  by  law,  against  the  peace  of  the  said  United  States  and  their
dignity,  and  agaiust  the  form  of  the  statute  of  the  said  United  States
in  such  case  made  and  provided.

SECOND  COUNT.—And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  on  their  oath  aforesaid,
do  further  present  that  J.  D.  Coles,  late  of  Pittsylvania  County,  in  the

To  the  marshal  of  the  United  States  for  the  western  district  of

Virginia,  and  to  his  deputies,  or  any  or  either  of  them  :
Whereas  at  a  district  court  of  the  United  States  of  America  for  the
western  district  of  Virginia,  began  and  held  at  Danville,  within  and  for
the  western  district  of  Virginia  aforesaid,  on  the  26  day  of  Feb’y,  in  the
year of our Lord  one  thousand  eight  hundred  and  seventy-nine,  the grand
jurors  in  and  for  the  said  western  district  of  Virginia  brought  into  the
said  court  a  true  bill  of  indictment  against  J.  D.  Coles  for  having  vio-
lated  civil  rights,  as  by  the  said  indictment  now  remaining  on  file  and  of
record  in  the  said  court  may  more  fully appear,  to  which  indictment  the
said  J.  D. Coles  has  not  yet  appeared  or  pleaded:

EX  PARTE:  COMMONWEALTH  OF  VIRGINIA  ET  AL.

Now,  therefore,  you  are  hereby  commanded  in  the  name  of the  Presi-
dent  of the  United  States  of America  to apprehend  the  said  J.  D.  Coles,
and  him  bring  before  the  said  court  in Danville  to answer  the  indictment
aforesaid  on  the  first  day  of next  term.

Witness  the  honorable  Alexander  Rives,  judge  of the  said  court,  and
the  seal  thereof,  at  Danville,  in  said  district,  the  8  day  of  M’ch,  in  the
year  of our  Lord  one  thousand  eight  hundred  and  seventy-9.

Preamble  and  joint  resolution  instructing  attorney-general  to  de-
fend  citizens  of  Virginia  unlawfully  molested  by Federal  courts,
and  requesting  our  Representatives  in Congress  to  ask for  a committee
of  investigation  on  the  same  subject.  Approved  March  3d,  1879.
Whereas  five  judges  of this  Commonwealth,  to wit, J. B. Coles,  of Pitt-
sylvania,  S.  I.  Mullens,  of  Henry,  G.  H.  Bernard,  of Franklin,  Sem  G.
Staples,  of  Patrick,  and  T.  T. Bouldin,  of Charlotte,  have  been  indicted
in  the  circuit  court  of  the  United  States  for  the  western  district  of Vir-
ginia  for  the  alleged  ottence  of  failing  to  execute  the  laws  of  Virginia,
which  action  of  said  court  is,  in  the  opinion  of  this  general  assembly,
usurpation  and  an  exercise  of  unwarranted  power:  Therefore,

.

1.  Resolved  by  the  general  assembly  of Virginia,  That  the  attorney-
general  is  hereby  directed  to  take  proper  steps  to  bring  the  aforesaid
proceedings  to  the  adjudication  of the  supreme  court  of Virginia,  or  the
Supreme  Court  of  the  United  States,  in  order  that  such  lawless  meas-
ures  may  be  checked  by competent  authority  ; and  he  is  likewise
directed  to  appear  at  once  in  behalf  of  these  and  all  other  citizens
of  this  Commonwealth  who  may  hereafter  be  seized  and  held  con-

18

trary  to  law  by any  Federal  judge  or  court  in  Virginia.

2.  The  governor  is  hereby  authorized  to  employ  necessary  counsel  to
assist  the  attorney-general  in  proceedings  undertaken  under  the  direc-
tion  of this  resolution.

3.  That  the  Representatives  in  Congress  from  this  Commonwealth  are
hereby  requested  to  move  in  the  House  of  Representativesot  the  United
States  for  a  committee  to  enquire  whether  the  Hon.  Alexander  Rives,
judge  of the  district  court  of the  United  States  for  the  western  district

EX  PARTE:  COMMONWEALTH  OF  VIRGINIA  ET  AL.

7

of  Virginia,  holding  the  circuit  court  of  the  United  States  for  said  dis-
trict,  has  not  been,  and  is  not  now,  usurping  jadicial  power  for  the  pur-
pose  of  bringing  about  an  unnecessary  contlict  between  the  authorities
of the  State  of Virginia  and  of  the  United  States  in  the  enforcement  of
the  criminai  laws  of  this  Commonwealth  within  said  district,  thereby
endangering  the  harmony,  peace,  and  good order  of this Commonwealth.
