It
would  not  be  claimed  that  refusing  to  hold  to  bail  or  to

Suppose
that  the  Supreme  Court  should  discharge  the  petitioner
charged  with  an  offense  from  imprisonment,  and  not-
withstanding  this  the court  below  should  go on  with  the
trial,  as  in  Virginia  it may  do  in  the  absence  of  the  ac-
cused  in  case  of  misdemeanor,  and  should  convict  him
and  sentence  him  to  imprisonment,  what  could  be  done
about  it?
This  court  could  do  nothing,  and  therefore
it would  not  attempt  to  make  an  authoritative  decision
which  could  have  no  force  only  by way  of advice.

In  Kearny’s  case  (7 Wh., 38) the  court  refused  the  writ
where  the  petitioner  was  in contempt  of the  circuit  court
in refusing  to  answer  a  question.
The  court  say:  “The
objection  is  not  that  the  court  acted  beyond  its jurisdic-
This
tion,  but  that  it erred  in  its judgment  of  the  law.
would  be  asserting  a  right  to  control  a  criminal  court
and  to  revise  its  opinion.
This  court  cannot  revise  such
a judgment  or  the  proceedings  which  lead  to  it.”

:

In  the  case  of  Tobias  Watkins  (3  Peters,  193)  the
motion  was  founded  “upon  the  allegation  that  the  in-
dictment  charged  no  offense  for  which  the  prisoner  was
punishable  in  the  court  below,  or  of  whieh  the  court

5

could  take  cognizance,  and  consequently  that  the  pro-
ceedings  were  coram  non  judice  and  totally  void.”
But
this  court  held  that  the  judgment  was  conclusive  as  to
the  law  as  well  as  to  the  facts.
‘To  determine  whether
the  offense  charged  in the indictment be legally punishable
or  not  is  among  the  most  unquestionable  of  its  powers
and  duties.
The  decision  of this  question  is  the  exercise
of jurisdiction,  whether  the  judgment  be  for  or  against
the  prisoner.
It must  remain  in  full  force  until  reversed
regularly  by a  superior  court  capable  of  reversing  it.”
(See  page.  207;  also  ex  parte  Watkins,  7  Peters,  574;
ex  parte  Gordon,  1 Black,  503.)

In  re  Kaine  (14  How.),  at  page  129, the  court  says:
‘The  circuit  court  has  power  upon  its  own  views  of the
law  to  inflict:not  only  punishment  but  even  the  punish-
ment  of  death  without  appellate  control  by this  court.
Even  when  it is alleged  that  the  proceedings  of a  circuit
court  by which  a  citizen  is  imprisoned  are  coram  non
judice  and  void  its judgment  is final,  and  no  relief  can
be  had  here  by  writ  of  error  or  appeal  or  by  habeas
corpus.”

In  ex  parte  Parks  it was  claimed  that  the  indictment
did  not  set  out  an  offense  against  the  United  States,  and
that  for  that  reason  the  court  did  not  have  jurisdiction
to  trv  it.  This  court  said  that  this  was  one  of the  very
questions  which  was  to be submitted  to the  circuit  court,
and  that  for that  very  reason  such  a question  could  not  be
considered  on  an  application  for  a  writ  of  habeas  corpus.
Counsel  admit  that  it is  the  sole  province  of the  inferior
court  to  pass  upon  the  construction  of  a  statute,  but  at-
tempt  to  distinguish  this  from  passing  upon  the  consti-

In  both  cases  it  is  a  question  for  the  court  to  pass

This  question  is  for  such  court  alone.
Whether  wisely  or  unwisely  it  is  not  submitted  to  this
In  this  respect  the  two  courts—the  circuit  court
court.
and  the  Supreme  Court—are  independent  of each  other,
and  this  court  must  be  concluded  by  the  action  of  the
court.
What,  then,  is  meant  by  the  declaration  that
this  court  can  interfere  where  the  cireuit  court  has  no
jurisdiction,  or  where  it  has  acted  beyond  its  powers?
Let  us  look  at  some  of  the  cases  where  the  jurisdiction
of  this  court  has  been  maintained.

The  cases  of  ex  parte  Lange  (18  Wall.)  and  ex  parte
Yerger  (8  Wall.)  are  cases  relied  upon  by  the  petitioner
to  sustain  the jurisdiction  to  issue  the  writ.

In  Lange’s  case  the  decision  of this  court  was  that  the
eirewit  court  had  undertaken  to  imprison  the  petitioner
after  he  had  fully  and  completely  satisfied  the judgment
of  the  court  itself.
The  court  had  adjudged  the  pay-
ment  of  a  fine  and  imprisonment  when  it could  lawfully
impose  but  one—fine  or  imprisonment.
After  he  had
paid  the  fine,  and  it had  been  paid  into  the  Treasury,  so
that  it  could  not  be  returned,  the  court  undertook  to
change  its  sentence  and  impose  imprisonment  without
relieving  him  from  the  punishment  already  inflicted  and
Sully  executed.
This  the  circuit  court  had  no  power  to
This  want  of power  is  illustrated  by a  court  ulder-
do.

oan

taking  to  impose  a  punishment  of  death  in  a  case  of
libel;  also  by  the  case  of  Bigelow  vs.  Forrest,  9 Wall,
where  the  court  undertook  to  confiscate  the  fee  when  the
statute  allowed  the  confiscation  only  of  a  life  estate.
This  as  to  the  excess  was  held  to  be  void  even  in  the
collateral  action  of  ejectment.

Suppose  that  a  statute  imposes  as  a  punishment  of an
offense  described  by  it  imprisonment  for  one  year,  and
that  for  this  offense  the  cireuit  court  should  impose  an
imprisonment  of  three  years.  We  can  readily  see  how
It  can

In  er  parte  Lange  the  turning  point  seems  to  have
been  that  one  legal  sentence  had  been  fully  satisfied  be-
fore  any  attempt  was  made  to  impose  another,  and  after
this  such  change  could  not  be  made  even  by the  same
court.
But  no  attempt  was  made  to  impeach  the  judg-
ment  or  to  question  any  matter  passed  upon  prior  to  the
judgment.

be  seen  that  this  is  no  violation  of  the  rule  I have  at-

The  question  was  as  to  the  sentence.

In  ex  parte  Yerger  (8  Wall.)  the  simple  question  was
whether  the  petitioner  was  in  military  custody  or  in  the
custody  of  the  court.
He  had  applied  to  the  circuit
court  to  be  relieved  from  military  custody,  and  had
been  remanded  by order of the  cireuit  court,  and  then
he  applied  to  this  court  for  the  writ,  and  it  was  held
that  he  was  in  custody  under  the  order  of the  court,  and
therefore  by a  tribunal  over  which  this  court  had  appel-
late jurisdiction.
No  question  of  guilt  or  innocence  or
affecting  it  was  considered.

The  case  of  ex  parte  Bradley  (7  Wall.)  has  been  cited
to  show  that  this  court  will  review  the  action  of  the  in-
ferior  court  when  acting  beyond  its  powers  and  jurisdic-
This  was  the  case  of  an  application  for  a  man-
tion.
damus.
One  court  had  undertaken  to  disbar  Mr.  Brad-
ley  as  an  attorney  for  a  contempt  committed  before
another  court.
There  was  no  judgment  that  he  had
committed  a  contempt  before  the  court  that  had  passed
the  order  to  disbar  him.
Justice  Miller  seemed  to  think
that  the  order  of  the  court  did  not  show  that  it  was  for
a  contempt  committed  before  another  court,  and  there-
fore  dissented,  but  in  this  the  majority  of  the  court  dis-
agreed  with  him.

In  all  of  these  cases  it  can  be  seen  that  this  court
does  not  review  any  question  which  the  law  had  sub-
mitted  to  the judgment  of  the  circuit  court  or  which  it  |
was  its  duty  to  pass  upon  at  the  trial.

The  question  whether  an  act  of  Congress  presenting
an  offense  and  declaring  the  mode  of  trial  is  constitu-
tional  is  a  question  submitted  to  the  judgment  of  the
It  is most  plainly  a  question  relat-
circuit  court  alone.
ing  to  the  guilt  or  innocence  of  the  prisoner  to  be
passed  upon  by  the  court  at  the  trial.
Such  a  question
is within  the jurisdiction  of  the  court,  and  its judgment
thereon  is final  whether  erroneous  or  not.

Let  us  look  a  little  more  closely  at  the  proceedings  of
a  criminal  cause.
They  can  be  divided  into  these  two
classes:  those  that  relate  to  the  punishment  or  impris-
onment,  or  those  that  relate  to  the  guilt  or  innocence  of
the  accused,  the  question  at  issue  and  to  be  determined
at  the  trial.

The  question  of  bail  before  trial  is only  a  question  of
probable  cause,  and  the judgment  of the  court  as  to  this

«oo
IID PEST
GIP

9

question  has  no  connection  with  the  judgment  of  the
court  upon  the  trial.
If  the  only  purpose  here  were  to
admit  to  bail,  perhaps  this  court  would  for  such  purpose
have  jurisdiction,  for  its  judgment  on  such  a  question
would  not  be  an  interference  with  the  judgment  of  the
court  at  the  trial;  but  such  is  not  the  purpose,  for  in
fact  the  accused  is  not  imprisoned  and  is  practically
already  bailed  for  trial.
The  object  here  is  to  prevent  a
trial  and  to  get  an  authoritative  decision  for the  purpose
of controlling  the  action  of the court  at  the  trial.
There
has  been  no  refusal  by  the  court  below  to  admit  to  bail.
Again,  after  the  trial,  and  when  the  court  has  passed
its  judgment  as  to  whether  an  offense  has  been  com-
mitted  within  the  law  or  the  Constitution,  which  ques-
tions,  we  say,  are  committed  to  the  court  below  alone,
and  the  decision  of  which  is  binding  upon  all  other
courts,  whether  erroneous  or  not,  then  another  and  dis-
tinct  question  is presented  to  the  court,  and  that  is  as
This  is a  distinct  matter  from  the judg-
to  the  sentence.
ment.
So far  as  the  court  acts  within  the  discretion  com-
mitted  to  it by the  law  in passing  the  sentence  its  decis-
ion  upon  this  is  binding  upon  other  courts.
But  if  it
then  sentences  the  prisoner  to  a  term  of  imprisonment
not  allowed  by the  law,  as  to  this  the  Supreme  Court
ean  correct  it.  When  the  court  exceeds  its  powers,
such  an  excess  of power  is subject  to correction  by  habeas
corpus  or  mandamus,  whichever  may  be  the  appropriate
remedy.
It  is  in  such  cases  only  that  the  Supreme
Court  has  assumed  jurisdiction.

If  the  court  releases  an  imprisoned  party  after  trial
and  judgment,  its  power  is effectual  to  accomplish  the
object  proposed ; but  if  it  releases  a  party  before  trial,
what  is the  process  or  mandate  that  would  be  issued  to

the  court  below  to  enforce  its judgment  at  the  trial?
none  can  be  named,  is  not  the  absence  of  any  such  pro
cess  a  strong  argument  against  the  jurisdiction  of  the
court?
This  court  does  not  hear  cases  simply  for  the
purpose  of  giving  advice  or  expressing  opinions.

If

It  may  be  suggested  that  upon  sentencing  a  prisoner
after  the  judgment  a  question  may  arise  as  to  the  con-
struction  of  the  powers  of  the  court  in  that.  respect.
This  may  depend  upon  the  construction  of a  statute,  as,
for  example,  whether  the  court  has  power  to  sentence
to  a  term  of one  year  upon  each  count  of  an  indictment,
making  four  years  if  there  are  so  many  counts,  or  only
This  question  of  construction  is  sub-
one  year in all.
initted  to  the  court,  and  it  must  pass  thereon.
Now,  it
may  be  asked,  why  can  the decision  of  this  question  be
reviewed  and  not  the  question  of the  construction  or  the
constitutionality  of  the  act  upon  which  the  indictment
and  the  judgment  are  based  ?

The  answer  is  simply  this:  Because  in  the  first  case
the  statute  gives  to  this  court  the  power  to  issue  the
writ  for  the  purpose  of  inquiring  into  the  “cause  of
restraint  of liberty,”  while  the  power  is denied  to  ques-
tion  any  decision  which  goes  to  make  up  the judgment.
The  court  will  look  at  the judgment  simply  to  ascertain
whether  it  is  a  sufficient  “‘cause”  for  the  restraint  of
liberty,  but  will  not  undertake  to  impeach  the judgment
itself  for  any  reason  whatever.
The  judgment  of  the
court  to  which  the  question  has  been  submitted  is just
as  conclusive  upon  the  constitutionality  of  the  act  upon
which  the  charge  is based  as  upon  any  other  judicial
question.

It  may  be  regarded  as  a  question  of  abstract  right
and  propriety  that  the  judgment  of the  court  as  to ques-

VST  aa a es  Seb  OSE a  nm  eet

k
f
f

|

'

1]

tions  properly  arising  at  the  trial  should  be  reviewed  by
this  court  as-well  as  to  matters  relating  purely  to  the
question  of restraint  of  liberty,  but  it  is sufficient  for  us
to  reply  such  is  not  the  will  of  the  legislative  power.

Whether  this  is  a  sufficient  reason  or  not  for  the  dis-
tinction  I have  endeavored  to make,  still  the  fact  remains
that  this  court  has  acted  upon  such  a  distinction,  and
has  not  in  a  single  instance  transcended  the  rule  I have
expressed,  and  has  never  undertaken  upon  an  inquiry  as
to  the  cause  of  commitment  simply,  to  pass  upon  the
construction  or  constitutionality  of  an  act  upon  which
a  criminal  charge  is  based,  or  upon  any  other  question
properly  cognizable  at  the  trial  of  the  cause.

By  the  act  of  March  3,  1879  (20  Stat.  L.,  p.  354),  writ
of  error  may  now  issue  from  the  cireuit  court  of  the.
United  States  to  correct  any  error  of  the  district  court.
The  petitioner  alleges  that  he  is  held  under  process
issuing  from  the  district  court.
He  has  a  full  and  com-
plete  remedy  for any  possible  wrong  that  may  be  done
to  him  by application  in  the  proper  form  to  the  cireuit
There  can  certainly  be  no  necessity  for  him  to
court.
come  to  this  court  in  the  first  instance;  and  can  he  do
it,  passing  by  the  cireuit  court?
I suggest  this  inquiry
for  the  consideration  of  the  court.

As  to  the  propriety  and  constitutionality  of  the  pro-
ceedings  complained  of,  I beg  leave  to  refer  to  a  brief
filed  by me  in  the  case  of  Virginia  vs.  Rives,  original
No.  1, now  pending  in  this  court.

W.  WILLOUGHBY,
On  behalf  of  United  States.

BERT  SIEBOLD  ET  AL.,  Petitioners.  ‘  pus  and  Certiorari.

To  the  Honorable  the  Supreme  Court  of the  United  States  :

The  petition  of  Albert  Siebold,  Walter  Tucker,  Martin
C.  Burns,  Lewis  Coleman,  and  Henry  Bowers,  all  citizens
of  the  State  of  Maryland,  respectfully  shows  to  this  honor-
able  court,  that  they  are  deprived  of  their  liberty  by John
M.  McClintock,  esq.,  marshal  of  the  United  States  for  the
District  of  Maryland,  and  James  H.  Irvin,  esq.,  warden  of
the  jail  of  the  city  of  Baltimore,  and  that  they  are  thus
held  in  custody,  by the  said  marshal  and  warden,  by color
of  the  authority  of  the  United  States,  contrary  to  the  Con-
stitution  and  laws  of  the  United  States.

Your  petitioners  show  that  on  the  5th  day  of  November,
1878,  an  election  was  held  in  the  State  of  Maryland,  at
which  representatives*in  Congress  of  the  United  States
were  voted  for, and  that  the  time,  place,  and  manner  of
holding  such  election  had  been  prescribed  in  Maryland  by
the  legislature  thereof,  which  had  enacted  such  laws  as  in
their  judgment  were  fit and  proper  to  regulate  the  manner
of  holding  such  election.

That  your  petitioners  were  judges  of  said  election,  duly
appointed  under  said  state  laws,  and  deriving  all  their  pow-
ers  and  functions  from  such  state  laws.

On  the  18th  day  of  October,  1879,  before  me,  a  commis-
sioner  of  the  United  States  Circuit  Court  for  the  District
of  Maryland,  personally  appeared  Albert  Seibold,  Walter
Tucker,  Martin  C.  Burns,  Lewis  Coleman,  and  Henry
Bowers,  the  above-named  petitioners,  and  made  oath,  in
due  form  of  law,  that  the  facts  set  forth  in  the  above  pe-
tition  are  true,  at  the  same  time  subscribing  their  signatures
thereunto.

[In  testimony  whereof,  witness  my,hand  and  seal,  the  day

and  date  last  above  mentioned.

ISAAC  BROOKS,  Jr.,

United  States  Commissioner

with  the  District  of Maryland.

To  the  Honorable  MORISON  Lt.  WAITE,  Chief  Justice  of
the  Supreme  Court  of the  United  States,  and  allotted  to
the  Fourth  Circuit,  in  which  1s  comprised  the  District  of
Maryland.

The  petition  of  Albert  Siebold,  a  citizen  of  the  State  of
Maryland,  respectfully  represents  to  your  Honor,  that  he  is
deprived  of his liberty  by John  M.  McClintock,  Esq.,  Marshal
of the  United  States  for  the  District  of Maryland,  and  James
H.  Irvin,  Warden  of  the  Jail  of  the  City  of  Baltimore,
and  that  he is thus  held  in  custody  by color  of the authority
of  the  United  States,  contrary  to  the  Constitution  and  Laws
of  the  United  States.

Your  petitioner  shows  that  on  the  5th  day of November,
1878,  an  election  was  held  in  the  State  of  Maryland  for
members  of  Congress  of  the  United  States,  and  that  the
time,  place  and  manner  of  holding  such  election  had  been
prescribed  by  the  Legislature  of  the  State  of  Maryland,
which  had  enacted  such  laws  as,  in  their  judgment,  were  fit
and  proper  to  carry  on  such  election.

[2]

That  your  petitioner  was  one  of  the  Judges  of  said
election,  duly  appointed  under  said  State  laws,  and  deriving
all  his  powers  and  functions  from  such  State  laws  ;

That  he  discharged  his  duty  as  such  State  officer,  under
said  State  laws  at  said  State  election,  to  the  best  of his  judg-
ment,  knowledge  and  ability  ;

That  he  was  indicted  and  convicted  at  the  November
Term,  1878,  of  the  Circuit  Court  of  the  United  States  for
the  District  of  Maryland,  on  an  indictment  charging  him,
under  the  name  of Albert  Siebold,  alias  Albert  Kloman,  that
being  a  Judge  of  the  election  for  members  of  Congress,  as
aforesaid,  under  State  laws,  on  the  5th  of  November,  1878,
did  an  act  unauthorized  by  the  laws  of  the  State  of  Mary-
land,  or  of  the  United  States,  contrary  to  section  5515  of
the  Revised  Statutes  of  the  United  States  ;

That  on  said  conviction  he  was  sentenced  to  be  im-
prisoned  eighteen  months  in  the  Jail  of  the  city  of  Balti-

more,  and  was,  by  said  Court,  committed  to  the  custody  of
said  John  McClintock,  Esq.,  Marshal,  and  James  H.  Irvin,
Esq.,  Warden,  as  aforesaid,  and  by  virtue  of  which  judg-
ment  said  Marshal  and  Warden  now  hold  him  ;

All  of  which  will  more  fully  appear  by the  copy  of  the
record  of  the  proceeding  in  said  Court,  duly  certified  and
herewith  exhibited  as  part  of  this  petition.

Your  petitioner  avers  and  charges  that  the  Acts  of
Congress  under  which  this  pretended  conviction  is alleged
to  have  taken  place,  are  unconstitutional  and  void,  and  con-
ferred  no  jurisdiction  on  the  said  Circuit  Court,  either  as  to
the  person  of  your  petitioner  or  the  subject-matter  with
which  said  Court  attempted  to  deal.

And  he  further  avers  that  no  law  of  Congress  exists
which  can  confer  jurisdiction  over  your  petitioner  in  the
matter  whereof  he  hath  been  convicted  by  said  Court,  to
wit:  the  due  discharge  of  his  duty  as  a  State  officer,  and
that,  therefore,  all  the  proceedings  of  said  Court  are  with-
out  jurisdiction,  null  and  void.

Wherefore  he  prays  from  your  Honor  the  benefit  of  the
writ  of Habeas  Corpus  to  the  said  John  M.  McClintock,  Esq.,
Marshal  as  aforesaid,  and  James  H.  Irvin,  Esq.,  Warden  as
aforesaid,  directed,  ordering  them  to  have  the  body  of  your
petitioner  before  your  Honor  on  some  day,  at  some  place,  to
be  in  said  writ  designated,  together  with  the  true  cause  of

his  detention.

JOHNSON  &  Por,

BERNARD  CARTER,

For  Petitioner.

CHARLES  J.  M.  Gwinn,

Attorney  General  of Maryland,
by direction  of the  Governor  of Maryland,

ALBERT  SIEBOLD.

MARYLAND,  Scr.

On  this  9th  day  of  September,  1879,  before  me,  a  Com--
missioner  of ‘the  United  States  Circuit  Court  for  the  District
of  Maryland,  personally  appeared  Albert  Siebold,  the  above
named  petitioner,  and  made  oath  in  due  form  of  law  that
the  facts  set forth  in  the  above  petition  are  true,  at  the  same
time  subscribing  his  signature  thereunto,

United  States

Commissioner  for the  District  of Maryland.

GeEoRGE  Morris  Bonn,

To  the  Honorable  MORISON  R.  WAITE,  Chief  Justice  of
the  Supreme  Court  of the  Uniled  States,  and  allotted  to
the  Fourth  Circuit,  in  which  is comprised  the  District  of
Maryland.

The  petition  of  Walter  Tucker,  a  citizen  of  Maryland,
respectfully  represents  to  your  Honor,  that  he  is  deprived  of
his  liberty  by  John  M.  McClintock,  Esq.,  Marshal  of  the
United  States  for  the  District  of  Maryland,  and  James  H.

[4]

Irvin,  Esq.,  Warden  of  the  Jail  of  the  city  of  Baltimore,
and  is thus  held  in  custody  by them  by color  of the  authority
of  the  United  States,  contrary  to  the  Constitution  and  Laws
of the  United  States.

:
Your  petitioner  shows  that  on  the  5th  day of November,
1878,  an  election  was  held  in  the  State  of  Maryland  for
members  of  the  Congress  of  the  United  States,  and  that  the
time,  place  and  manner  of  holding  such  election  had  been
prescribed  by  the  Legislature  of  the  State  of  Maryland,
which  had  enacted  such  laws  as,  in  their  judgment,  were  fit
and  proper  to  carry  on  such  election.

|

That  your  petitioner  was  one  of  the  Judges  of  said
election,  duly  appointed  under  said  State  laws,  and  deriving
all  his  powers  and  functions  from  such  State  laws.

That  he  discharged  his  duty  as  such  State  officer,  under

said  State  laws,  at  said  State  election.

That  he  was  indicted  and  convicted  at  the  November
Term,  1878,  of  the  Cirenit  Court  of  the  United  States  for
the  District  of  Maryland,  on  an  indictment,  charging  that
he,  being  a  Judge  of  election,  under  State  laws,  at  the  said
election  of  November  5th,  1878,  at  which  a  member  of  Con-
gress  was  voted  for,  did,  by  the  exercise  of  his  power  and
authority  as  such  State  Judge,  hinder  and  prevent  a  certain
United  States  Deputy  Marshal  and  a  certain  so-called  United
States  Supervisor  of  Elections  from  discharging  duties  of in-
specting,  superintending  and  supervising  the  said  election
for  member  of  Congress;  which  said  Deputy  Marshal  and
so-called  Supervisor  were  appointed  under  sections  2012,
2021,  2022  of  the  Revised  Statutes  of  the  United  States.

Your  petitioner  was  also  charged  in and  convicted  under
the  same  indictment,  that  he,  being  a  Judge  of election,  ap-
pointed  under  the  laws  of Maryland,  did  an  act  unauthorized
by any  law  of  Maryland  or  of  the  United  States,  contrary
ta  cantinm~  5514,  5515  and  5522  of the  said  Revised  Statutes.
her  shows,  that  on  said  conviction,  he  was  sen-
imprisoned  for  twelve  months  in  the  jail  of  the

la  Le

A  OM  Ns  me  08  1  DE aaa a

[5 J

city  of  Baltimore,  and  was,  by said  Court,  committed  to  the
custody  of  the  said  John  M.  McClintock,  Esq.,  Marshal,  as
aforesaid,  and  James  H.  Irvin,  Esq.,  Warden  as  aforesaid,
and  by virtue  of  which  judgment  said  Marshal  and  Warden
now  hold  him,  and  unconstitutionally  deprives  him  of  his
liberty ; all  of  which  will  more  fully  appear  by a certified
copy  of  the  proceedings  before  said  Court,  herewith  filed  as
part  of  this  petition.

Your  petitioner  avers  and  charges  that  the  Acts  of
Congress  under  which  this  pretended  conviction  is alleged
to  have  taken  place,  are  unconstitutional,  and  conferred  no
jurisdiction  on  the  said  Circuit  Court,  either  as  to  the  person
of  your  petitioner  or  the  subject-matter  with  which  said
Court  attempted  to  deal.

And  he  further  avers  that  all  of  the  proceedings  of said
Court,  under,  and  by  color  of  which,  he  is  held  in  custody,
are  without  jurisdiction  and  void.

Wherefore  he  prays  your  Honor  to  grant  him  the  ben-
efit  of  the  writ  of  Habeas  Corpus  to  the  said  John  M.
McClintock,  Esq.,  Marshal  as  aforesaid,  and  James  H.  Irvin,
Esq.,  Warden  as  aforesaid,  directed,  ordering  them  to  have
the  body  of  your  petitioner  before  your  Honor  at  some  time
and  place  to  be  in  said  writ  designated,  together  with  the
true  cause  of  his  detention.

And  as  in  duty  bound  he  will  ever  pray,  &c.

On  this  9th  day  of  September,  1879,  before  me,  a  Com-
missioner  of  the  United  States  Circuit  Court  for  the  District

| 6

of Maryland,  personally  appeared  Walter  Tucker,  the  above
petitioner,  and  made  oath  in  due  form  of  law  that  the  facts
set  forth  in  the  above  complaint  are  true,  and  at  the  same
time  subscribed  his  name  thereuntw.

me  at  the  city  of  Baltimore  and  State  of

identical  with  that  of  Tucker,  except  name  of  peti-

ORDER  OF  THE  CHIEF  JUSTICE.

Lyme,  Connecticut.

The  petition  of Henry  Bowers,  Martin  C.  Burns,  Walter
Tucker,  Lewis  Coleman  and  Albert  Siebold,  that  the  benefit
of  the  writ  of  Habeas  Corpus  be  extended  to  them  and
issued  to  John  M.  McClintock,  Marshal  of the  Uuited  States
for  the  District  of  Maryland,  and  James  H.  Irvin,  Warden
of  the  Jail  of  the  city  of  Baltimore,  directing  them  to  have
the  bodies  of  the  said  petitioners  before  me,  together  with
the  true  cause  of  their  detention,  at  some  time  and  place  to
be  therein  designated,  having  been  read  and  considered,  it
is  this  10th  day  of September,  1879,  ordered  by  me,  Morison
R.  Waite,  Chief  Justice  of the  Supreme  Court  of  the  United
States,  allotted  to  the  Fourth  Circuit,  in  which  is  comprised
the  District  of Maryland,  that  the  said  John  M.  McClintock,
Marshal  as  aforesaid,  and  James  H.  Irvin,  Warden  as  afore-
said,  do  show  cause  before  me,  on  the  second  Tuesday  of
October  next,  in  the  city  of  Washington,  why  the  said  writs
should  not  issue  as  prayed  in  the  said  petitions.

And  it  is  further  ordered  that  copies  of  said  petitions,
and  of  this  order,  shall  be  served  on  the  said  Marshal  and

Siiaitharca ee

Warden  at  least  ten  days  before  the  day  above  appointed  for
the  hearing.

Unrtep  STATES  OF  AMERICA,

District  OF  MARYLAND,

In  the  Circuit  Court  of the  United  States,

for  the  Fourth  Circuit,  in  and  for  the  Maryland  District.

The  Grand  Inquest  of  the  United  States  of  America,
for  the  Maryland  District,  inquiring  for  the  body  of  that
district,  do,  on  their  oaths,  present,  that  heretofore,  on  the
fifth  day  of  November,  in  the  year  of  our  Lord  eighteen
hundred  and  seventy-eight,  at  the  Fourth  Congressional
District  of  the  State  of  Maryland,  in  the  Maryland  District
aforesaid,  a  lawful  election  was  held,  whereat  a  Representa-
tive  for  that  Congressional  District  in  the  Forty-Sixth  Con-
gress  of  the  United  States  was  voted  for.

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  a  certain  John  T.  Hodges  was,  then
and  there,  a  Supervisor  of  Election  of  the  United  States,
duly  appointed  such  by the  Circuit  Court  aforesaid,  pursuant
to  the  two  thousand  and  twelfth  section  of  the  Revised

[8 |

Statutes  of  the  United  States,  for  the  third  voting  precinct
of  the  Fifteenth  Ward  of  the  city  ot  Baltimore  in  the  said
Congressional  District,  for  and  in  respect  of  the  election
aforesaid,  thereat.

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  a  certain  Charles  A.  Lusby  was,  then
and  there,  a  Special  Deputy  Marshal  of  the  United  States,
duly  appointed  such  by the  United  States’  Marshal  for  the
Maryland  District,  pursuant  to  the  two  thousand  and  twenty-
first  section  of  said  Revised  Statutes,  and  assigned  for  such
duty  as  is  provided  by that  section  and  the  thereto  next  fol-
lowing  section  of  said  Revised  Statutes,  to  the  said  third
voting  precinct  of  said  Fifteenth  Ward  of  the  said  city  of
saltimore,  at  the  Congressional  election  aforesaid,  thereat.

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  Albert  Siebold,  late  of the  said  district,
yeoman,  otherwise  called  Albert  Kloman,  on  the  said  fifth
day  of  November,  in  the  year  aforesaid,  at  the  Maryland
District  aforesaid,  to  wit:  At  the  said  precinct  of  said  ward
of  the  said  city,  in  the  District  and  Congressional  District
aforesaid,  (being  then  and  there  a  judge  of  said  election  in
and  for  the  said  precinct,  created  such  under  the  authority
of  the  State  of  Maryland),  unlawfully  did  obstruct,  hinder,
and  by threats,  intimidation,  and  menace  of arrest,  interfere
with  the  said  Charles  A.  Lusby,  who  was  then  and  there
such  Deputy  Marshal  as  aforesaid,  in  the  performance  of  a
certain  duty  required  of  him,  and  which  he  was  then  and
there  authorized  to  perform  by section  twenty  hundred  and
twenty-two,  before  mentioned  of  said  Statutes,  to  wit:  the
duty  of  supporting  and  protecting  the  said  Supervisor  of
Election  at  the  polls  of  said  precinct,  during  said  election
thereat,  in  the  discharge  of  the  duties  of  him,  (the  said
Supervisor),  and  preventing  fraudulent  voting  at  the  said
polls  of  said  election,  contrary  to  section  fifty-five  hundred
and  twenty-two  of  said  Statutes  and  against  the  peace,  goy-
ernment  and  dignity  of  the  United  States.

aforesaid,  (he  being  then  and  there  such  judge  of  said  elec-
tion  for  that  precinct  as  aforesaid),  did  refuse  to  receive  the
vote  of  one  Asa  Rue,  who  was  then  and  there  entitled to
vote  at  the  same  election  in  the  same  precinct,  contrary  to
the  section  last  aforesaid  of  said  Statutes,  and  against  the

Count  Firtu.

‘

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  the  said  Albert  Siebold,  otherwise
called  Albert  Kloman,  on  the  day  and  year  aforesaid,  at  the
precinct  aforesaid,  within  the  district  aforesaid,  (he  being
then  and  there  such  judge  of  said  election  as  aforesaid),  did
violate  a  certain  duty  on  him  as  such  judge  as  aforesaid,
imposed  by the  law  of the  State  of  Maryland ; that  is  to  say,
by  the  twentieth  section  of  the  Act  of  Assembly  of  that
State,  entitled  an  Act  to  provide  for  the  revision  of  the  lists
of  registered  voters  in  this  State,  approved  April  7th,  1876,
A.  D.,  which  requires  that  the  judges  of  election  of  said
State  shall  not  receive  or  deposit  in  the  ballot-box  the  ballot
of  any  person  offering  to  vote  until  they  have  found  his
name  in  the  list  of  qualified  voters;  by  then  and  there
knowingly  receiving  and  depositing  in  the  ballot-box  of said
precinct  the  vote  of  a  certain  person,  (whose  name  is  to  said
jurors  as  yet  unknown),  at  the  said  election  in  said  precinct,
before  the  name  of  the  same  person  had  been  found,  to  wit:
by the  judges  of  said  election  of  said  precinct  in  the  list  of
the  qualified  voters  thereof;  whereas,  in  truth,  and  in  fact,
the  name  of  the  same  person  was  not  then  and  there  on  the
list  of  the  qualified  voters  of  said  precinct,  contrary  to  sec-
tion  fifty-five  hundred  and  fifteen  of  said  Statutes,  and
against  the  peace,  government  and  dignity  of  the  United
States

Count  SIXTH,

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  the  said  Albert  Sieman,  otherwise

"

{ 11  |

called  Albert  Kloman,  on  the  day  and  year  aforesaid,  at  the
precinct  aforesaid,  within  the  district  aforesaid,  (he  being
then  and  there  such  judge  of the  same  precinct  as  aforesaid),
unlawfully  at  the  said  election,  by  threat  and  intimidation,
did  prevent  a  certain  qualified  voter  of  the  same  precinct,
whose  name  is  to  said  jurors  as  yet  unknown,  from  freely
exercising  the  right  of  sutfrage,  to  wit:  at  said  election  in
the  said  precinct,  contrary  to  section  fifty-five  hundred  and
eleven  of  said  Revised  Statutes,  and  against  the  peace,  gov-
ernment  and  dignity  of  the  United  States.

CounT  SEVENTH.

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  de
further  present,  That  the  said  Albert  Siebold,  otherwise
called  Albert  Kloman,  afterwards,  to  wit:  on  the  day  and
year  first  aforesaid,  at  the  precinct  and  district  aforesaid,
(he  being  then  and  there  such  officer  of  the  State  election  as
aforesaid),  unlawfully  did,  as  such  judge  of  the  election
aforesaid,  (as  herein  aforesaid),  for  said  voting  precinct,
violate  a  duty  on  him  imposed  by the  said  twentieth  section
of  the  Act  of  Assembly  of  the  State  of  Marvland,  by,  then
and  there,  and  thereat  wrongfully  and  corruptly,  and  con-
trary  to  said  Act  of  Assembly,  refusing  and  denying  duly
to  ascertain  whether  the  name  of  a  certain  person,  (whose
name  is  to  the  jurors  as  yet  unknown),  who  duly  offered  to
vote  at  said  election  in  said  precinct,  and  in  that  behalf  ten-
dered  his  ballot  to  the  said  judge  thereof,  was  then  and
there  to  be  found  in  the  list  of  the  qualified  voters  thereof ;
but  to  the  contrary  thereof,  causing  and  inducing,  by and
through  threats,  intimidation,  artifice  and  deceit,  the  said
person,  offering  to  vote  as  aforesaid,  to  leave  and  depart  the
poll  of  election  of  said  precinct,  without  having  voted
thereat,  and  without  it having  been  ascertained  by  the  said
judge  of  election,  (to  whom  the  said  ballot  was  tendered  as
aforesaid),  whether  or  not  the  name  of  the  identical  person
who  then  and  there  offered  to  vote  as  aforesaid,  was  to  be
found  in  list  of qualified  voters  of the  said  precinct,  contrary

[ 12]

to  the  said  fifty-five  hundred  and  fifteenth  section  of  said
Statutes,  and  against  the  peace,  government  and  dignity  of
the  United  States.

Count  EIGnta.

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  the  said  Albert  Seibold,  otherwise  called
Albert  Kloman,  afierwards,  to  wit:  on  the  day  and  year
aforesaid,  at  the  precinct  aforesaid,  within  the  district  afore-
said,  (he being  then  and  there  such  officer  and  judge  of said
election  as  aforesaid),  knowingly  did  an  act  at  the  said  _
tion  unauthorized  by any  law  of the  State  of  Maryland,  and
unauthorized  by  any  law  of the  United  States,  by,  then  and
there,  and  thereat,  at  the  said  election,  clandestinely  and
fraudulently  puiting  and  placing  tn  the  ballot-box  of the  said
voting  precinct  certain  ballots,  (within  the  intent  and  meaning
of the jifty-five  hundred  and  fourteenth  section  of
said Statutes
of the  United  States),  to  ihe  number  of four  and  more,  after
the  voling  at  the  said  precinct  at  and  for  said  election  had
begun,  and  before  the  ballots  voted  at  the  same  election  in  that
precinct,  and  lawfully  deposited  in  the  same  ballot-box,  had

been  counted,  with  intent  to  affect  the  same  election,  and  the
result  thereof,  contrary  to  section  jifiy-jive  hundred  and fifteen
of the  Revised  Staiutes  of the  United  States,  and  against  the
peace,  government  and  dignity  of the  United  States.

ARCHIBALD  STIRLING,  JR.,

Whereupon  the

said  Grand  Inquest  having  retired
from  the  bar  of  the Court  here  to  inquire  of  and  concerning
the  said  indictment,  it afterwards  returned  the  said  indict-
ment  to  the  Court  thereon  indorsed,  to  wit:

[A  true  biil. |

W.  B.  Brooks,  Foreman.

And  the  said  Albert  Siebold,  alias  Albert  Kloman,
having  heard  the  said  indictment  read,  saith  that  he  is  Noy
guilty  thereof,  and  hereupon  puts  himself  upon  the  country  |
and  Archibald  Sterling, Jr.,  Esq.,  District  Attorney  afore.

[13 ]

said,  who,  for  the  said  United  States  in  this  behalf  prose-
cuteth,  doth  the  like.

Whereupon  the  panel  of  petit  jurors,  returned  to  the
Court  here  by the  Marshal  of  the  district  aforesaid,  accord-
ing  to  the  Statutes  in  such  case  made  and  provided,  being
called,  come,  whereupon  certain  of  them,  to  wit:  Philip  8.
Cross,  Charles  W.  Beatty,  William  Galloway,  James  A.
Lewis,  W.  C.  Dickinson,  Arthur  Stabler,  William  Brown,
Benj.  Chesney,  8.  C.  Gorrell,  Jacob  Campbell,  Frisby  J.
Davis,  and  Jas.  Z.  Browning  are  sworn  to  say  the  truth  in
the  premises,  and  the  said  jurors  so  being  empannelled  and
sworn  to  say  the  truth  in  the  premises,  upon  their  oath  and
affirmation  do  say,  that  the  said  Albert  Siebold,  alias  Albert
Kloman,  zs  guilty  of the  premises  aforesaid  in  the  eighth
count  in  the  indictment  aforesaid,  above  specified  and  laid
to  his  charge,  in  manner  and  form  as  by the  said  indictment
is  above  charged  upon  him,  and  not  guilty  of  the  premises
aforesaid  in  the  first,  second,  third,  fourth,  fifth,  sixth  and
seventh  counts  in  the  said  indictment,  above  specified  and
laid  to  his  charge  in  manner  and  form  aforesaid.

Thereupon  it is considered  by  the  Court  here  that  the
said  Albert  Siebold,  alias  Albert  Kloman,  do  pay  a  fine  to
the  said  United  States  of  five  dollars,  and  the  cost  accrued
It  is  also  considered  by
upon  the  prosecution  aforesaid.
the  Court  here  that  the  said  Albert  Siebold,  alias  Albert

Kloman,  be  imprisoned  in  the  Jail  in  Baltimore  city  for  and
during  the  term  of  eighteen  months;  and  the  said  Albert
Siebold,  alias  Albert  Kloman,  is  now  committed  by  the
Court  here  to  the  custody  of  the  Marshal  of  the  district
aforesaid,  by him  to  be  taken  and  delivered  into  the  custody
of  the  Warden  of  the  Jail  of  Baltimore  city,  there  to  be
kept  in  safe  custody,  &c.

MEMORANDUM.

Verdict  was  rendered  in this  case  on  the  27th  February,
1879 ; and  judgment  was  rendered  on  the  28th  February,
1879.

The  Grand  Inquest  of  the  United  States  of  America,
for  the  Maryland  District,  inquiring  for  the  body  of  that

i  November,  in  the  year  of  our  Lord  eighteen
fifth  day  o
hundred  and  seventy-eight,  at  the  Fourth  Congressional

gress  of  the  United  States  was  voted  for.

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do

further  present,  That  a  certain  Frederick  8.  Hammond  was,
then  and  there,  a  Supervisor  of Election  of the  United  States,
duly  appointed  such  by the  Circuit  Court  aforesaid,  pursuant

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  a  certain  James  N.  Schofield  was,  then
and  there,  a  Special  Deputy  Marshal  of  the  United  States,
duly  appointed  such  by the  United  States’  Marshal  for  the

lowing  section  of  said  Revised  Statutes,  to  the  said  first
voting  precinct  of  said  ward  of  the  said  city  of  Baltimore,
at  the  Congressional  election  aforesaid,  thereat.

ARCHiBALD  STEKLING,  JR.,

Whereupon  the  said  Grand  Inquest  having  retired  from
the  bar  of  the  Court  here,  to  inquire  of  and  concerning  the

Court  here,  thereon  endorsed,  to  wit:

'  A  true  bill.  —

W.  3B.  Brooks,  Foreman.

And  the  said  Justus  J.  Gude  and  Walter  Tucker  having
heard  the  said  indictment  read,  say  that  they  are  not  guilty
thereof,  and  hereupon  put  themselves  upon  the  country,  and
Archibald  Sterling,  Jr.,  Esq.,  District  Attorney  aforesaid,

who  for  the  said  United  States  in  this  behalf  prosecuteth,
doth  the  like.

Whereupon  the  panel  of  petit  jurors  returned  to  the
Court  here  by the  Marshal  of  the  district  aforesaid,  accord-
ing  to  the  Statutes  in  such  case  made  and  provided,  being

[18 ]

called,  come,  whereupon  certain  of  them,  to  wit:  W.  W.
Sullivan,  Philip  8S.  Cross,  Charles  W.  Beatty,  William
Galloway,  N.  C.  Dickerson,  Joseph  H.  Kelly,  J.  Z.  Brown-
ing,  Frisby  J.  Davis,  Jacob  Campbell,  Charles  B.  Fox,
Skipwith  C.  Gorrell,  Benjamin  Chesney,  are  sworn  to  say
the  truth  in  the  premises,  and  the  said  jurors  so  being  em-
panneled  and  sworn  to  say  the  truth  in  the  premises,  upon
their  oath  do  say,  that  the  said  Justus  J.  Gude  and  Walter
Tucker  are  guilty  of the premises  aforesaid  in  the  second  and
Fifth  counts  in  the  indictment  aforesaid,  above  specified  and
laid  to  their  charge,  in  manner  and  form  as  by  the  said  in-
dictment,  is  above  charged  upon  them,  and  not  guilty  of  the
premises  aforesaid  in  the  first,  third  and  fourth  counts  in
the  said  indictment,  above  specified  and  laid  to  their  charge

in  manner  and  form  aforesaid.

Thereupon  it  is  considered  by  the  Court  here  that  the
said  Justus  J.  Gude  and  Walter  Tucker  do  each  of  them
pay  a  fine  to  the  said  United  States  of  five  dollars,  and  the
costs  accrued  upon  the  prosecution  aforesaid,

It  is  also  considered  by  the  Court  here  that  the  said
Justus  J.
Gude  and  Walter  Tucker  be  imprisoned  in  the  jail
in  Baltimore  city  for  and  during  the  term  of twelve  months,
from  January  27th,  1879,  and  the  said  Justus  J.  Gude  and
Walter  Tucker  are  now  committed  by the  Court  here  to  the
custody  of  the  Marshal  of  the  district  aforesaid,  by  him  to
be taken  and  delivered  into  the  custody  of the  Warden  of the
Jail  of  Baltimore  city,  there  to  be  kept  in  safe  custody,  &c.

MEMORANDUM.

Verdict  was  rendered  in  this  case  on  the  27th  day  of

January,  1879.

Judgment  was  rendered  on  the  15th  February,  1879.

INDICTMENT.

Unrrep  STATES  OF  AMERICA,

District  oF  MARYLAND,

In  the  Circuit  Court  of the  United  States,

for  the  Fourth  Circuit,  in  and  for  the  Maryland  District.

The  Grand  Inquest  of  the  United  States  of  America,
for  the  Maryland  District,  inquiring  for  the  body  of  that
district,  do,  on  their  oaths,  present,  that  heretofore,  on  the
fifth  day  of  November,  in  the  year  of  our  Lord  eighteen
hundred  and  seventy-eight,  at  the  Fifth  Congressional  Dis-
trict  of  the  State  of  Maryland,  in  the  Maryland  District
aforesaid,  a  lawful  election  was  held,  whereat  a  Representa-
tive  for  that  Congressional  District  in  the  Forty-Sixth  Con-
gress  of  the  United  States  was  voted  for.

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  a  certain  James  McKenny  was,  then
and  there,  a  Supervisor  of  Election  of  the  United  States,
duly  appointed  such  by the  Circuit  Court  aforesaid,  pursuant
to  the  two  thousand  and  twelfth  section  of  the  Revised
Statutes  of  the  United  States,  for  the  fifth  voting  precinct
of  the  Seventeenth  Ward  of the  city  ot  Baltimore  in  the  said
Congressional  District,  for  and  in  respect  of  the  election
aforesaid,  thereat.

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  a  certain  David  Duncan  was,  then
and  there,  a  Special  Deputy  Marshal  of  the  United  States,
duly  appointed  such  by the  United  States’  Marshal  for  the
Maryland  District,  pursuant  to  the  two  thousand  and  twenty-
first  section  of  said  Revised  Statutes,  and  assigned  for  ‘such
duty  as  is provided  by that  section  and  the  thereto  next  fol-
lowing  section  of  said  Revised  Statutes,  to  the  said  fifth
voting  precinct  of  said  ward  of  the  said  city  of  Baltimore,
at  the  Congressional  election  aforesaid,  thereat ; and  that
John  M.  McClintock  was  then  and  there  United  States
Marshal  for  the  District  of  Maryland.

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  Martin  C.  Burns,  late  of  the  said  dis-
trict  yoeman,  on  the  said  fifth  day  of November,  in  the  year
aforesaid,  at  the  Maryland  District  aforesaid,  to  wit:  at  said
precinct  of  said  ward,  within  the  Congressional  District
aforesaid,  (he being  then  and  there  a jndge  of said  election  for
the  said  voting  precinct,  created  such  under  the  authority  of
the  State  of  Maryland),  unlawfully  at  the  said  election  did
obstruct,  hinder,  and  by use  of  his  power  and  authority  as
such  judge  as  aforesaid,  interfere  with  the  said  James
McKenny,  (who  was  then  and  there  such  Supervisor  of
Election  as  aforesaid  in  the  due  execution  of  that  office),  in
the  performance  of  certain  duty  of  him  required,  and  which
he  was  then  and  there  authorized  by  the  two  thousand  and

seventeenth  section  of  said  Statutes  to  perform,  that  is  to
say,  the  duty  of  personally  inspecting  and  scrutinizing,  on
the  day of said  election,  the  manner  in  which  the  voting  was
done  at  the  polls  of  said  election  for  said  precinct,  by  re-
fusing  and  denying  to  allow  the  said  Supervisor  of  Election
to  inspect  the  ballot-box  of  said  poll  of  election  before  any
vote  voted  thereat  had  been  deposited  in  the  same  box,  con-
trary  to  section  fifty-five  hundred  and  twenty-two  of  said
Statutes,  and  ayainst  the  peace,  government  and  dignity  of
the  United  States.

Count  SECOND.

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  the  said  Martin  C.  Burns,  afterwards,
to  wit:  on  the  day  and  year  aforesaid,  at  the  precinct  afore-
said,  unlawfully,  at  the  said  election,  did  hinder  and  prevent
the  free  attendance  and  presence  of the  said  James  McKenny,
(who  was  then  and  there  such  Supervisor  of  Election  as
aforesaid,  in  the  due  execution  of  that  office),  at  the  said
poll  of  said  election,  to  wit:  at  and  within  the  room  where
the  same  poll  of  election  was  then  and  there  had  and  held,
by then  and  there  refusing  and  denying  to  admit  him  into

[ 21]

the  same  room,  and  forcibly  keeping  him  out  of  the  same,
contrary  to  the  said  fifty-five  hundred  and  twenty-second
section,  and  against  the  peace,  government  and  dignity  of
the  United  States.

Count  THIRD.

And  the jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oath  aforesaid,  do
further  present,  That  the  said  Martin  C.  Burns,  afterwards,
to  wit:  on  the  day  and  year  aforesaid,  at  the  precinct  afore-

said  David  Duncan,  (who  was  then  and  there  such  Special

office),  in  the  performance  of  the  duty  of  him  required,  and

ing  and  taking  into  custody,  and  before  the  chief  Supervisor
of  Elections  for  the  said  district,  the  said  Martin  C.  Burns,
for  committing  the  offences  upon  him  respectively  imposed
in  the  first  and  second  counts  of  this  indictment,  contrary
to  the  said  fifty-five  hundred  and  twenty-second,  and  against
the  peace,  government  and  dignity  of  the  United  States.

Count  Fourtu.

And  the  jurors  aforesaid,  upon  their  oaths  aforesaid,  do
further  present  the  said  Martin  C.  Burns,  afterwards,  to

said,  unlawfully,  at  the  said  election,  did  obstruct  and  hin-
der  the  said  John  McClintock,  (who  was  then  and  there  such
Marshal  of  the  United  States  as  aforesaid,  in  the  due  execu-
tion  of that  office),  in  the  performance  of the  duty  (which  he

voing  counts  of  this  indictment,  contrary  to  the  Statute  of

to  section  fifty-five  hundred  and  twenty-two  of  said  Revised

ARCHIBALD  STERLING,  JR.,

/

Whereupon  the  said  Grand  Inquest  having  retired  from

7

[ A  true  bill.  |

W.  B.  Brooks,  foreman.

And  the  said  Martin  C.  Burns  having  heard  the  said
indictment  read,  saith  that  he  cannot  deny,  but  that  he  is
guilty  of the  misdemeanor  aforesaid  in  the  indictment  afore-
said,  above  laid  to  his  charge,  in  manner  and  form  as  by
the  said  indictment,  is  above  against  him  alleged,  and  puts
himself  upon  the  mercy  of  the  Court  here.

Therefore,  it  is  considered  by  the  Court  here  that  the
said  Martin  C.  Burns  do  pay  a  fine  to  the  said  United
States  of  five  dollars,  and  the  costs  accrued  upon  the  prose-
cution  aforesaid.
It  is  also  considered  by  the  Court  here
that  the  said  Martin  C.  Burns  be  imprisoned  in  the  jail  in
Baltimore  city  for  and  during  the  term  of  twelve  montlis  ;
and  the  said  Martin  C.  Burns  is  now  committed  by  the
Court  here  to  the  custody  of  the  Marshal  of  the  district
aforesaid,  by him  to  be  taken  and  delivered  into  the  custody
of  the  Warden  of  the  Jail  of  Baltimore  city,  there  to  be
kept  in  safe  custody,  &c.

MEMORANDUM.

Plea  of guilty,  and  judgment  of the  Court  was  rendered

on  the  24th  February,  1879.

[31]

NOTE  OF  ARGUMENT.

These  cases  involve  the  question  of the  constitutionality
of  certain  sections  of  Title  NAVI  of  the  Revised  Statutes,
entitled  ‘*  The  Elective  Franchise.’’

Sect.  2011.  The  Judge  of  the  Circuit  Court  of  the
United  States,  wherein  any  city  or  town  having  upwards  of
twenty  thousand  inhabitants  is  situated,  upon  being  in-
formed  by two  citizens  thereof,  prior  to  any  registration  of
voters  for,  or  any  election  at  which  a  Representative  or  Dele-
gate  in  Congress  is  to  be  voted  for,  that  it is  their  desire  to
have  such  registration  or  election  guarded  and  scrutinized,
shall  open  the  Circuit  Court  at  the  most  convenient  point  in
the  circuit.

Sect.  2012.  The  Judge  shall  appoint  two  Supervisors  of

Election  for  every  election  district  in  such  city  or  town.

Sect.  2016.  The  Supervisors  are  authorized  and  re-
quired  to  attend  all  times  and  places  fixed  for  registration  of
voters;  to  challenge  such  as  they  deem  proper;  to  cause
such  names  to  be  registered  as  they  may  think  proper  to  be
so  marked  ; to  inspect  and  scrutinize  such  register  of voters  ;
and  for  purposes  of  identification  to  affix  their  signatures  to
each  page  of  the  original  list.

Sect.  2017.  The  Supervisors  are  required  to  attend  the
times  and  places  for  holding  elections  of  Representatives  or
Delegates  in  Congress,  and  of  counting  the  votes  cast;  to
challenge  any  vote,  the  legality  of  which  they  may  doubt ;
to  be  present  continually  where  the  ballot-boxes  are  kept,

until  every  vote  cast  has  been  counted,  and  the  proper  re-
turns  made,  required  under  any  law  of tne  United  States,  or

any  State,  Territorial  or  Municipal  law;  and  to  personally
inspect  and  scrutinize  at  any  and  all  times,  on  the  day  of
election,  the  manner  in  which  the  poll  books,  registry  lists
and  tallies  are  kept  ;  whether  the  same  are  required  by any
law  of  the  United  States,  or  any  State,  Territorial  or  Muni-
cipal  laws.

These  sections  provide  for  ‘guarding  and  scrutinizing”’
the  conduct  of  State  officers,  and  the  manner  in  which  they
hold  the  election  under  State  laws.

|

Section  2021  requires  the  Marshal,  wherever  any  elec-
tion  at  which  Representatives  or  Delegates  in  Congress  are
to  be  chosen,  upon  application  by  two  citizens  in  cities  or
towns  of more  than  twenty  thousand  inhabitants,  to  appoint
Special  Deputy  Marshals,  whose  duty  it  shall  be  to  aid  and
assist  the  Supervisors  in  the  discharge  of  their  duties,  and
attend  with  them  at  all  registrations  of  voters  or  elections
at  which  Representatives  in  Congress  may  be  voted  for.

Section  2022  requires  the  Marshal,  and  his  General  and
Special  Deputies,  to  keep  the  peace  and  protect  the  Supervi-
sors  in  the  discharge  of  their  duties  ;  preserve  order  at  such
place  of  registration,  and  at  such  polls  ;  prevent  fraudulent

registration  and  voting,  or  fraudulent  conduct  on  the  part  of
any  officer  of  election,  and  immediately  to  arrest  any  person
who  commits,  or  attempts  to  commit,  any  of  the  offences
prohibited  herein,  or  any  offence  against  the  laws  of  the
United  States.

These  sections  provide  the  power  by  which  State  officers

of  election  are  to  be  prevented  from  committing  fraud  on
The  Supervisors  are  to  guard  and  scrutinize  the
elections.
©
manner  in  which  the  State  officers  perform  their  duties,  and
the  Special  Deputy  Marshals,  to  prevent  them  from  commit-
ting  frauds  on  the  State  laws.
The  Supervisors  are  to  de-
tect  crime,  and  the  Special  Deputy  Marshals  are  to  arrest  the
These  are  the  duties  imposed  by
criminal  State  Judges.
the  Statute  on  these  Federal  officers.

Then  follow  the  sections  imposing  punishments  for  the
erimes  detected  by the  Supervisors,  and  of the  criminals  ar-
rested  by  the  Special  Deputy  Marshals.

Section  5514  provides  and  enacts  the  particular  kind
of  testimony  which  shall  be  sufficient  to  convict  of  any  of
these  crimes.,  It  declares  that  where  the  law  of  any  State

requires  the  name  of any  candidate  to  be  voted  for  as  Repre-
sentative  in  Congress  to  be  written  on  any  ballot,  with

| 33]

the  names  of  other  candidates  to  be  voted  for  at  the  same
election  as  State,  &c.  officers,  it  shall  be  deemed  sufficient
prima  facie  evidence,  to  convict  any  person  charged  with
voting,  or  offering  to  vote  unlawfully,  under  the  provisions
of  this  chapter,  to  prove  that  the  person  so  charged,  cast,
or  offered  to  cast  such  a  ticket,  wherein  the  name  of  such
Representative  might,  by law,  be  printed  or  written;  or  that
the  person  so  charged,  committed  any  of  the  offences  de-
nounced  in  this  chapter  with  reference  to  such  ticket.

Thus  the  amount  of  evidence  necessary  to  convict  is

designated  and  marked  out.

Section  5515  enacts  that  every  officer  of an  election,  at
which  any  Representative  or  Delegate  in  Congress  is  voted
for,  whether  such  officer  of  election  be  appointed,  or  created
by  or  under  any  law  or  authority  of the  United  States,  or  by
or  under  any  State,  Territorial  district  or  municipal  law  or
authority,  who  neglects  or  refuses  to  perform  any  duty  in
regard  to  such  election  required  of  him  by  any  law  of  the
United  States,  or  of  any  State  or  Territory  thereof;  or  who
violates  any  duty  so  imposed  ;  or  who  knowingly  does  any
act  thereby  unauthorized,  with  intent  to  affect  any  such
election,  or  the  result  thereof;  or  who  fraudulently  makes
any  false  certificate  of  the  result  of  such  election  in  regard
to  such  Representative  or  Delegate  ;  or  who  withholds,  con-
ceals  or  destroys  any  certificate  of record  so  required  by law,
respecting  the  election  of  any  such  Representative  or  Dele-
gate ; or  who  neglects  or  refuses  to  make  and  return  such
certificate,  as  required  by  law;  or  who  aids,  counsels,  pro-
cures  or  advises  any  voter,  person  or  officer  to  do  any  act  by
this  or  the  preceding  sections  made  acrime;  or  to  omit  to
do  any  duty,  the  omission  of  which  is by this  section  made
a  crime;  or  attempts  to  do  so,  shall  be  punished  as _ pre-
scribed  in  section  5510.

This  section  defines  such  acts  as,  when  done  by State

officers  of  election,  shall  be  crimes.

os

ied BAe EOE  EI  SI

[ 34  |

Section  5522  enacts  that  every  person,  whether  with  or
without  any  authority,  power  or  process,  or  pretended
authority,  power  or  process  of any  State,  Territory  or  Munic-
ipality,  who  obstructs,  hinders,  assaults,  or  by bribery,  solic-
itation  or  otherwise,  interferes  with  or  prevents  the  Super-
visor  of  Election,  or  the  Marshal,  or  his  general  or  special
deputies,  in  the  performance  of  any  duty  required  of  them,
or  which  they  may  be  authorized  to  perform  by any  law  of
the  United  States,  in  the  execution  of  process  or  otherwise  ;
or  who,  by  any  of  the  means  before  mentioned,  hinders  or
prevents  the  free  attendance  and  presence  at,  and  full  and
free  access  to  and  egress  from  the  place  of  registration  or
poll  of election  ; or  place  of canvass  of votes  ; or  making  any
returns  thereof;  or  who  removes  from  any  of  these  said
places  any  Supervisor  or  Marshal,  or  threatens,  or  attempts
to  do  so;  or  who  refuses  to  assist  the  Supervisor  and  Marshal,
or  the  Special  Deputy  Marshal,  when  required  by them,  or
either  of  them,  to  give  such  aid,  shall  be  liable  to  instant
arrest,  without  process,  and  shall  be  punished  by  fine  and
imprisonment.

|

This  section  extends  the  panoply  of  perfect  immunity
over  the  Supervisor  and  the  Special  Deputy  Marshal  at  the
times  specified.
No  Sheriff,  with  process  from  any  State
Court,  can  arrest  either  of  them  for  any  crime,  but  they  can
instantly  arrest  the  Sheriff,  without  process,  for  attempting
to  execute  his  writ.

All  State  laws  on  those  days  are  prostrate  under  the
feet  of  the  Supervisor  and  the  Special  Deputy  Marshal,  and
all  State  officers  as  well,  for  no  matter  what  infraction  of
State  laws  they commit,  nor  what  duties  State  officers  perform,
the  State  laws  are  paralyzed  and  the  State  officers  powerless.
Such  are  the  provisions  of  law  for  guarding  and  scruti-
nizing  the  elections,  and  for  providing  the  means  whereby
State  officers  can  be  compelled  to  do  their  duty.

This  being  the  state  of  the  law,  as  set  out  on  the  Stat-
ute  book,  the  petitioners  were  convicted  under  these  sections,
and  duly  sentenced  to  prison,  where  they  now  are.

[35]
They  were  all judges  of the  election  for  Representatives
in  Congress,  held  in  the  city  of  Baltimore  on  the  5th  day  of
November,  1878,  appointed  under  the  laws  passed  by  the
Legislature  of  Maryland,  which  had  also  prescribed  the
time,  place  and  manner  of  holding  elections  for  Representa-
tives  in  Congress.  They  have  been  indicted,  tried,  convicted
and  imprisoned  as  State  Judges  of  Election,  appointed
under  State  authority  to  exccute  State  laws.

Siebold  has  been  convicted  on  the  eighth  count  of  the
indictment  against  him,  which  charged  him,  that  being  a
State  Judge  of  Election  he  knowingly  did  an  act  unautho-
rized  by any  law  of  the  State  of Maryland,  and  unauthorized
by any  law  of  the  United  States,  by then  and  there,  at  said
election,  clandestinely  and  fraudulently,  putting  in  the  bal-
lot-box  of  the  precinct,  certain  ballots,  after  the  voting  at
said  precinct  at  said  election  had  begun,  and  before  the  bal-

lots  lawfully  deposited  in  the  ballot-box  had  been  counted,
with  intent  to  affect  the  same  election,  and  the  result
thereof.

Tucker  was  convicted  on  the  second  and  fifth  counts  of

the  indictment  against  him.

The  second  count  charges  that  he,  being  a  Judge  of
Election,  created  as  such  under  the  laws  of  Maryland,  did
unlawfully,  and  by the  exercise  of  his  power  and  authority
as  such  judge,  prevent  and  hinder  a  Special  Deputy  Mar-
shal  in  his  free  attendance  and  presence  at  the  polls,  and  his
full  and  free  access  to  the  said  poll.

The  fifth  count  is  identical  with  the  one  on  which
Siebold  was  above  convicted,  to  wit:  being  a  State  Judge,
doing  an  act  unauthorized  by  the  laws  of  Maryland,  or  of
the  United  States,  with  the  intent  to affect  such  election,  and
the  result  thereof.

-  Burns  plead  guilty  to  the  indictment  against  him,
which  charged  him  in  four  counts,  that  being  a  State  Judge
of  Election  he  did,  by the  use  of his  authority  and  power  as
such  judge,  unlawfully  interfere  with  a  Supervisor  of  Elec-

the  manner  in  which  the  voting  was  done  at  the  election,  by

ballot-box  containiag  no  ballots  whatever.

These  are  the  charges  on  which  the  petitioners  have
been  tried,  convicted  and  sentenced,  and  under  which  they
are  now  suffering  imprisonment,  from  which  imprisonment
they  pray  to  be  delivered  by Habeas  Corpus.

If  these  Statutes  under  which  they  are  imprisoned  are

void,  they  are  entitled  to  their  liberties.

If  they  are  valid  exercises  of  legislative  p  wer,  then

the  petitioners  are  without  wrong.

We  shall  attempt  to  demonstrate  that  the  power  exer-
cised  by  Congress,  through  these  sections  of  tle  Revised
Statutes,  is  not  granted  by the  first  clause  of  the  fourth  sec-

[37 |

tion  of the  first  article  of  the  Constitution,  and  is  not  a  con-
stitutional  exercise  of  the  power  to  make  by law  such  regu-
lations  as  to  the  times,  places  and  manner  of  holding  elec-
tions  for  Representatives  in  Congress,  as  is granted  by the
|
said  clause.

We  shall  attempt  to  establish  these  propositions.

1.  That  the  power  to  make  regulations  as  to  the  times,
places  and  manner  of  holding  elections  for  Representatives
in  Congress,  granted  to  Congress  by the  Constitution,  is an
exclusive  power  when  exercised  by  Congress.

_ 2.  That  this  power,  when  so  exercised,  being  exclusive
of all  interference  therein  by the  States,  must  be so  exercised
as  not  to  interfere  with  or  come  in  collision  with  regulations
presented  in  that  behalf  by  the  States,  unless  it provides  for
the  complete  control  over  the  whole  subject  over  which  it is
exercised.

3.  That  when  put  in operation  by Congress  it must  take
the  place  of  all  State  regulations  of  the  subject  regulated,
which  subject  must  be  entirely  and  completely  controlled
and  provided  for  by Congress.

3efore  entering  upon  the  immediate  discussion  of  these
propositions,  it  is  necessary  to  make  a  preliminary  examina-
tion  of  the  origin  and  development  of  the  American  institu-
tions  of  free  government,  and  of  the  organization  of  the
American  Constitutions  under  them.

The  American  Constitutions  were  the  development  by
natural  evolution,  from  time,  place  and  circumstance,  of those
institutions  of  Anglo  Saxon  liberty,  which  had  been  the
growth  of  centuries  of  struggle  between  power  and  the
sturdy  spirit  of  liberty  of  our  ancestors.

Class  after  class  forced  itself  forward  into  the  enjoy-
ment  of  rights,  which  were  continually  enlarged  by  their
growing  power  and  intelligence,  and  to  which,  in  turn,  they
Rights  achieved  were  se-
as  constantly  admitted  others.

cured  by  guarantees  against  power,  and  checks  upon  its
absolute  exercise.

Trial  by jury  was  the  substitution  of  the  judgment  of

twelve  men,  for  the  irresponsible  will  of  one  or  many.

Magna  Charta  was  a  grant  of  security  of  life,  limb  and
member,  and  property,  as  well  against  the  strong  hand;  and
;t provided  that  twenty-five  Barons  should  be  guardians  of
the  liberties  thereby  granted,  with  the  right  and  duty  of
making  war  on  the  King  whenever  he  denied  any  of  those
privileges.

And  as  property  and  intelligence  "increased,  further

checks  and  safeguards  were  devised.

Parliament  was  the  great  council  of  the  King,  sum-
moned  by his  royal  mandate  to  inform  and  advise  with  him.

His  Chancellor  issued  writs  of  summons  and  election,
and  likewise  decided  upon  the  returns  thereof,  whether  they
had  been  properly  executed  and  the  proper  Councillors  had
But  in  time  the
been  sent  up  in  obedience  to  his  mandate.
people  began  to  claim  the  right  to  participate  in government,
with  larger  liberty  and  more  effective  interference.
They
long  claimed  the  right  to  pay  only  such  taxes  as  their  rep-
resentatives  had  levied,  but  it  was  not  until  the  reign  of the
Tudors  had  passed  that  they  asserted  and  exercised  the  power
of free  deliberation  and  action  in their  law  making  assembly.

In  the  reign  of  James  the  First,  the  Commons  insisted
that  they  should  be  sole  judges  of  the  election,  qualification
and  return  of  their  members,  and  that  writs  for  elections
should  be  issued  only  by their  speaker.
Thus  was  achieved
the  right  to  make  their  own  laws,  and  levy their  own  taxes,
after  full  and  free  debate,  and  statement  of grievances.  And
still  further,  to  secure  this  liberty,  privilege  of  Parliament
was  extended  so  as  to  give  the  Commons  power  to  protect
itself  against  all  intrusion.

Close  following  these  came  the  Petition  of  Right,  the
armed  protest  against  infringement  of  their  rights  by  the

'  And  the  history  of  England,  for  seven  centuries,  had

liberty  against  absolute  and  irresponsible  power,  and  every

thus  planted  by  Coke  blossomed  in
America,  and  bore  fruit  in  the  independence  of  the  Judici-

This  history  was  well  known  to  our  ancestors,  who
The  fathers  of some  of
framed  the  American  Constitutions.
them  had  borne  arms  in  the  struggle  between  William  of
The  grandfathers  of  others  had
Orange  and  James  II.
fought  in  the  great  rebellion.
The  grandfather  of  George
Mason,  the  author  of  the  Bill  of  Rights  of  Virginia,  had
