The  lessons  of  those  times  were  deeply  impressed  on
and  thoroughly  appreciated  by  them.
From  them  they
learned ; they  knew  that  power  concentrated  in  one  centre
is  destructive  to  liberty ; that  power  divided  among  many,
each  acting  as  check  on  the  other,  tends  to  preserve  and
The  safeguards  devised  by  the  experi-
perpetuate  liberty.

ence  of  their  Anglo-Saxon  ancestors  had,  in  the  main,  been
based  on  force,  and  had  relied  on  the  strong  hand  for  pres-

ervation  and  enforcement.

but  they  knew  that  to  preserve

their  liberties,  inherited  from  the  struggles  of  centuries,  en-
jarged  by  their  own  virtue  and  valor,  the  powers  of  society,
which  were  necessary  for  government,  must  not  be  concen-

trated  to  be  exercised  from  one  or  two  or  a  few  central  or-
ganizations,  but  must  be  separated  and  divided  among  as
many  as  possible,  consistent  with  efficient  administration  ;
and  these  separations  of  the  repositories  of  power  must  be
effective,  and  prevent  any  collision  or  fusion  between  them.
While  they  kuew  that  the  sanction  of  liberty  and  order  in
society  ultimately  rests  on  force,  they  believed  that  by thus
dividing  the  governmental  functions  between  different  de-

ec ee _

{41}

partments,  which  should  mutually  check  and  guard  each
other,  they  would  remove  as  far  as  possible  the  necessity  for
the  recourse  to  force  to  prevent  or  to  remedy  usurpation  of
power,  and  to  preserve  their  liberties.

All  the  old  systems  were  studied.

The  organizations
of  Athens,  of  Rome,  of  Poland ;  the  checks  of  the  ancient
republics ; and  the  balance  of  all  the  popular  governmerts
of  classic  times,  were  discussed  in  the  public  journals,  by
pamphlets,  in  addresses  and  writings.

The  debates  in  the  convention  of 1787,  and  in  the  State
conventions  ; the  papers  of  the  Federalist,  and  contempora-
neous  discussions  like  John  Adams’  Defence  of the  American
Constitution,  exhausted  all  history—sacred  and  profane—in
their  examinations  of  the  experience  of  former  nations  who
had  achieved,  who  had  maintained,  and  who  had  then  lost
their  liberties.

~

The  discussion  in  America,  from  1764  to  1790,  on  the
nature  and  objects  of  government,  is  without  parallel  in
recorded  history,  for  its  knowledge,  analysis  and  logic;  and
the  experience  of their  ancestors,  illuminated  by the  light  of
the  history  of  other  people,  taught  them  that  power,  by its
own  nature,  always  tends  to  extend  itself,  and  by the  law  of
its  existence,  constantly  develops  and  expands  itself;  and,
therefore,  they  understood  and  clearly  appreciated  that  the
work  for  them  to  do,  and  the  problem  for  them  to  solve,  was
to  organize  government  for  society,  so  as  to  preserve  the
largest  liberty  with  the  least  risk  of losing  it  by  usurpation.
In  the  infancy  of  States,  says  Montesquieu,  men  mould

institutions ; in  their  maturity,  institutions  mould  men.

So  the  men  of  America—hardy,  frugal,  self-reliant ;

conquering  the  savage  and  the  wilderness  by their  own  in-
dividual  will  and  strength—had  established  institutions  in
which  all  men  were  equal  in  rights;  for  privileges  could  not
equal  in  station  ;
exist  among  the  freeman  of  the  forest
for  courage  and  manhood  make  peers  of  all—and  equal  in
fortune  ; for each  had  equal  enjoyment  to  the  earth,  the  air

| 42  |

and  the  water.
These  institutions  recognized  all  men  as
free  and  equal,  and  with  equal  rights  to  life,  liberty  and  the
They  were  the  outgrowth  of  the  cir-
pursuit  of happiness.
cumstances ; the  product  of  the  time  and  place  in  which
men  found  themselves.
They  were  the  institutions  of  the
pioneer,  the  hunter  and  the  woodsman,  adopted  by  the
society  which  sprang  from  their  efforts  ; and  they had  develop-
ed a  race  of men  who  werestrong  enough  and  had  will  enough
to  unite  and  combine  this  vital  principle  with  the  institu-
tions  of their  ancestors,  and  by a  natural  evolution,  develop
the  plan  of government  under  which  we  live.

The  people  of  America  had  grown  up  under  separate
political  societies,  originating  under  different  nationalities,
impulses  and  objects.
They  had  at  last  been  arranged  with
similar  relations  to  the  government  of  Great  Britain,  and
had  long  been  increasing  in  power  and  strength  under  the
fostering  care  of  institutions  of  local  self-government,  long
rooted  in  England  from  the  time  of  the  Heptarchy.

There  cities,  boroughs  and  hundreds,  had  enjoyed,  by
common  right,  whereof  the  memory  of  men  recorded  no
beginning—or  by  royal  grant;  by  royal  grace;  or  won
by their  own  arms,  from  the  fears,  the  weakness  or  the  ne-
cessities  of  their  monarchs,  many  privileges  of  security  for
persun  and  property,  and  many  safe  guards  for  them.

Here  their  charters  gave  them  the  right  to  try  all  felo-
nies,  except  treason,  by  Courts  of  their  own  creation,  as  in
the  case  of  Yarmouth,  whose  charter  was  granted  by John.
There  they  exercised  the  power  of  arming  and  disciplining
their  own  citizens  for  the  maintenance  of  order  in  their
midst,  as  in  the  case  of  the  Trainbands  of  London.
This
one  enjoyed  the  privileges  of  free  commerce.
That  one  was
exempted  from  feudal  burdens  ; and  before  the  settlement  of
the  colonies,  the  practice  and  enjoyment  of municipal  rights
and  liberties  were  firmly  fixed  in England,  as  they  had  been
for  centuries  in  the  republican  cities  of  Italy  or  the  free

[ 43 |]

towns—the  growing  centres  of  free  thought  and  civilization
in  Western  Europe. |

These  rights  our  ancestors  brought  with  thém  to  these
shores,  and  they  were  firmly  planted  and  deeply  rooted
here.

Whether  under  the  Royal  grant  to  a  corporation,  as  in
the  case  of  Virginia;  or  to  an  individual,  as  the  grant  to
Baltimore  ; or  for  a  commercial  enterprise,  as  of  New  Eng-
land  or  Carolina;  each  community  soon  claimed  for  itself
the  right  to  govern  itself  in  many  matters  of vital  and  local
concern,  and  soon  they  asserted  larger  power  than  had  ever
been  claimed  by  any  corporation  under  the  common  law;
and  as  distinct,  separate  and  several  political  societies,  long
before  the  revolution,  had  assumed  all  the  rights  of  local
self-government,  making  their  own  laws  to  suit  their  own
wants,  and  bound  only  by the  acts  of  the  mother  country  in
those  matters  of  exterior  or  general  concern,  which  they  in-
dividually  were  incompetent  or  unwilling  to  undertake  for
themselves.
These  rights  of  local  self  government  they  ex-
ercised,  equally  jealous  of  interference  by each  other,  or  by
the  Crown  or  Parliament.
They  exercised  as  separate  polit-
ical  societies,  that  large  mass  of  govermental  function,
which  pertains  to the  protection  of society,  leaving  only those
parts  of such  functions  as  pertained  to  their  relations  to  each
other,  and  to  foreign  nations,  to  be  regulated  by the  govern-
Of  all  internal  affairs  each  one
ment  of  Great  Britain.
assumed  entire  control;
all  of
in  all  external  relations
them  were  subject  to  the  dominion  of  the  mother  country.

The
But  in time  these  relations  proved  unsatisfactory.
subordinate  political  societies  were  discontented  with  the
manner  in  which  the  paramount  government  performed  its
They,  therefore,  met
part  of  the  governmental  functions.
as  colonies  in  Congress,  and  as  colonies  and  Englishmen
consulted  and  acted  together,  for  defence  of  their  common
rights,  as  their  fathers  had  done  against  Charles  I  and
James  II.

[ 44  ]

They  claimed  and  insisted  that  “‘  their  ancestors,  at  the
time  of  their  emigration,  were  entitled  to  all  the  rights,
liberties  and  immunities  of  free  and  natural  born  subjects
within  the  realm  of  England.’’  —

Resolution  of  Congress,  October  14,  1774.

And  on  the  8th  of  July,  1775,  their  deputies  in  Congress
ail  signed  a  petition  to  the  King,  ‘‘  with  a  view  to  reconcile
differences,”’

But,  nevertheless,  the  people  of  North  Carolina  had
fought  the  King’s  governor  at  Alamance,  in*the  summer  of
1774,  and  the  freemen  of Massachusetts  had  driven  back  the
King’s  troops  on  the  19th  of  April,  1775;  and  this  very
Congress  had  appointed  a  commander-in-chief  of  its  armies
on  the  15th  day  of  June,  1775.

North  Carolina  declared  her  independence  of the  Crown
on  the  19th  of  May,  1776;  Virginia  on  the  29th  of  June,
1776;  and  Maryland  on  the  4th  of  July,  1776.

They  were  asserting  the  rights  of  Englishmen  to  resist

usurpation  by arms.

And  on  the  4th  of  July,  1776,  each  one  of  these
societies  became,  by  the  force  of  their  own  arms,  States—
separate  political  societies.
Hach  one  became  ‘‘a  State—a
moral  person,  which  is a  complete  body  of  free  natural  per-
sons,  uniting  together  for  their  common  benefit  ;  having  an
understanding  and  a  will  ; deliberating  and  resolving  ; pos-
sessed  of  interests  which  it  ought  to  manage  ;  enjoying
rights  which  it onght  to  maintain;  and  lying  under  obli-
gations  which  it ought  to  perform.’’—James  Wilson.

And  each  of  these  States,  or  moral  persons,  became  at
once,  in  itself,  entitled  to  all  powers  of  government  of every
kind  which  can  be  exercised  by  society.

They  were  founded  on  the  principles  set  forth  in  the
Declaration  of  Independence.
That  instrument  was  the
necessary  and  logical  result  of  the  principles  of  English

2 ee

| 45  |

liberty,  operating  on  the  times,  circumstances,  and  the
people  living  in  them.

The  Great  Charter  was  a  grant  of  enumerated  privi-

leges  from  a  superior  to  certain  inferiors.

The  Petition  of  Right  was  an  application  by inferiors

to  their  King,  to  acknowledge  certain  righ/s  claimed.

The  Declaration  of  Rights  was  an  assertion  of  rights,
insisted  on  as  appertaining  to  freemen,  and  the  Bill  of
tights  was  the  guarantee  of  those  rights  by  King,  Lords
Still  this  was  only  of
and  Commons,  by common  consent.
They
certain  defined  rights,  belonging  to  certain  classes.
had  struggled  on  from  a  grant  of a  few  franchises  to  a  small
class,  to  the  guarantee  of  many  rights  to  the  large  mass  of
society.

gut  the  Declaration  of  Independence  disclaimed  all
ileas  of  grants,  or  petitions,  or  guarantees,  of  special  privi-
leges  or  class  rights  ; threw  down  all  barriers;  swept  away
all  distinctions ; ignored  and  denied  all  theories  of  social
compacts  between  rulers  and  people;  and  asserted,  estab-
lished  and  proclaimed  the  fact,  as  it existed  then  and  there,
of  the  equal  and  inalienable  right  of  all  men  to  every  fran-
chise,  and  to  all  rights  for  the  whole  people  who  had  taken
arms  and  embarked  in  a  common  enterprise.

Thus  was  born  the  sovereignty  of  the  people.
And  these  States  were  thus  formed  on  these  principles.
The  people  of  each  State,  sovereign,  and  entitled  to  all  the
powers  which  society  may  exert  for  its  preservation,  and  men
may  use  for  the  promotion  of their  happiness;  and  each-man
in  each  State  equal  in  rights  to  every  other  man.

To  maintain  these  rights  and  principles,  the  articles  of
confederation  were  formed.
But  ‘‘it  was  the  strong  sympa-
thies,  not  the  feeble  government  which  bound  the  States
together  during  a  common  war.’’

This  governmert  proved  inefficient  to  protect  the  lib-
erties  inherited  and  achieved,  and  for  the  sole  and  only  pur-
pose  of  forever  perpetuating,  protecting  and  transmitting  to

ET oe

[ 46  |

us,  their  posterity,  and  to  ours,  in  turn,  all  the  rights,  priv-
ileges  and  immunities  of  free  and  natural  born  Englishmen,
together  with  the  equal  rights  won  by themselves  for  every
man  to  participate  in  political  power,  and  to  be  protected  in
life,  liberty  and  property,  our.  ancestors  framed  the  Consti-

tution  under  which  we  live.

All  govermental  power  resided  in  and  emanated  from
the  people  in  each  State.
If  the  immense  mass  of  power,
necessary  to  regulate  the  extensive  territory  of  America,
were  concentrated  in  one  centre,  it  was  evident  such  concen-
tration  would  inevitably  and  speedily  destroy  all  liberty.
For  power  to  govern  such  a  population,  as  this  territory
must  soon  contain,  would  have,  of  necessity,  to  be  so  strong
as  to  be  without  check.
On  the  other  hand,  if the  power
were  left  in  each  State,  by  itself,  to  govern  itself,  it  would
not  be  sufficient  to  protect  the  weak  from  the  strong  among
the  States  themselves,  nor  anv  of  them,  from  the  ambition
of  foreign  nations.
It  was  necessary,  therefore,  so  to  unite
this  power  that  it  would  be  strong  enough,  and  so  to  divide
it  that  it  would  be  not  too  strong—the  first  for  protection  ;
the  last  for  liberty.

The  functions  of  government  in  the  best  days  of  Rome
were  divided  between  the  comitia  centuriata  and  the  comitia
In  Athens  the  power  was  distributed  between  the
tributa.
Senate,  which  prepared  the  laws,  and  the  people,  who  rati-
fied  or  rejected  them.

|

In  England,  with  whose  recent  experience  all  were  fa-
miliar,  the  powers  of  society  had  only  recently  been  sepa-
rated  between  the  executive,  legislative  and  judicial  depart-
ments,  although,  without  clear  division.  And  the  struggles
for  liberty  in England,  more  than  any  anything  else,  colored
tne  deliberations  of  the  men  of  the  revolution,  and  directed
their  action.

That  all  men  were  entitled  to  equal  rights,  and  that  all
political  power  equally  resides  in  them,  was  their  belief,
and  to  preserve  and  perpetuate  these  rights,  this  power  must
be  organized  and  us-d.

|

|

[ 47  ]

The  object  then  being  the  protection  of  all—the  means
being  the  whole  power  of  society—they  devised  a  system
of  checks  and  balances,  for  the  accomplishment  of  this
object  by these  means,  without  parallel for simplicity,  wis-
dom  and  strength.

The  principle  announced  by Montesquieu,  that  the  sep-
aration  of  the  functions  of  government  between  the  execu-
tive,  the  legislative  and  the  judicial  departments.  is  neces-
sary  for  the  preservation  of liberty,  was  applied  and  extended
far  beyond  his  suggestion.

All  the  people  of  all  the  States  could  doubtless  have
formed  a  government,  and  vested  all  power  in  it;  or  they
could  have  found  two  governments,  dividing  the  powers
between  them ; or  they  could  have  organized  one  govern-
ment  to  exercise  certain  specified  powers,  and  other  compli-
mentary  governments  to exercise  all  the  remaining  functions.
But  they  did  none  of  these  things.

They  preserved  the  States  as  they  existed:

They  delc-
gated  certain  general  powers  to  be  exercised  for  the  protec-
tion  of  all  the  States,  and  all  the  people,  to  one  central
government,  and  distinctly  defined  the  extent  of  the  grant
to  it.

|

They  retained  State  governments  to  exercise  a  large
mass  of power,  but  by written  constitutions  prohibited  them
from  exercising  certain  other  powers  ; and  al]  the  residue  of
political  power  they  retained  in  themseives.
But  above  and
beyond  all,  they  retained  in  their  own  hands  the  right  and
power  to  change,  overthrow,  alter  and  amend  both  or  either
of  these  agencies  at  their  will  and  pleasure.
Being  the
creator  they  reserved  full  power  at  any  time  to  destroy ;
being  principal  they  retained  the  right  at  any  time  to  revoke
the  agency.

By this  scheme  of the  division,  separation  and  reserva-
tion  of powers,  it was  hoped  and  believed  that  the  most  per-
fect  system  of government  ever  known  had  been  devised  and
put  in  operation.

[ 48  ]

The  division  and  separation  were  so  perfect,  that  it was
believed  that.  the  checks  would  so  operate  on  each  other  as
to  render  any  future  appeal  to  force  against  wrong,  improb-
able,  if not  impossible.

The  Federal  government  was  made  a  check  on  the  State
governments,  and  was  authorized  to  prevent  their  exercising
If  the  Federal  government
powers  prohibited  to  them.
usurped  power  reserved  to  the  States  or  to  the  people,  the
States,  through  their  Senators,  were  intended  to  check  it.
But  beyond  this  separation  and  division  of  powers  between
Federal  and  State  gov  _rnments,  each  operating  to  keep  the
other  in  its  place,  they  further  divided  and  separated  the
functions  entrusted  to  each  government  between  these  sev-
eral  and  distinct  departments  in  each.  The  powers  entrusted
to  the  Federal  government  were  divided  between  the  execu-
tive,  legislative  and  judicial  departments,  with  clear  and
precise  provisions  to  prevent  either  department  infringing
upon  or  usurping  the  duties  imposed  upon  any  other.
And
the  same  provisions  were  made  as  to  the  State  governments.
Therefore,  such  powers  as  were  considered  necessary  and
proper  were  imparted  to  the  two  governments,  and  the  resi-
due  were  reserved  to  the  people  acting  through  their  States.
Ample  provision  was  made  that  neither  government  should
trench  upon  the  province  of  the  other,  and  to  render  collis-
ion  impossible,  it is declared  that  the  Federal  government
shall  be  the  ultimate  arbiter  in  case  of  dispute  between  the
two  governments,  as  to  their  proper  functions.

And  further  checks  were  provided  by the  division  and
separation  of  the  powers  of  each  between  their  distinct  de-
partments.

But  over  ‘and  above  all  these  checks,  they  devised  a
further,  and  stronger,  and  more  efficient  one  than  any  known
To  the  judiciary  department  of
to  the  history  of  England.
both  governments  was  entrusted.a  veto  upon  the  acts  of both
the  other  departments  of  both  governments,  with  the  right
of  ultimate  decision  in  the  Federal  judiciary.

[ 49  |

Under  this  power,  every  judge,  Federal  and  State,  can
pronounce  any  act  of  any  executive  or  Legislature,  Federal
or  State,  void,  because  contrary  to  the  constitutions  of  the
two  governments.

Under  this  system,  absorption  by the  one  government  of
the  functions  of  the  other  is  as  equally  forbidden  as  the
usurpation  by  one  department  of  the  powers  of  the  other ;
each  one  must  be  kept  separate,  distinct  and  apart,  neither
nor  colliding  the  one  with  the

nor  interfering

between  any  of  the  parts  throws  the  whole  out  of  order.

With  this  review  of  the  causes  and  objects  of  the  for-
mation  of  the  Union  and  the  Constitutions  of  America,  it  is
respectfully  submitted  that  no  question  can  arise  of  strict
construction,  or  liberal  construction,  concerning  the  chart-
ers  or  written  grants  of  power to these  governments,  co-
ordinate  and  together  making  one  whole.

The  intention  of the  people,  from  whom  they  proceeded,
was  to  allot  and  divide  out  certain  governmental  functions
to  two  governments,  and  to  three  different  departments  in
each  of  them.
‘To  make  those  allotments  and  divisions  so
clear  and  distinct,  that  no  confusion  could  possibly  arise,
whereby  one  government  or  one  department  could  absorb  or
usurp  any  of  the  functions  of  another  government,  or
another  department  ; their  object  being  to  protect  their  own
liberties  by making  each  government,  and  each  department,
such  a  check  and  balance  on  the  others,  that  in  no  event
could  one  preponderate  or  over-balance  any  other  one.  .

The  two  governments  were  intended  to  move  in  separate

orbits.

No  contact  was  ever  to  occur  between  them.

Two  spheres  in  the  same  atmosphere,  with  no  physical
connection,  but  deriving  all  their  living  powers  of  light,
heat  and  motion  from  the  same  source—the  sun  of  popular
Each  government  has  its  separate  and  distinct
rights.

But  WHERE  THE  POWER  IS  NATIONAL  IN  ITS  CHARACTER,  THE

SLATES  CANNOT  EXERCISE  IT,  ALTHOUGH  IT  BE  DOKMANT  AND  NOT

In  Holmes  vs.  Jennison,  14  Pet.  574,  the  Governor  of
Vermont  had  siezed  Holmes,  who  was  a  fugitive  from  the
justice  of  Canada,  and  held  him,  to  be  delivered  up  to  the
Canadian  authorities.
The  cause  coming  before  this  Court,
Chief  Justice  Marshal,  speaking  for  himself  and  Justices
Story,  McLean  and  Wayne,  held  that  the  right  of  inter-

course  and  agreement  with  foreign  States  was  delegated  to
the  United  States,  and  forbidden  to  the  States.

‘Jt  is  almitted  that  an  affirmative  grant  of  power  to
the  general  government  is  not  of  itself  a  prohibition  of  the
same  power  to  the  States,  and  that  there  are  subjects  over
which  the  Federal  and  State  governments  exercise  concur-
rent  jurisdiction.

*

of  the  Federal  government  is  necessarily  exclusive,  and  the
same  power  cannot  be  constitutionally  exercised  by  the
States,

Hence  it  appears—

First.

THAT  THIS  IS  A  GOVERNMENT  OF  C)-ORDINATE  GOVERNMETS,

EACH  WITH  CO-ORDINATE  DEPARTMENTS.

[ 56 |

Second.

THAT  ALL  POWERS  OF  GOVERNMENT  CONSIDERED  NECESSARY

BY  THE  PEOPLE  TO  BE  EXERCISED,  HAVE  BEEN  DIVIDED—  FIRST,  BE-

TWEEN  THE  TWO  GOVERNMENTS  ;  AND  SECOND,  BETWEEN  THE  THREE

DEPARTMENTS  OF  EACH.

Third.

THAT  THESE  POWERS  ENTRUSTED  TO  THE  TWO  GOVERNMENTS

ARE  SOME  OF  THEM  EXCLUSIVE,  AND  SOME  CONCURRENT:  AND  THAT

WHEN  ANY  OF  THE  CONCURRENT  POWERS  ARE  EXERCISED  BY  TEE

FEDERAL  GOVERNMENT  IT  BECOMES  AT  ONCE  EXCLUSIVE,  AND  BY  ITS

OWN  FORCE  EXPELS  AND  SUPERSEDES  THE  STATE  POWER  APPLIED  TO

THE  SAME  SUBJECT.

Fourth.

THAT  COLLISION  BETWEEN  THE  TWO  GOVERNMENTS  IS,  THERE-
FORE,  CONSTITUTIONALLY  IMPOSSIBLE,  AND  WHENEVER  SUCH  COLLI-
SION  IS  BROUGHT  ABOUT,  IT  IS  BECAUSE  ONE  OF  THE  GOVERNMENTS
IAS  TRANSCENDED  ITS  POWERS  OR  EXERCISED  THEM  IN  A  MANNER
NOT  WARRANTED.

Fifth.

THAT,  THIS  EXCLUSIVE  AND  THEREBY  PARAMOUNT  POWER,
WHEN  APPLIED  TO  A  SUBJECT,  MUST,  OF  NECESSITY,  CONTROL  AND
PROVIDE  FOR  THE  WHOLE  OF  THAT  SUBJECT—OTHERWISE  IT  WOULD
LEAVE  A  PART  OF  IT  UNPROVIDED  FCR,  WHICH  IT  HAS  NO  RIGHT  TO
DO,  AS  IT  THEREBY  PRODUCES  COLLISION.

With  these  propositions  established,  it  is  proposed  to
consider  the  scope  and  operation  of  the  first  clause  of  the
fourth  section  of  the  first  article  of  the  Constitution  of  the
United  States,  the  source  from  which  the  power  of  Congress
now  under  consideration  must  be  drawn,  if  it can  be  drawn
at  all  from  that  instrument.

/

[57]

It  is,  ‘‘  the  times,  places  and  manner  of  holding  elec-
tions  for  Senators  and  Representatives  shall  be  prescribed  in
ach  State  by  the  Legislature  thereof;  but  the  Congress
may  at  any  time,  by  law,  make  or  alter  such  regulations,
except  as  to  the  places  of  choosing  Senaiors.’’

The  history  of  that  clause  is  this —
As  soon  as  the  convention  waich  frainel  the  Constitu-
tion  of  1789  was  ready  for  business,  Governor  Randolph,  ef
Virginia,  brought  forward  his  plan,  since  known  as  the  Vir-
ginia  plan  for  a  government  for  the  Union,  in  the  form  of
fifteen  resolutions.
The  third  of  these  was  that  the  mem-
bers  of  the  first  branch  of  the  National  Legislature  ought  to
be  elected  by  the  people  of  the  several  States  every  year,  for

the  term  of

,  ee,

On  the  same  day  Mr.  Charles  Pinekney  laid  before  the
House  the  draft  of  a  Federal  government,  which  he  had  pre-
This  was  in  the  form  of  a  regular  Constitution,
pared.
divided  into  sixteen  articles,  and  was  the  precedent  for  the
form  and  manner  in  which  the  Constitution  was  subsequently
adopted.
Article  V  of  this  draft  is  the  germ  from  which
article  1,  section  4  of  the  Constitution  was  subsequently
developed
It  is:  ‘‘  Each  State  shall  prescribe  the  time
and  manner  of  holding  elections  by  the  people  for  the  House
of  Delegates;  and  the  House  of  Delegates  shall  be  the  judves
of  the  elections,  returns  and  qualifications  of  their  mem-
bers’?

(5  Elliott's  Deb.  128.)

After  a  long  debate  tae  House  came  to  certain  resolu-
tions,  indicating  their  determination  of  what  the  powers  of
the  new  government  should  be,  which  resolutions  were  re-
ferred  to  a  Committee  of  Detail,  to  arrange  the  Constitution
thus  agreed  upon  into  formal  articles  and  sections.

This  article  in  Mr.  Pinckney’s  draft  was  neither  con-
sidered  nor  debated  in  convention,  but  notwithstanding  this
it reappears  in  the  draft  of  a  Constitution,  reported  by the
Committee  of Detail  ; being  in  the  main  the  present  Constitu-
tion  ;  but  Mr.  Pinckney’s  article  was  greatly  enlarged  and
altered.

It  was  reported  thus:

| 98  |

‘Art.  VI,  sec.  1.

The  times,  and  places,  and  manner
of holding  the  elections  for  the  members  of each  House  shall
be  prescribed  by  the  Legislature  of  each  State ; but  their
provisions  concerning  them  may  at  any  time  be  altered  by
the  legislation  of  Congress.’’

This  is  the  first  intimation  of  any  power  in  Congress  to
intermeddle  in  any  manner  with  elections  of  members  of
Congress.

(5 Elliott,  377.)

On  the  9th  of  August,  1789,  this  sentence  and  clause
came  on  to  be  debated.
Mr.  Madison  and  Mr.  Gouverneur
Morris  moved  to  strike  out  ‘‘  each  house  ’’  and  insert  ‘‘  the
House  of  Representatives  ;’’  the  right  of  the  Legislature  to
regulate  the  times,  places  and  manner  of  electing  Senators,
being  involved  in  the  right  of  appointing  them,  and  the
motion  was  disagreed  to.

oy

The  first  part  having  been  agreed  to  nem  con,  Mr.
Pinckney  and  Mr.  Rutledge  moved  to  strike  out-the  remain-
ing  part,  ‘‘but  their  provisions  concerning  them  may  at  any
time  be  altered  by the  Legislature  of the  United  States.
The
States,  they  contended,  could  and  must  be  relied  on  in  such
cases.

o

Mr.  Gorham.

It  would  be  as  improper  to  take  this
power  from  the  National  Legislature  as
the
British  Parliament  from  regulating  the  circumstances  of
elections,  leaving  this  business  to  the  counties  themselves.

to  restrain

Mr.  Madison.

It  seems  as  improper  in_  principle,
though  it  might  be  less  inconvenient  in  practice,  to  give  the
State  Legislatures  this  great  authority  over  the  election  of
the  representatives  of  the  people  in  the  General  Legislature
as  it  would  be  to  give  the  Jatter  a  like  power  over  the  elec-
tion  of  their  representatives  in  the  State  Legislatures.
He
was  supported  by  King,  Morris  and  Sherman,  and  the
motion  was  lost.
The  word  ‘‘  respectively  ’’’  was  inserted
after  the  word  ‘*  State.  ’”’

The  clause  was  then  amended  by  the  words  ‘  their
provisions  concerning  them’’  being  stricken  out  and  ‘‘  reg-
ulations  in  such  cases’  >  inserted ; the  clause  then  reading:
‘*  But  regulations  in  each  of  the  foregoing  cases  may,
at  any  time,  be  made  or  altered  by  the  Legislatures  of  the
United  States.”’

[ 59  |

This  was  meant  to  give  the  National  Legislature  a
power  not  only  to  alter  the  provisions  of  the  States,  but  to
make  regulations,  in  case  the  States  should  fail  or  refuse
(Madison’s  Debates,  5 Gill,  401.)
altogether.

The  Constitution  reported  by  the  Committee  of  Detail
having  been  discussed,  altered  and  amended  in  divers  par-
ticulars,  was  then  referred  to  another  committee,  ‘‘  to  revise
the  style,  and  arrange  the  articles  which  had  been  agreed  to
bv the  House.’’

This  committee  reported  the  Constitution  in  almost  the

exact  language  in  which  it  is  now  set  forth.

The  entire  work  of  re-writing,  revising  and  polishing
the  work  of  the  convention  was  done  by  Gouverneur  Morris
with  his  own  hands.
The  clause  under  consideration  was
reported  in  the  exact  words  in  which  it  now  stands,  except
that  the  words  ‘‘except  as  to the  places  of choosing  Senators’ ’
was  added  by  the  convention  ‘‘  nem-con’’  to  the  end  of  it,
in  order  to  exempt  the  seats  of  government  of  the  States
from  the  power  of  Congress.
(Madison’s  Debates,  5  Elliott,
401-542.)

|

The  Constitution  thus  framed  was  then  submitted  to
the  Congress  of  the  Confederation,  and  by  it  forwarded  to
the  government  of  each  State,  to  be  considered  in  conven-
It  was  duly  ratified  by  nine  States,  and  went  into  op-
tion.
eration  by  their  secession  from  the  Confederation,  which,  in
their  preamble,  and  also  in  the  clause  of  ratification  of  its
Constitution,  were  declared  to  be  a  ‘‘  Confederation  and  per-
petual  Union.’’

But  the  power  thus  granted  to  Congress  over  the
election  of  Representatives  was  fiercely  assailed  in  every
convention  of  which  there  are  recorded  proceedings.
Prob-
ably  no  clause  in  the  Constitution  was  discussed  with  a  more
It
searching  analysis,  or  attacked  with  more  forcible  logic.
ras charged  on  the  one  hand  that  it gave  the  Congress  the
power  to  order  the  elections  of  members  of  Congress  for

it  ene

[ 60  |

Georgia  to  take  place  on  the  Mohawk  river,  and  would  en-
able  them  to  perpetuate  themselves  without  end.

It  was  defended  mainly  on  the  ground—

Ist.  That  it  might  be  used  to  correct  the  negligence  in

elections,  of  electors  themselves.

2d.  That  it  would  prevent  the  dissolution  of  the  gov-

ernment  by designing  and  refractory  States.

3d.  That  it  would  operate  as  a  check  in  favor  of  the
people  against  any  designs  of  the  Federal  Senate,  and  their
constituents,  the  State  Legislatures,  to  deprive  the  people  of
their  right  of  election.

J

]

4.  That  it  provides  a  remedy  tor  the  evil,  should  any
State,  by  invasion  or  other  cause,  not  have  it  in  its  power  to
appoint  a  place  where  the  citizens  thereof  may  meet  to
choose  their  Federal  Representatives.
(Debates  in  Massa-
chusetts  convention,  2 Elliott,  61,  62.)

3

Messrs.  Pierce  and  Bishop,  in  that  convention,  urged
that  ‘*  Congress  may  require  a  majority  of  all  the  votes  in  a
State  to  elect,  or  may  order  an  election  to  take  place  in
Boston.

‘<'lhe  House  of  Representatives  form  the  Democratic
part  of  the  Government;  the  Senate  represents  the  States,
Now,  the  Democratic  part  ought  not  to  be  interfered  with
by  the  States.
The  elections  by  the  people  of  their  Repre-
sentatives  ought  not  to  be  interfered  with  by  the  Senate,
unless  the  States  refuse  to  hold  elections.
Therefore,  section
4,  article  1 of the  Constitution  ought  to  be  amended  so  as  to
read,  ‘  but  if  any  State  refuse  or  neglect  so  to  do,’  Congress
(2 Elliott,  51.)
may  make  or  alter  such  regulations,  &c.’’
In  reply  to  this,  it was  argued  by  Strong,  ‘‘  that  the
power  to  regulate  Federal  elections  must  be  lodged  some-
where;  and  there  are  but  two  bodies  wherein  it  can  be
lodged:  the  Legislatures  of  the  several  States  and  the  gen-
eral  Congress.
The  State  Legislatures,  said  he,  are  the
best,  but  if  they  refuse  to  do  so,  the  General  Government
will  be  dissolved  unless  Congress  acts.’”’

i 61  |

“Mr.  Cabot  said:  The  House  represents  the  people ;

the  Senate  the  States.
If  theStates  are  allowed  unrestricted
power  in  regulating  elections  of  the  people’s  House,  the
States  may  make  the  House  their  representatives,  and  thus
destroy  the  balance  between  the  two.”

The  conduct  of  Rhode  Island  was  dwelt  upon,  in  with-
drawing  her  delegates  from  the  Confederacy  at  a  critical

period,

It  was  stated  that  measures  of  vital  hinportance  iad

been  defeated  in  Congress  by  the  withdrawal  of  ihe  Rhode
Island  delegates  and  their  factious  refusal  to  return.  although

o

.

the  Government  of  Rhode  Island  hal  been  requested,  by  a

resolution  of  Congress,  tO  send.  them  or  other  delesates  back.
(2 Elliott,  52.)

The  result  of  all  this  was  that  the  Constitution  was  rat-

ified  by  the  convention  of  Massachusetts,  with  the  distinet
understanding  that  certain  amendments  should  at  once  be
made  to  it.  Among  these  amendments  was  this:

‘TIT.  That  Congress  do  not  exercise  the  powers  vested
in  them  by  the  4th  section  of  the  Ist  article,  but  in  cases
when  a  State  shall  neglect  or  re'use  to  make  the  regulations
ot
therein  mentioned,  or  shall  make  regulations  subversive
the  rights  of  the  people  to  a  free  and  equal  representation  in
(2  Elliott,  180  )
Congress,  agreeably  to  the  Constitution.’”

So.  likewise,  New  Hampshire  ratified  it  with  this  dee-

‘And  as  it  is  the  opinion  of  this  convention  that  al  er-
ations  would  remove  apprehensions  and  fears,  and  more
effectually  guard  against  the  undue  administration  of  the

In  Maryland,  the  convention  refused  to  consider  any
amendments ; but  the  following  was  proposed  by  William
Paca,  and  supported  by Samneil  Chase,  afterwards  a  Justice
of  this  Court:

‘*The  Congress  shall  have  no  power  to  alter  or  change
the  time,  place  or  manner  of  holding  elections  tor  Senators
and  Representatives,  unless  a  State  shall  neglect  to  make
regulations,  or  to  execute  ifs  regulations,  or  shall  be  pre-
vented  by invasion  or  rebellion  ;  in  which  cases  only,  Con-
(2
gress  may  interfere
Elliott,  512.)

the  cause  be  removed.’’

until

In  the  Virginia  convention,  ‘‘  Mr.  Monroe  wished  to
know  why  Congress  had  an  ultimate  control  over  the  time,
place  and  manner  of  election  of  Representatives,  and  the
time  and  manner  of that  of Senators.”’

‘*Mr.  Madison  said  that  if Congress  could  fix  the  place
of electing  Senators,  it  might  compel  the  State  Legislatures
to  elect  them  ina  different  place  trom  that  of  their  usual
sessions,  Which  might  be inconvenient.
But  it  was  necessary
to  give  the  General  Government  a  control  over  the  time  and
manner  of  choosing  the  Senators,  to  prevent  its  own  disso-
lution.

‘*  As  tothe  other  point,  it  was  thought  that  the  regu-
lation  of time,  place  and  manner  of electing  the  Representa-
tives  should  be  uniform  throughout  the  Continent.

‘¢Some  States  might  regulate  elections  on  the  princi-
ples  of  equality,  and  others  might  regulate  them  otherwise.
This  diversity  would  be  obviously  unjust.
It  was
found  necessary  to  leave  the  regulation  of  these,  in  the  first
place,  to  the  State  Governments,  as  being  best  acquainted
with  the  situation  of the  people,  subject  to  the  control  of the
General  Government,  in  order  to  enable  it  to  produce  uni-
formity  and  to  prevent  its  own  dissolution.
But  if
they  were  regulated  properly  by the  State  Governments,  the

congressional  control  will  probably  never  be  exercised.
The
power  appears  to  me  satisfactory.  and  as  unlikely  to  be
(3  Elliott,  344.)
abused  as  any  part  of  the  Constitution.’’
This  assurance  appears  to  have  been  satisfactory,  but
the  convention,  nevertheless,  proposed  the  following  as  the
sixteenth  of  the  amendments.
‘*  That  Congress  shall  not
alter.  modify  or  interfere  in  the  times,  places  or  manner  of
hoiding  elections  tor  Senators  and  Representatives,  or  either
of  them,  except  where  the  Legislature  of  any  State  shall
neglect,  refuse,  or  be  disabled  by  invasion  or  rebellion,  to
prescribe  the  same,’  aud  then  declared  ‘‘that  the  conven-
tion  do,  in  the  name  and  belialf  of  the  people  of  this  Com-
monuwealth,  enjoin  it  upon  their  representatives  in  Congress
to  exert  all  their  influence,  and  use  all  reasonable  and  legal
methods  to  obtain  a  ratification  of  the  foregoing  alterations
and  provisions,  in  the  manner  provided  by  the  fifth  article
of  the  said  Constitution  ;  and  in  all  Congressional  Jaws  to
be  passed,  in  the  meantime,  to  conform  to  the  spirit  of  these
(3
amendments  as  taras  the  said  Constitution  will  admit.’’
Elliott,  596.)

‘ e  Island,
New  York,  Pennsylvania,  Maryland,  Virginia,  North  Caro-
lina,  and  South  Carolina  each  either  proposed  such  amend-
ments  to  the  Constitution  as  would  define  this  power  in  Con-
gress  to  be  only  aiternative,  after  the  State  had  neglected,
refused  or  been  disabled from  executing  it,  or  show  by the
record  of  their  debates,  that  such  was  the  agreement  or
understanding  of  its  friends  and  supporters.
They  are  the
only  conventions  whose  proceedings  were  reported  and  are

known.

Connecticut,  New  Jersey,  Delaware  and  Georgia  all
ratified  the  Constitution  without  amendment,  with  what
explanations  and  understanding  cannot  be  known,  ior  none
of  their  proceedings  were  preserved.

In  the  first  Congress  that  met  after  the  final  ratification
the  Constitution,  elected,  organized  and  acting  under  it,
immediate  steps  were  taken  to  propose  amendments,  so  gen-
erally  demanded  by  the  people  of  the  several  States

Various  amendments  were  proposed  and  considered  in
committee  of  the  whole,  and  seventeen  were  agreed  to  and
reported  to  the  House.

ipa

gi AR  tt Sh ca tg SN  LOG  RO

r 66]

On  August  20th,  1789,  the  House  agreed  to  this  report,
when  it was  moved  to  add  to  the  others  the  following  article  :

‘¢  Congress  shall  not  alter,  modify  or  interfere  with  the
times,  places  and  manners  of  holding  elections  of  Senators
and  Representatives,  except  when  any  State  shall  refuse,  or
neglect,  or  be  unable  by invasion  or  rebellion  to  make  such
election’’—which  was  lost,  ayes  23,  nays  28.

These  amendments  being  considered  by  the  Senate,  it
was  moved  on  September  7th,  1789,  that  this  amendment  be
added  to  them:

‘¢  That  Congress  shall  not  exercise  the  powers  vested  in
them  by the  4th  section  of  the  Ist  article  of  the  Constitu-
tion  but  in  cases  where  a  State  shall  neglect  or  refuse  to
make  regulations  therein  mentioned,  or  shall  make  regula-
tions  subversive  of  the  rights  of  the  people  to  a  free  and
equal  representation  in  Congress,  agreeably  to  the  Constitu-
tion  ’’—which  was  lost.

On  the  8th  September,  1759,  in  the  Senate,  it  was  moved

to  add  the  article:

‘That  Congress  shall  not  alter,  modify  or  interfere  in
the  times,  places  or  manuer  of holding  elections  for  Senators
and  Representatives,  or  either  of  them,  except  when  the
Legislature  of  any  State  shall  neglect,  or  refuse,  or  be  dis-
abled  by invasion  or  rebellion  to  prescribe  the  same—which  |
was  lost.’’

(History  of  Congress,  159-167.)

It  appears  from  these  amendments,  proposed  by and  re_
jected  in  State  conventions,  and  in  Congress,  from  the  objec-
tions  of  its  opponents  and  the  explanations  of  its  friends,
that  the  object  and  meaning  of this  clause  was  that  Congress
should  only  interfere  when  States  neglected  or  were  disabled
from  performing  their  duty.

It  is clear  that  Congress  was  intended  to  act  instead  of
the  States.
Federal  action  was  to  supersede  Sate  action.
Some  contended  that  it  should  only  intervene  when  State
action  was  wanting ; some  when  States  acted  improperly ;
But  every  one  agreed  that
some  when  States  were  disabled.
State  action  ought  to  be  superseded  in  certain  contingencies.
It  was,  as  Wilson  of  Pennsylvania  said,  to  prevent’  the
The  State
States  destroying  or  controlling  the  Congress.

| 67  |

Legislatures  have  the  absolute  and  sole  right  to  elect  Sena-
tors.
If  the  State  Legislatures  had  the  same: absolute  and
sole  right  to  provide  for  the  election  of  members  of  Con-
gress,  then  the  State  Legislatures  could  control  Congress.
It was  to  check  this  that  Congress  was  vested  with  the  power
to  supersede  State  action.
But  it  is  nowhere  intimated  that
State  provisions  and  Congressional  regulations  shall  remain
in  force  at  the  same  time.
It  is always  admitted  that  Con-
gress  can  supersede  State  provisions,  but  no  one  ever  inti-
mated  that  Congress  could  leave  State  regulations  in  full
force  and  attempt  to  control  State  officers  executing  them.
Jt  is clear,  from  this  contemporaneous  record  and  con-
struction,  that  the  spirit  and  intent  of  the  Constitution  1s,
that  the  power  cannot  be  exercised  by  Congress  unless
States  refuse,  neglect  or  are  disabled  from  executing  it.
This  was  the  construction  placed  on  it  in  debate  and  in
amendments,  proposed  by the  friends  of  the  Constitution  in
the  several  State  conventions.
They  insisted  that  amend-
ments  were  unnecessary,  and  agreed  to  propose  them  only
‘*  to  remove  the  fears  and  quiet  the  apprehensions  of  many
of  the  good  people  of  this  commonwealth  ’’—in  the  lan-
guage  of Massachusetts,  in  proposing  them.
And  when  tlie
Constitution  was  ratified  and  put  in  operation,  the  men  who
had  promoted,  framed,  supported  and  secured  the  ratifica-
tion  of  it, were,  in  the  main,  entrusted  with  the.  duty  of
putting  it  in  operation.

.

Ellsworth,  Jay,  Rutledge,  Samuel  Chase,  Wilson,  Ire-
dell,  Blair,  Johnson  and  Patterson  were  selected  to  exercise
the  judicial  power  of  the  new  government.

The  President  of  the  Federal  convention  was  made  the

President  of  the  Federal  Union.

The  Congress  was  under  the  entire  and  absolute  control
They
of  the  friends  and  advocates  of  the  changed  system.
had  all  insisted  that  the  amendments  were  unnecessary,  for,
by  the  assurances  of  Wilson,  Madison  and  Hamilton,  and
others  like  them,  the  people  had  been  satisfied  that  the

69

It  may  be  true  that  Congress  -s  the  sole  judge  as  ¢o
when  the  contingency  has  arisen  in  w  ich  it  is  proper  to  ex-
ercise  the  power,  but  the  exercise  thereof  is,  nevertheless,
subject  to  the  Constitution,  and  the  review  of  this  Court.

Congress  has  the  power  to  regulate  commerce,  but  it
cannot,  in  regulating  it, give  a  man  aright  to  sell  spirituous
liquors  in  a  State  which  forbids  suc)  sale  ;  to  establish  uni-
form  rules  of  naturalization  and  on  th:  subject  of  bankruptey;
but  it  certainly  could  not  made  a  law  requiring  aliens  to  be
naturalized  before  the  judges  of  the  Courts  of  last  resort  of
the  States,  and  impose  penalties  on  te  judges  for  fraudulent
execution  of  the  law.
Nor  could  it  mike  State  Sheriffs,  ex-
ecutors  of  a  bankrupt  system,  and  punish  them  tor  disobe-
dience  to  its  provisions

So,  likewise,  the  Congress  can  make  regulation  of  the
manner  of holding  elections;  but  if  it  were  to  require  that
the  manner  of holding  should  be  by  the  Governor  of  a  State,

assisted  by the  Judges  of  all  the  Courts,  who  should  proceed
from  place  to  place,  and  hold  the  elections  at  the  times  and
places  specified,  in  order  to  secure  a  fair  e-ection,  everybody
would  admit  that  such  a  regulation  would  be  void;  because,
as  this  Court  says  in  The  Collector  vs.  Day,  [1  Wallace,  127,
“any  Government  whose  means  employed  in  conducting  its
operations,  if  subject  to  the  control  of  another  and  distinct
Government,  can  exist  only  at  the  mercy  of that  Government.”

It  can  regulate  the  value  of  money,  but  it cannot  make
a  pound  of  silver  equal  to  a  pound  of  gold  in  the  payment  of
debts.

,

|

To  fix  the  standard  of weights  and  measures,  but  it would
be  an  unconstitutional  exercise  of  power  to  declare  by law
that  an  inch  should  be  considered  as  long  as  a  yard.

And  for  the  same  reason,  rules  made  for  the  government
and  regulation  of  the  land  and  naval  forces,  and  for  organi-
zing  the  militia,  which  swept  into  the  ranks  by conscription
all  the  officers  of the  State  Governments—Executive  and  Leg-
islative,  would  be  void.

| 70  |

And  if  the  Congress,  in  the  attempted  exercise  of  the
power “ to  regulate  the  manner  of holding  the  elections,”  were
to  enact  laws  to  regulate  “  the  manner  of electing,”  such  laws
would  be  void.

If it were  to  enact  that  at  the  election  for Representatives
it  should  require  a  unanimous  vote  to  elect,  or  eighty  per
cent.  of  the  vote;  or  that  the  person  receiving  the  smallest
number  of  votes  should  be  elected;  or  that  a  person  should
receive  an  actual  majority  of all  persons  qualified  to  vote,  and
ascertain  by law  that  the  number  of qualified  voters  amounted
to  twenty  per  cent.  of the  entire  population,  then,  in  each  one
of  these  cases,  such  regulation  would  be  void—first,  because
they  would  be  regulations  of  the  manner  of electing,  and  not
regulations  of  the  manner  of  holding  the  elections;  and
second,  because  such  regulations  would  be  contrary  and  re-
pugnant  to  the  whole  spirit  of  our  institutions,  which  require
an  election  to  be  decided  by  the  voters  actually  voting.
Therefore,  it is clear  that  while  the  Congress  is sole  judge  as
to  the  time  of  making  the  regulations  by law,  still  the  regula-
tions,  when  made,  must  be  consistent  with  the  Constitution.
The  discretion  is given  as  to  the  time  of exercising  the  power ;
the  manner  of  exercising  it  is controlled  by the  supreme  law
and  the  judicial  power,  and  would  be  adjudged  void  by com-
mon  consent  as  well  as  by the  decisions  of this  Court.

Therefore,  while  the  Congress  “  may  at  any  time  make
regulations,”  it  cannot  at  any  time  make  any  regulations.
The  regulations  it does  make  must  be  consistent  with  the  in-
tent  and  meaning  of  the  Constitution  in  all  its  parts  and  in
every  provision.

We  have  already  seen  that  this  is a  system  of  co-ordinate
Governments  with  co-ordinate  departments;  that  the  main
object  of their  organization  was  to  preserve  the  liberties  of the
peop!e;  and  that  the  means  devised  and  applied  through
them  was  a distinct  separation  and  division  of powers  between
the  two  Governments  and  between  their  different  depart-
ments;  and  that  any  action  by either  Government  or  either

[71

department,  which,  of  nec*ssity,  produced  collision  between
the  two  Governments  or  any  two  of  their  departments,  was
contrary  to  the  whole  scheme,  object  and  intent  of  the  gov-
ernmental  organization,  and  therefore  void.

IT.

We  will  now  consider  the  fourth  section  of  the  first

article  of  the  Constitution.

This  section  is mandatory  on  the  States.
“  The  times,  places  and  manner  of  holding  elections  for
Senators  and  Representatives  sad  be  prescribed  in  each  State
by the  Legislature  thereof.”

It says:

v;

They  “  shall  be  prescribed,’  because  the  reason  of  the
thing  and  the  example  of Khode  Island  had  proved  the  neces-
It
sity  of requiring  this  duty  to  be  performed  by the  States.
It  was  imposed  as  a  duty,  not  left  to
was  made  binding.
their  mere  will  and  pleasure.
It  does  not  confer  a  right;  it
commands  the  performance  of an  obligation.

As  in  the  second  section  of  article  first,  *‘  the  actual  enn-
meration  (of  population  by  Congress)  shall  be  made  within
three  vears  after  the  first  meeting  by  Congress,  and  within
every  subsequent  term  of ten  years.”

!

“When  vacancies  happen  in  the  representation  from  any
State,  the  Executive  thereof  shall  zssue  writs  of election  to  fill
such  vacancies.”

In  section  5:  *  Each  House  shall  keep  a  journal  of  its

proceedings.”

In  article  2,  section  1:  “  Each  State  shall  appoint,  in
such  manner  as  the  Legislature  thereof  may  direct,  a  number
of electors,  &e.”

In  article  4, section  1:  “Full  faith  and  credit  shall  be
given  in  each  State  to  the  public  acts,  records  and  judicial
proceedings  of  every  other  State.  Any  person  charged  with
treason,  felony  or  other  crime,  who  shall  flee,  &c.,  shall  be de-
livered  up.
Persons  held  to  service  or  labor  in  one  State,
escaping  to  another,  shall  be delivered  up.”

All  these  clauses  create  duties  and  impose  obligations;
most  of  them  make  no  provision  for  the  case  of  a  refusal  to
perform  them.

If  the  Congress  does  not  provide  for  making  the  enumer-
ation  every  ten  years;  if  the  Executive  of  a  State  does  not
issue  writs  to  fill  vacancies  in  the  representation  of  his  State;
if  each  House  does  not  keep  a  journal  of  its  proceedings;  if
each  State  does  not  appoint  electors  in  such  manner  as  the
Legislature  thereof  may  direct;  if  a  person  charged  with
treason  or  felony  zs not  delivered  up  by the  Executive  of  the
State  in  which  he  may  be  found,  no  provision  is made  in  the
Constitution  for  enforcing  the  obligation.

When  States  do  not  give  full  faith  and  credit  to  the  pub-
lic  acts  of  other  States,  the  Federal  judiciary  compels  obe-
dience  to  that  mandate;  and  Congress  has  supplied  the  means
by which  the  constitutionai  obligation  with  regard  to  fugitive
slaves  might  be  enforced.

3ut  as  to  the  duties  required  to  be  performed  by  the
States,  viz:  to  prescribe  the  times,  places  and  manner  of hold-
ing  elections;  for  the  Executive  of  a  State  to  issue  writs  of
election  to  fill  vacancies,  and  to  appoint  electors,  the  Consti-
tution  supplies  no  means  cf compelling  States  to  perform  it in
The  law  made  by the
ease  they  neglect  or  refuse  so  to  do.
Constitution  for  States  as  States  is  without  sanction.

But  as  to  the  duty  of prescribing  time,  place  and  manner
of  holding  elections,  full  discretion  is  given  also  to  the  Con-
gress  to  do  the  same  thing;  and  thus,  while  no  means  are
supplied  for  compelling  the  States  to  perform  the  obligation
imposed  on  them,  ample  provision  is  made  for  having  that

thing  done  by Congress  in  case  of  their  default.

The  thing  to  be  done  is to  prescribe  the  manner  of  hold-

ing the  elections.
To  prescribe

to  write  down;  to  arrange  by  rule;  to

direct  beforehand—the  manner  of  holding.

“The  manner  of  holding”  is  the  method  by which  and
the  instruments  which  are  provided  by  law  for  holding  the
elections.
It  is  the  act  of  receiving,  registering,  keeping,
counting,  ascertaining  and  declaring  the  result  of  the  votes

received,  registered,  kept,  counted,  ascertained  and  declared.
It is the  arrangement  of  circumstances  under  which  the  elec-
tions  are  held.
The  meaning  of  the  words  “  manner  of  hold-
ing the  election”  is defined  in  the  Constitution,  article  IT, sec-
tion  1, where  the  manner  of holding  the  election  for  President
and  Vice-President  is provided  for.

“The  manner  of  holding  the  election”  is a  thing  or  an
action.
Time,  place  and  manner  adhere  to,  appertain  to  and
co-exist  with  this  thing  or  action  ; for  time,  place  and  manner
are  the  necessary  accompaniments,  ingredients  and  parts  of
An  action  or  a  thing  must
every  thing  and  every  action.
have  time,  place  and  manner  surrounding,  identified  and  co-
existent  with  it.
It  cannot  be  without  time,  place  and  man-
ner  of  being;  it cannot  be  done  without  time,  place  and  man-
ner  of  doing;  and,  therefore,  time,  place  and  manner  must
pertain  to  and,  as  it  were,  form  a  part  of  every  “holding  of
every  election  ”

|

The  States  are to  prescribe  therefor—

1st.  The  time  cf holding  the  election.

2d.  The  place  of holding  the  election.

3d.  The  manner  of holding  the  election.

The  power  then  given  to  the  States,  and  duty  imposed
upon  them  by this  section,  is over  the  circumstances  of  time,
place  and  manner.

These  they  are  bound  to  prescribe.

Il.

The  second  clause  of the  section  confers  the  power  on  the
Congress.
It  imposes  no  duty,  nor  creates  an  obligation,  as
the  first  clause  does  on  the  States;  but  it creates  in  the  Con-
gress  the  right,  power  and  privilege  to  interfere  and  set  aside
the  time,  place  and  manner  prescribed  by  the  States,  and
make  new  regulations,  or  alter  the  old  ones,  concerning  the

same.

[ 74  |

The  language  of  the  clause  is:  “  But  the  Congress  may,
make  or  alter  such  regulations,

AT  ANY  TIME,  BY  LAW,
except  as  to  the  place  of  choosing  Senators.”

T

a

a

2

This  clause  gives  the  Congress  the  right—

Ist.  At  any  time  in  its  discretion.

2d.  By law,  by a  fixed  rule,  prescribed  beforehand,  made
by  the  Congress,  subject  alone  to  its  control;  to  be  altered,
amended  and  modified  solely  by the  Congress.

3d.  To  make  regulations  of the  times  of holding  elections.

4th.  To make  regulations  of the  places  of holding  elections.

Sth.  To  make  regulations  of  the  manner  of  holding

elections.

6th.  Or  to  alter  such  regulations  of  these  circumstances

as  have  been  prescribed  by the  States.

The  time  at  which  the  Congress  may  exercise  the  power
is
But
“any  time;”  it is in  the  discretion  of  the  Congress.
the  power  must  be  exercised  by Jaw,  by a  rule  of  action  pre-
Congress  could  not  delegate  the  power
scribed  betorehand.
to  the  President  to  make  regulations;  nor  to  the  Judges  of

nor  to  the  Governor  of  a  State;  nor  to  the  Legislatures  of the
It  must  be  exercised  by itself,  by its  own  law,  which
States.
law  must  be  subject  to  its  supervision  or  alteration  alone.

The  daw  by which  it is thus  executed  must,  of  necessity,
be  subject  to  the  inspection,  of  and  comparison  by this  Court,
with  the  supreme  law—the  Constitution  of  the  United  States.
And  the  Congress  may  thus  make  or  alter  such  regulations  of
the  time,  place  and  manner  “  of holding  the  elections.”

Now,  where  the  power  of  the  Congress  and  the  States
may,  at  the  same  time,  be  applied  to  the  same  subject,  the
power  of  the  Congress  being  paramount,  at  once  excludes  the
exercise  of  the  State  power.

The  rezulations,  therefore,  to  be  made  by  the  Congress

must  be-—

apse

Ist.  By their  own  law.

2d.  By excluding  the  State  power  from  all  operation  on

the  subject  regulated.

3d.  They  must  be  consistent  with  the  intent  and  object

of the  two  Governments,  and  not  produce  collision.

4th.  They  must  be  enforced,  by  the  power  of  the  Con-
gress,  by officers  of its  own  creation,  executing  laws  of its own

enactment.

IV.

The  sections  of  the  Revised  Statutes  under  consideration

do  not  comply  with  these  requisite  conditions,  because—

1.  The  regulations  enacted  adopt  the  regulations  already
provided  by the  States;  adopt  the  State  laws  and  the  State
officers  as  the  laws  and  officers  of  the  Congress,  and  seek  to
compel  them  to  execute  the  same  by  the  Federal  power;  and
are  not  such  regulations  as  Congress  may  make.
They  are
regulations  of  the  regulations  of  the  manner  of  holding  the
elections  prescribed  by the  States.
They  do  not  regulate  the
manner  of  holding  the  election,  but  they  regulate  the  State
regulations.
They  create  Supervisors  and  Special  Deputy
Marshals,  and  charge  them  with  the  duty  of  “  guarding  and
scrutinizing ” “the  manner  of  holding  the  elections”  pre-
scribed  by the  States.
They  denounce  punishments  against
State  officers  for  disobeying  State  laws;  and  further  than  that,
actually  create  a  new  crime  “of  doing  acts  not  authorized  by
the  laws  of any  State,  or  of  the  United  States,  with  the  intent
The  language  of  the  Statute  is—
to  influence  the  elections.”
“  Section  5515:  Every  officer  of an  election  at  which  any
Representative  in  Congcess  is  voted  for,  whether  such  officer
of  election  be  appointed  or  created  by, or  under  any  law  or
United  States,  or  by or  under  any  State,  ter-
authority  of  the
ritorial  or  municipal  law  or  authority,  who  neglects  or  refuses
to  perform  any  duty  in  regard  to  such  election,  required  of
him  by any  law  of  the  United  States,  or  of  any  State  or  Ter-
ritory  thereof;  or  who  violates  any  duty  so  imposed;  or  who

knowingly  does  any  acts  thereby  unauthorized,  with  intent  to

Therefore,  this  is not  a  regulation  of the  manner  of  hold-
ing the  elections,  prescribed  by a  law  of  the  Congress;  but  it
is an  attempt  to  enforce,  to  scrutinize,  to  guard  and  to  apply
the  regulations  of  the  manner  of  holding  the  election  pre-
It  is not  an  exeen-
scribed  by the  Legislatures  of  the  States.
tion  of the  Federal  power  as  granted.

2d.  The  regulations  made  by  Congress  in  the  Revised
Statutes,  are  not  the  ‘‘  regulations  to  be  made  by  law,’’
authorized  and  required  by the  Constitution.

The  regulations  of  the  manner  of  holding  the  elections.
required  by the  Constitution  must  be  made  by  law.
Now
law  is  a  rule  of  action,  operating  equally  at  all  times,  in
every  place,  on  all  persons,  under  the  same  conditions.

The  regulations  made  by Congress  profess  to  adopt  the
election  laws  of  the  States,  and  thereby  make  them  pro  hae
Federal  laws,  as  if  they  had  been  re-enacted  totidem  verbis
It  is  undoubtedly  competent  for  Congress  to  adopt,  by
Statute,  a  complete  Code  or  separate  enactments  of  laws.
They  have  done  so  in  the  act  regulating  practice  in  the  Cir-
cuit  Courts  of  the  United  States,  and  in  providing  for  the
enforcement  of  State  Pilot  and  Quarantine  Laws.
But  this
was  done  in  the  exercise  of  implied  powers  to  pass  all  laws
necessary  and  proper,  to  enforce  the  judicial  power  vested  tn
the  United  States,  and  necessary  and  proper  to  regulate
commerce.

It  is  clear  that  a  power  to  make  regulations  by law  can
not  be  exercised  by  adopting  the  systems  of  thirty-eight
States,  and  of  hundreds  and  thousands  of cities  and  counties
in  States  on  this  subject.

Now  the  regulations  made  by this  law,  of  the  manner
of holding  elections,  adopt  the  laws  of each  State,  city,  town
or  county.

The  same  act  may  be  punished  in  Virginia;  and  may  be

lawful  in  New  York.

things,  and  the  law  of  Massachusetts  may  permit  the  same
things ; and  the  citizen  of  New  York  may  be  imprisoned
under  this  Statute  and  be  committed  to  the  custody of a  cit-
izen  of  Massachusetts,  who  openly,  avowedly  and  meritori-
ously  does  the  same  thing.

This  cannot  be  such  law  as  is  required  by the  Constitu-
What  is  forbidden  in  one
tion  and  the  spirit  of  all  law.
place  must  be  forbidden  in  all.
What  is  criminal  in  one
must  be  criminal  in  all,  just  as  punishment  must  be  the
A  law  declaring  crimes  and  punishments
same  everywhere.
must  declare  the  same  things  crimes,  and  denounce  the

Otherwise  it  is  not  law.

And  if  the  Federal  Statute  be  construed  to  adopt  the
election  laws  of  every  State,  and  the  regulations  of  every
county,  city  and  town  in  every  State,  it  must  follow  that  it
has  adopted  them  as  they  existed  at  the  date  of  its  passage,
to  wit:  as  on  the  3lst  of  May,  1870.

The  whole  body  of  State,  Territorial  and  municipal
laws,  as  it existed  on  that  day,  thereby  became  the  laws  of
the  United  States.

Whenever  such  States,  Territories  and  municipalities
have  since  that  day  repealed,  altered  or  amended  their  elec-
tion  laws,  then  the  Federal  Statute,  by  adoption,  still  re-
mains  the  Federal  law  ;  but  the  new  State  or  municipal  law
is the  one  that  is  in  operation,  and  the  machinery  of  Super-
visors  and  Special  Deputy  Marshals  provided  by the  regula-
tions  made  by the’  Federal  laws,  do  not  apply  to  these  new
State  or  municipal  laws.

Hence  it  would  follow,  that  these  regulations  made  by
law  of the  Congress,  of the  manner  of  holding  the  elections,
are  at  the  mercy  of every  State  or  municipality.
They  need
only  repeal all these  old  election  laws  and  enact  new  ones,
when  the  Federal  law  will  have  no  machinery  to  enforce  it,
and  fails.

It  cannot  be  that  such  an  exercise  of  Federal  power
could  ever  have  been  contemplated  by the  Constitution—the
making  of  regulations  that  could  be  rendered  inoperative  at
the  will  of  each  State,  so  far  as  elections  held  therein  are
concerned,

When  the  Congress  has  adopted  State  laws  as  the  laws
regulating  practice,  which  it  intended  should  be  enforced,  it
has  provided  its  own  officers  to  enforce  it.
In  other  cases,
as  in  the  Pilot  and  Quarantine  law,  it  has  left  State  officers
to  execute  them,  uninterrupted  by  Congress.

3.  The  regulations  made  cannot  adopt  State  officers  and
make  them  Federal  officers,  and,  therefore,  are  without
machinery  to  enforce  them.

If  it be  considered  that  these  regulations  operate  as  an
adoption  of  State  election  laws,  they  cannot  operate  as  an
adoption  of  State  election  officers.

A.  The  State  officers  are  appointed  by the  State,  paid
She  can  abolish
by  the  State,  and  responsible  to  the  State.
their  officers,  expel  them  from  office,  and  appoint  new
officers  in  their  place.

It  is  impossible  that  Iederal  officers  can  exist  who  can
thus  be  created  or  destroyed  at  the  pleasure  of  another

government,

B,  The  Constitution,  article  2, section  2,  provides  that
the  President  shall,  with  the  advice  and  consent  of  the
Senate,  appoint  all  other  officers  of the  United  States,  whose
appointments  are  not  herein  otherwise  provided  for,  and
which  shall  be  established  by  law.
But  the  Congress  may,
by law,  vest  the  appointment  of such  inferior  officers  as  they
think  proper,  in the  President  alone,  in  the  Courts  of  law,  or
in  the  heads  of  departments.

State  election  officers  are  appointed  by  none  of  these
authorities,  and  cannot,  therefore,  be  Federal  officers.
It  is
clear,  therefore,  that  these  regulations  made  by  Congress

.

[ 79  |

may  at  any  time  be  paralyzed  by  the  States,  by  repealing
their  election  laws,  or  by  changing  their  election  officers,
which  would  then  be  neither  the  laws  nor  the  officers
adopted  by the  Congress.

While  the  sole  and  entire  object  of  vesting  the  power
in  Congress,  to  make  regulations  by  law  of  the  manner  of
holding,  &c.,  was  to  prevent  State  interference,  and  provide
for  the  execution  of  it,  secure  from  the  State  power—these
regulations,  which  are  entirely  subservent  to  the  States,  are

not  a  constitutional  execution  of  the  power.

4.  The  regulations  cannot  be  executed  without  collision
with  State  authorities,  and,  of  necessity,  require  an  inter-
mingling  of  Federal  and  State  functions,  and  is,  therefore,
unconstitutional.

They  punish  by  fine  and  imprisonment,  one  or  both,
every  offense  punished  by the  laws  of  the  States,  thus  put-
ting  each  man  twice  in  jeopardy.

They  require  State  officers  to  do  things  prohibited  by
State  laws;  as,  for  instance,  they  require  the  State  judges
to  allow  the  Supervisors  and  Special  Deputy  Marshals  to  be
present  in  the  polling  places,  and  when  the  votes  are
counted;  when  State  laws  make  special  provisions  for  proper
parties  to  be  present,  and  prohibit  the  presence  of all  others.

7,

We  will  re-state  these  propositions,  and  the  argument

to  establish  them.

First.

1.  The  regulations  made  must  be  by law  of Congress.

2.  Such  law  must  not  produce  collision  or  intermed-

dling  with  State  laws.

3.  Therefore,  it must  control  and  provide  for  the  whole

subject  ‘‘  of  the  manner  of  holding  the  elections.”’

[ 80 ]

Second.

1.  The  regulations  must  not  be  subject  to  be  altered,

amended  or  modified  by the  States.

2.  Therefore  they  must  adopt  the  State  laws  in  force,
and  State  officers  provided  for  by  those  laws  at  the  date
Congress  makes  the  regulations  by  law;  or  they  must  not
adopt  them.

3.  If they  adopt  them,  and  the  States  afterwards  repeal
or  alter  the  State  law,  the  old  law  adopted  and  made  part
of  the  law  of  Congress  remains  in  force;  but  it  does  not
apply  to  the  new  regulations  and  officers  presented  by the
States,  and  is  left  with  nothing  to  operate  with  or  upon.
Hence  it is subjected  to  the  will  and  pleasure  of  the  States,
and  is  not  the  regulation  authorized  to  be  made  by laws  of
Congress.

4.  If  they  do  adopt  the  State  laws  then  in  force,  and
thereafter  to  be  enacted  by the  States,  such  adoption  is  void
as  to  future  laws.

a.  Because  it  is  a delegation  of  the  legislative  power

committed  to  the  Congress.

‘¢  One  of  the  settled  maxims  of  Constitutional  law  is
that  the  power  to  make  laws  cannot  be  delegated  by that
department  to  any  other  body  or  authority.
When  the
Sovereign  power  of  the  State  has  located  that  authority
there,  it  must  remain,  and  by  the  Constitutional  agency
alone  must  the  laws  be  made.”’

Cooley  on  Const.  Lim.  117.

In  Martin  vs.  Hunter  it  is  held,  that  no  part  of  the
judicial  power  of  the  United  States  can  be  conferred  upon
State  officers—a  fortiorz  is it that  no  part  of  the  legislative
power  of  the  United  States  can  be  delegated  to  the  State
officers.

The  power  is  ‘‘  to  make  regulations  by law.”’

—y—

{ 81  |

It  is  not  like  the  power  to  regulate  commerce.
In  the  latter  case  Congress  may  provide  by  law  for
making  regulations.
It does  soin  authorizing  thé  Secretary
of  the  Treasury  to  make  regulations,  and  in  adopting  the
Pilot  laws  of  the  States  by  the  Act  of  1759,  which  enacts
that  pilots  shall  continue  to  be  regulated  in  conformity  with
the  existing  laws  of  the  States  respectively,  wherein  such
pilots  may  be,  or  with  such  laws  as  the  States  may  respec-
tively  enact  for  that  purpose.—section  4235.

|

Cooley  vs.  Board  of  Wardens,  12  How.  299.
Ex  parte  McNeil,  13  Wall.  236.
License  cases,  5 How.  580.

which  declares  that  the  quarantines  and  other  restraints  es-
tablished  by the  health  laws  of  any  State
shall  be

This  is construed  as  an  adoption  of  laws  established,  or

to  be  established,  by long  custom.

Passenger  cases,  T How.  406.

There  the  regulations  must  be  in  the  law  itself.

The  power  in  the  one  case  is  to  make  regulations  by
law.
In
the  other,  it  is  to  provide  by  law  for  making  regulations.
In  such  case,  the  law  sets  out  the  manner  in  which,  and  by
whom  they  shall  be  made.

b.  Because  it  subjects  the  regulations  made  by  law  of

Congress  to  be  controlled  by the  legislation  of  the State.

The  regulations  made  by  law  of  Congress  are  the
supreme  laws,  and  to  subject  them  to  the  control  of the  State
is contrary  to  reason.

‘That  there  is a  plain  repugnance  in  conferring  on  one
government  a  power  to  control  the  Constitutional  measures
of another,  which  other,  with  respect  to  those  very  measures,
is  declared  to  be  supreme  over  that  which  exerts  the  control,
is  a  proposition  not  to  be  denied.”’

McCulloch  vs.  Maryland,  4  Wheat.  431.

5.  If,  however,  the  Congressional  regulations  do  not
adopt  State  laws  in  esse  or  in  posse,  but  are  intended  to  en-
force  State  laws,  then  again  they  are  void,  because  they
Jeave  the  whole  matter  to  be  altered  by the  States  as  they

piease.

The  States  can  make  an  act  acrime  one  year  and  declare
it  lawful  the  next;  or  one  State  may  make  that  lawful,
which  another  punishes  as  criminal.

But  no  regulations  made  by law  of Congress  can  punish
in  one  State,  an  act  which  it considers  lawful,  and,  therefore,
protects  in  another  ;  or  punish  at  one  time,  an  act  which  it
protects  at  another,  in  the  same  place.

The  cases  under  examination  offer  a  perfect  illustration

of  these  propositions.

The  laws  of  the  United  States,  providing  for  the  ap-
pointment  of  Supervisors  of  Elections  and  Special  Deputy
Marshals,  defining  their  duties,  and  imposing  penalties  on
all  persons  for  interfering  with  them,  were  passed  May  3lst,
1870,  and  February  28th,  1871.

They  applied  to the  Registration  of voters  and  to  Officers
of Registration  and  of Elections,  provided  for,  by the  laws  of
Maryland,  then  in force.

_

But  the  Registration  laws  of  Maryland  were  repealed

by the  Act  of  1874,  chapter  490,  passed  April  10th,  1874.

‘‘Section  1.  That  an  act  passed  January  session,  1870,
relating  to  Registration  of Voters,  be and  the  same  is  hereby
repealed,  and  re-enacted  as  follows,  &c.”’

[83 |

And  the  Judges  of  Election  provided  for  the  city  of
Baltimore  by the  Maryland  law  in  force  1870  and  1871,  have
all  since  been  removed,  their  offices  abolished,  and  new  ones
created  and  appointed  under  a  new  law  by  a  different  ap-
pointing  power.  The  new  Judges  thus  created  have  different
qualifications  and  requisites.

Prior  to  1876  the  Judges  of  Election  in  the  city  of Bal-
timore  were  appointed  by the  Board  of Police,  a  body  elected
by the  Legislature.

In  1876,  chapter  223,  passed  April  3d,  1876,  (page
307),  an  act  was  passed  repealing  those  sections  of  the  Code
of  Public  Local  Laws  of  Maryland  which  thus  provided  for
the  appointment  of  these  Judges,  and  created  instead  a
Board  of  Supervisors  of  Elections  for  the  city  of  Baltimore,
to  be  appointed  by  the  Governor,  who  shall  appoint  the
Judges  of  Election  for  each  election  precinct  of  said  city,
‘Cone  of  whom  shall  be  of a  political  party  different  from  that
with  which  the  other  two  are  connected.”’

Therefore,  the  Officers  of  Registration  and  of  Election,
and  the  mode  and  manner  of  registration  operating  in  Mary-
land  in  November,  1878,  were  not  the  officers  and  the  modes
adopted  by  the  Acts  of  Congress.
They  were  different
They  were  not  the  modes  nor
modes  and  different  officers.
the  officers,  the  Supervisors  and  Special  Deputy  Marshals
were  directed  ‘‘  to  scrutinize  and  to  guard.’’
And  these
petitioners  not  being  the  Judges  of  Election  referred  to  in
the  Acts  of  1870  and  1871,  and  to  whom  those  laws  apply,
must  be  released,  because  the  Court  which  convicted  them
had  no  jurisdiction  of  the  subject-matter.

If, on  the  other  hand,  it is intended  that  the  regulutions
made  by law  of  Congress  shall  apply  to  this  new  method  of
regulation,  and  to  these  new  judges,  then  the  regulations
made  by Congress  have  been  changed  and  controlled  by the
State  of  Maryland.
And  the  spectacle,  in  fact,  will  be  pre-
sented,  foretold  by  the  General  Court  of  Virginia,  through

[ 84  ]

the  President,  Judge  White,  and  quoted  in  U.  S.  vs.
Lathrop,  17  Johnson,  23.

‘¢  When  tke  criminal  laws  of  each  of  the  States  are  en-
forced  by the Federal  Judiciary,  then,  say  the  Court,  a  system
of a strange  kind  of  mosaic  work  in  the  judiciary  of nations
will  arise.  Here  a  Cadi  sitting  in judgment  upon  an  Italian
denying  the  Pope’s  infallibility  ;  there  the  stern  fathers  of
the  Holy  Inquisition,  putting  a  poor  Turk  to  the  rack
because  he  denies  that  Mahomet  is the  Prophet  of God  ; the
Judges  of  republican  Virginia  pillorying  an  Englishman
for  libelling  royalty  ; and  the  Court  of King’s  Bench  inflict-
ing  the  same  punishment  upon  an  American  for  libelling
the  government  of  the  United  States  for  the  late  declaration
of  war.”’

So  the  strange  spectacle  will  be  presented  of  the  Chief
Justice  of  this  Court,  sentencing  a  person  in  Maryland,  for
recording  a  viva  voca  vote;  the  next  week,  inflicting  the
same  punishment  on  another  in  Virginia,  for  receiving  and
counting  a  ballot ; the  very  next  week,  sending  Judges  of
Election  to  prison  in  North  Carolina  for  opening  the  voter's
ballot  as  he  hands  it  to  them  ; and  the  week  after,  inflicting
the  same  punishment  on  Election  Judges  in  South  Carolina
for  failing  to  open  such  ballot.

And  the  anomaly  of  the  proceeding  will  be  greater,
when  two  years  after,  on  returning  to  his  circuit,  the  same
eminent  functionary  shall  find  every  thing  reversed  by the
action  of  the  States.
This  time  he  will  have  to  punish  the
judge  in  Maryland  for  acts  that  were  innocent  before,  and  to
acquit  that  one  in  Virginia  for  acts  which  he  had  punished

before.

It  is  respectfully  submitted,  that  an  Act  of  Congress,
from  which  such  extraordinary  and  astonishing  consequences
flow,  is not  the  ‘‘  regulation  made  by law  of  the  manner  of
holding  the  election  for  Representatives  in  Congress,’’  au-
thorized  to  be  made  by the  Constitution.

Tne  Act  oF  CONGRESS  IMPOSES  ON  THE  CrrecurIt  CouRTs
DUTIES  NOT  JUDICIAL,  AND  IS,  THEREFORE,  IN  THAT  RESPECT,  VOID.

Section  2011  of  the  Revised  Statutes  provides,  that

whenever  an  election,  or  registration  of  voters  for  an  election
at  which  a  Representative  in  Congress  is  to  be  voted  for,  is
about  to  take  place  in  any  county,  city  or  town,  upon  the
statement  in  writing  of  a  certain  number  of  citizens,  to

the  Judge  of  the  Circuit  Court  of  the  United  States,  for
the  circuit  wherein  such  county,  &c.,  1s  situated,  if  these

desire  to  have  such  election  both  guarded  and  scrutinized,
the  Judge  shail  open  the  Cirenit  Court  in  the  most  con-
venient  point  in  the  circuit.

Section

The  Court,  when  so  opened  by  the
Judge,  shall  proceed  to  appoint  and  commission  from  day  to

2012.

Section  2013.

day,  and  from  time  to  time,  and  under  the  hand  of  the
Judge,  and  under  the  seal  of  the  Court,  certain  officers,  who
shall  be  known  and  designated  as  Supervisors  of  Election.
The  Circuit  Court,  when  opened  by  the
Judge  as  required,  shall  be  open  from  day  to  day,  and  be
always  open  tor  the  transaction  of  business  under  this  title  ;
and  the  powers  and  jurisdiction  hereby  granted  and  con-
ferred  shall  be  exercised  as  well  in  vacation  as  in  term  time  ;
and  a  Judge  setting  in  chambers  shall  have  the  same
powers  and  jurisdiction—including  the  power  of  keeping
order  and  of  punishing  jor  any  contempt  of  his  authority—

as  when  sitting  in  Court.

Sections  2014  and  2015  authorize  the  Judge  of  the  Cir-
cuit  Court,  when  unable  to  perform  any  duties  herein  im-
posed,  to  designate  any  District  Judge  in  his  circuit,  to  per-
form  them  as  fully  as  he  could  have  done.

Section  2025  directs  the  Circuit  Courts  to  appoint,  from
among  the  Circuit  Court  Commissioners  of  each  Judicial
District  in  each  Judicial  Circuit,  one  who  shall  be  known
as  the  Chief  of  Supervisors  0°  Election  of  such  district.

These  Supervisors  thus  appointed  are  required  by  sec-
tion  2016  to  attend  the  registrations  of  voters;  to  challenge
any  person  offering  to  register  ; to  scrutinize  such  registry,
and  to  affix  their  names  to  each  page  thereof  in  such  man-
ner  as  will,  in  their  judgment,  detect  and  expose  fraud.

for  members  of  Congress,  and  to  challenge  any  voter  whose
legal  qualifications  they  doubt,  and  to  remain  until  every

vote  is  counted

ness  of  the  election.

GL.  The  functions  directed  to  be  performed  by the  Cir-
