ecutive  and  judicial  departments  of  government  1s  produced.
The  judicial  power  of  the  United  States  extends  only

to  ‘‘  cases  and  to  controversies,  ’’

‘*  A  case  in  law  or  equity  consists  of  the  right  of  one
party  as  well  as  the  other,  and  it  may  be  truly  said  to  arise
under  the  Constitution  or  a  law  of  the  United  States,  when-
ever
the  construction  of
either.”’

decision  depends  on

its  correct

Cohen  vs.  Virginia,  6  Wheat.  377.

‘‘  A  controversy,’  in  the  sense  of  the  Constitution,  em-
braces  a  wider  field  than  ‘‘  cases,’’  for  it  includes  discus-
sions,  which  before  the  Constitution  were  political  only,  but
by  the  Constitution  were  subjected  to  the  judicial  power—
as  controversies  between  States  as  to  boundary  ;

thode  Island  vs.  Massachusetts,  12  Peters,  657;
or  controversies  as  to  acts  of  States,  affecting  the  rights  of
citizens  of  neighboring  States.

South  Carolina  vs.  Georgia,  93  U.  8.  14.

‘¢<

A  case’  or  ‘controversy’  involves  the  idea  of  a
party  prosecuting  in  a  Court,  to  establish  or  maintain  some
right  or  enforce  some  duty  against  another  party.”’

Courts  or  judges,  except  judicial  powers,  functions  or  duties,
else  the  separation  between  the  departments  of  government

Congress  passed  a  law,  March  23,  1792,  requiring  the
Circuit  Courts  to  examine  into  the  claims  of parties,  claiming
Revolutionary  pensions.
The  Judges  of  the  Circuit  Courts
all  declined  to  perform  those  duties,  because  they  were  not
judicial.  They  were  James  Wilson,  of Pennsylvania;  John
Blair,  of Virginia  ;  James  Iredell,  of North  Carolina;  John
Jay,  Chief  Justice,  of  New  York,  and  William  Cushing,
of  Massachusetts—all  of  whom  had  been  members  of  the
Convention  which  framed  the  Constitution,  and  all  of whom
were  Justices  of  this  Court.
They  comprised  the  whole
Court,  for  Mr.  Justice  Johnson,  of  Maryland,  was  not  qual-
ified  in  this  Court  until  August  Term,  1792,  although  his
commission  was  dated  November  7,  1791

They  unanimously  ceclined  to  perform  the  duty  imposed

upon  them.

preme  Court  for  a  mandamus,  to  be  directed  to  the  Circuit
Court  ot the  United  States,  for  the  District  of Pennsylvania,

burn,  who  had  applied  to  be  put  on  the  pension  lists of the
The  Court  held  the  matter  sub  curia,  and

| 89  |

the  Act  of  Congress  was  repealed  ;  but  to  set  the  matter  at
rest,  a  case  was  made  up  in  the  Supreme  Court,  to  recover
back  money  paid  to  a  pensioner  under  the  certificate  of  the
Judges  of  the  New  York  Circuit,  who  had,  at  first,  acted
under  the  law,  in  a  few  cases.

A  suit  was  brought  by  the  United  States,  to  recover
back  the  sum  of  $179.06,  paid  a  person  on  the  certificate.
To  the  Court  had  then  been  added  William  Patterson,
of New  Jersey,  the  author  of the  Jersey  plan  in  the  Conven-
tion,  and  the  Court  unanimously  decided  that  the  power  pro-
posed  to  be  conferred  by the  Act  of  Congress  was  not  judi-
cial  power  in  the  meaning  of  the  Constitution,  and  was,
therefore,  unconstitutional,  and  could  not  be  lawfully  exer-
cised  by the  Courts.

See  note  to  Ferreira’s  case,  18  Howard,  53,  pre-
pared  and  reported  by  order  of  Chief  Jus-
tice  Taney.

The  Court  in  that  case  held  “  that  an  Act  of  Congress  of
1849,  authorizing  the  District  Judge  for  the  District  of  Flor-
ida,  to  hear  and  adjudicate  on  claims  for  injuries  suffered  by
the  inhabitants  of  Florida,  by  the  American  army  in  1512;
which  claims  thus  certified  were  to  be  paid,  if  the  Secretary  of
War  should  deem  them  just,  was  a  void  act  because  the
duties  to  be  performed  are  entirely  alien  to  the  legitimate
functions  of  a  judge  in  a  Court  of justice.”

See  also,  Murray  vs.  Hoboken  Land  Co.,  15  How-

ard,  27.

It  seems  clear,  therefore,  that  the  creation  of  Supervisors
of  Elections  is unauthorized  and  void,  and  they  have  no  legal
existence  or  power.

The  only  duty  left  the  Marshals  is  to  keep  the  peace  and

This  Court  has  so  often,  so  lately,  and  so  clearly  declared,
that  the  duty  of  preserving  order  in  society,  of  protecting

life,  liberty  and  property  in  society,  of  regulating  and  taking
care  of  society  in  all  its  domestic  affairs,  was  reserved  to,

assumed  by and  still  inheres  in  the  States,  that  no  authority
will  be  cited  for  the  proposition.

kKivery  Chief  Justice  of  this  Court  has  laid

it  down  in
succession,  but  by none  with  more  force  than  by the  present

Chiet  Justice,  who  declares  in  Cruikshank’s  ease  that  “  Sover-

evgnty  for  this  purpose  rests  alone  with  the  States.”’

And  it  cannot  be  that  when  the  right,  duty  and  obliga-
tion  of  preserving  peace  and  order  rests  alone  with  the  States,
another  authority  can  intervene,  expel  the  State  authority,
arrest  the  State  officers,  and  assume  to  the  very  thing,  which

VIII.

THe  ACT  OF  CONGRESS  PROPOSES  TO  OPERATE  ON  OFFICERS  OR

PERSONS,  AUTHORIZED  AND  REQUIRED  BY  STATE  LAWS,  To  PERFORM

CERTAIN  DUTIES  UNDER  THEM.

AND  TO  REQUIRE  THEM  TO  DISOBEY

AND  DISREGARD  STATE  LAWS,  WHEN  THEY  COME  IN  CONFLICT  WIT!
IT  THEREBY  OF  NECESSITY  PRODUCES  COL-
THE  Act  oF  ConerREss.

sv sections  5515  and  5522  of  the  Act,  every  officer  of an
election,  and  every  person  acting  under  any  authority  of  any
State  or  municipality,  who  obstructs,  hinders  or  prevents  the

Supervisors  and  Marshals  in  the  discharge  of  their  duties,  is
liable  to  punishment.

This  provision,  of  necessity,  brings  on  conflict  with  State

laws.

The  case  at  bar  is  the  case  of  Judges  of  election.

In  another  case  a  Governor  of a  State  was  indicted  in  the
Circuit  Court  of  the  United  States  for  not  properly  perform-
U.S.  vs.  Clayton,2  Dil-
ing  his  duty,  under  the  State  laws.

lon,  219.

Every  officer  or  person  who  commits  an  offence  against
the  State  law,  may  be  punished  by  the  State;  and  under  the
Act  of  Congress,  may  be  punished  a  second  time  for  the  same
offence.
It  is  true  the  same  facts  may  constitute  an  oflence
against  both  of  the  governments,  and  may  be  punished  by
They  may  thus  constitute  fico  offences  against
each  of  them.

two  governments.

Sut  this  Act  of  Congress  makes  one  offence,  to  wit:  the
offence  against  the  State  law,  punishable  in  the  Federal  Courts,
when  it  is  at  the  same  time  punishable  in  State  Courts.

Such  collision  must  necessarily  and  unavoidably  oceur,  so

long  as  an  attempt  is  made  to  enforce  State  regulations  of  the
manner  of  holding  elections,  with  State  machinery,  by  the
The  proper  ex-
added  sanction  of  Congressional  enactment.
ercise  of  the  power  requires  such  regulations  to  be  made  by
laws  as  will  supersede  the  State  laws  and  officers,  and  provide
all  the  instrumentalities  by  which  the  regulations  of  Con-
gress  shall  be  enforced.

IX.

We  therefore,  claim  that  we  have  established  these  prop-

ositions.

First.

That  this  is  a  system  of  co-ordinate  governments,  each

edministered  by  co-ordinate  departments.

7

|

ie

Second.

That  the  division  and  separation  of powers,  between  these
governments,  and  departments,  must  be  preserved  in  all  their
acts.

Third.

That  confusion,  and  intermingling,  of  the  functions  of

rovernments,  or  departments,  are  alike  prohibited.
~

.

’

Fourih.

That  the  power  conferred  on  the  Congress,  by the  fourth
section  of  the  first  article  of  the  Constitution,  is exclusive  of
the  power  of  the  States,  over  the  manner  of  holding  the  elec-
tions  for  Representatives,  and  must  be  so  exercised  as  not  to
produce  confusion  and  intermingling  between  the  functions  of
the  co-ordinate  governments  or  co-ordinate  departments.

Fifth.

That  as  exercised  by the  Congress  in  the  Statute  under  re-
view,  it does  not  exclude  the  application  of  the  power  of  the
States,  at  the  same  time,  to  the  subject  of the  manner  of hold-
ing the  elections,  and  is therefore  void.

Sa

Sizth.

That  as  exercised  by the  Congress,  it is subject  to  be  con-
trolled,  altered,  and  modified,  by the  £ tate,  and  is  therefore
void.

| 93  |

Seventh.

That  as  exercised  by the  Congress,  it produces  an  inter-
mingling  of  the  functions  of  the  judicial  department  of  the
Federal  Government,  with  those  of  its  executive  department,
and  in  that  respect  is void.

Eighth.

That  as  exercised  by the  Congress,  it attempts  to  regulate
the  manner  of  voting  at  the  election  for  Representatives,  in-
stead  of  regulating  by  Jaw  the  mannre  of  holding  elections,
and  is  therefore  void.

Ninth.

That  as  exercised  by the  Congress,  it  attempts  to  provide
by law, for  the  making  of regulations  of the  manner  of holding
the  election,  instead  of  making  those  regulations  by  law,  and
instead  of making  the  regulation  by its  own  law,  delegates  the
power  to  make  them  to  the  States,  and  is therefore  void.

t

Judges  of Election  at  a  certain  Congressional  Election  in  the  City
of Baltimore,  and  State  of Maryland,  who  were  convicted  in  the
Circuit  Court  of the  United  States for the  District  of Maryland,
under  sections  5514,  5515  and  5522  of the  Revised  Statutes  of
the  United  States,  and  who  have  made  application  for  the  Writ
of Habeas  Corpus.

BRIEF  FILED  IN  THIS  CASE,  ON  THE  PART  OF  THE  PETITIONERS,
BY  DIRECTION  OF  THE  GOVERNOR  OF  MARYLAND.

It  is  respectfully  submitted  that  the  Petitioners,  in  these  cases,
now  in  custody,  under  color  of the  authority  of  the  United  States,
are  entitled  to  be  discharged  therefrom,  because  sections  5514,
5515  and  5522,  of  the  Revised  Statutes  of  the  United  States,
under  which  their  convictions  were  obtained,  are  void,  being  con-
trary  to  the  Constitution  of the United  States.

2

The  Constitution  of  the  United  States  provided  that  all  the
legislative  powers,  granted  by that  instrument,  should  be  vested
in  a  Congress  of  the  United  States,  which  should  consist  of  a
Senate  and  House  of Representatives.

Art.  1, sec.  1.

It  directed  that  the  House  of  Representatives  should  be  com-
posed  of members  apportioned  among  the  several  States  according
to  a  prescribed  standard,  who  should  be  chosen  every  second  year
by the  people  of  such  respective  several  States;  that  such  mem-
ters  should  be  of  a  certain  age,  and  should  have  been,  for  a  pre-
scribed  period,  citizens  of the  United  States  and  inhabitants  of the
State  in  which  they  should  be  chosen;  and  that  the  electors  in
each  State  of  the  members  to  be  thus  chosen,  should  have  “the
qualifications  requisite  for  electors  of the  most  numerous  branch  of
the  State  Leyislature.”

Const.  U.S.,  Art.  1, sec.  2.

The  people  of  each  State.had  the  exclusive  power  to  prescribe
by their  constitution  and  laws  the  qualifications  which  the  citizens
of  such  State  should  possess  to  be  entitled  to  be  electors  of  the
most  numerous  branch  of  its  State  Legislature.
They  had,  there-
fore,  the  exclusive  power  to  determine,  by means  of  the  constitu-
tion  and  laws  of their  State,  what  persons  in  such  State  should  be
electors  for  members  of the  House  of  Representatives.

iG

=
7

,~

As  the  people  of  each  State  had  the  exclusive  power  to  choose
the  members  of the  House  of Representatives  apportioned  to  such
State,  they  had  the  exclusive  right  to  provide  by  law  for  the
appointment  of those,  who  should  collect,  examine,  and  announce
the  votes,  which  determined  such  choice.

a

They  had  the  right  to  prescribe  the  times  and  places  at  which
This  right  would  have  been  exclu-

such  choice  should  be  made.

3

sive,  except  for  the  reservation  to  Congress  of  the  right  to  make
its  own  regulations  as  to  these  particulars,  or  to  alter  those  of  the
States.

Const.  U.S.,  Art.  1, sec.  4.

They  had  the  right  to  preseribe  the  regulations  which  those
who  were  appointed  under  the  laws  of  the  respective  States  to
collect,  examine,  and  announce  the  votes  for  members  of  the
House  of Representatives,  should  observe  in  collecting,  examining
and  announcing  such  votes;  for  such  regulations  would  govern
the  manner  of  holding  such  elections.
This  was  a  power  wholly
distinct  from  the  power  to  appoint  the  officers  who  should  collect,
examine  and  announce  the  votes.
The  power  to  appoint  such
officers  not  having  been  given  by the  Constitution  to  the  United
States,  nor  prohibited  by it  to  the  States,  must  be‘considered  to
have  been  wholly  reserved  to  the  States  respectively,  or  to  the

people.

The  regulations  of  the  manner  of  holding  elections  for  Repre-
sentatives  in  Congress,  which  the  Congress  of  the  United  States
was  authorized  to  make,  were  not  intended  to  be  punitive  regula-
The  power,  which  was  intended  to  be  conferred,  was  a
tions.
right  to  establish  general  regulations  as  to  the  manner  of  holding
elections.
The  exercise  of  this  power  by  Congress,  would  have
compelled  the  States  to  enforce  such  regulations  by appropriate
For  if  the  regulations,  made  by Congress  as  to  such
legislation.
particulars,  had  not  been  enforced  in  any  State  by  appropriate
legislation,  the  elections  held  in  such  States  for representatives  in
Congress  would  have  been  invalid;  and  the  House  of  Represen-
tatives,  by  its  power  of judging  the  election,  returns  and  qualifi-
cations  of  its  own  members,  could  have  excluded  the  representa-
tives  from  the  State  which  had  neglected  its  constitutional  duty,
and  have  thus  compelled  the  observance  of such  duty.

4

As  the  people  of  the  respective  States  had  the  exclusive  power
of determining,  by their  organic  and  general  laws,  the  persons  who
were  entitled  to  be  electors  for  members  of  the  House  of  Repre-
sentatives,  the  power  of  punishing  evasions,  or  violations  of,  or
frauds  committed  against,  such  organic  and  general  laws  by indi-
vidual  offenders,  necessarily  belonged  exclusively  to  the  respective
States.

It  is,  with  great  deference,  submitted  that  the  theory  of  the
complete  power  of  Congress  over  the  elections  of  members  of  the
Iiouse  of  Representatives,  urged  in  an  argument  entitled  to  the
highest  respect,  (federalist,  No.  59,)  does  not  rest  upon  conclusive
reasoning.
Chief  Justice  Marshall,  in  McCulloch  vs.  Maryland,  4
Wheat.,  433,  said  that  in  applying  the  opinions  of  the  authors  of
that  work  to  the  cases  which  may  arise  in  the  progress  of  our
government,  “a  right  to  judge  of  their  correctness  must  -be  re-
tained.”
It  is true  that  the  failure  of  the  people  of the  States  for
two  years  to  elect  a  majority  of  the  members  of  Congress  would
deprive  the  country  for  a  time  of  the  services  of  its  House  of
Representatives,  and  so  absolutely  stop  legislation.
It  is  equally
true  that  the  failure  of the  Legislatures  of a  majority  of  the  States
to  perform  for  four  years  their  duty  of  choosing  Senators,  would
deprive  the  country  for  a  time  of  the  services  of  its  Senate,  and  so
absolutely  stop  legislation.
It  is  not  pretended  that  Congress
would  have,  in  the  contingency  last  referred  to,  the  power  under
the  Constitution  to  appoint  officers  to  canvass  the  votes  of  such
If the  fourth
Legislatures  and  to  declare  their  choice  of Senators.
section  of the  first  Article  of the  Constitution  does  not,  as  is  in  effect
admitted  in  the  Federalist,  enable  Congress  to  provide  for  the  elec-
tion  of Senators,  in case  of a failure  of the  Legislatures  of any  of the

States  to  elect  them,  it is difficult  to  understand  how  it can  suffice
to  support  a  claim  to  provide  for  the  election  of  members  of  the
House  of Representatives,  in case  of  some  default  in  the  people  of

the  State  to  elect  them.
powers  of congress  would  seem  to  be  most  reasonable.

The  more  limited  construction  of  the

5

If this  argument  is  well  founded,  sections  2017,  2018,  2019  and
2022,  and  sections,  5511,  5514,  5515  and  5522,  which  depend  for
their validity  upon  the  power  of Congress  to  share  with  the  several
States  the  power,  or  to  exercise  the  ultimate  power,  of  conducting
the  elections  for  members  of  the  House  of  Representatives,  and  to
punish  offences  against  the  election  laws  of  the  several  States,  are
all  plainly  in  excess  of  the  constitutional  powers  of  Congress.
They  are  encroachments  upon  the  exclusive  power  of  the  several
States  to  provide,  by law,  for  the  appointment  of those  who  should
collect,  examine  and  announce  the  votes  at  an  election  for  mem-
‘They  are  encroachments
bers  of  the  House  of  Representatives.
upon  the  exclusive  rights  of the  respective  States  to  punish  offences
against  their  own  several  organic  and  general  laws  ascertaining
those  who  are  entitled  to  vote  fur  members  of the  House  of Repre-
sentatives.

Nor  are  these  sections  free  from  other  difficulties.

They  depend
for  their  validity  on  the  power  of  Congress  to  devolve  upon  the
Judges  of  the  Circuit  Courts  of  the  United  States  the  duty  of
appointing  Supervisors  of  Elections.

Revised  Stat.  U.S.,  secs.  2011,  2012.

The  clanse  of  section  2  of  Art.  2,  of  the  Constitution  of  the
United  States,  which  authorizes  Congress  to  vest,  by law, the  ap-
pointment  of such  inferior  officers  as  they  may  think  proper,  in  the
President  alone,  in  the  Courts  of Law,  or  in  the  Heads  of  Depart-
ments,  must  be  read  and  construed  distributively ; it  being  the
manifest  purpose  of  the  clause  to  authorize  Congress  to  provide
that  minor  executive  and judicial  officers,  whose  duties  were  within
the  scupe  of  the  general  duties  assigned  to  the  President,  the
Courts  of  Law,  or  Heads  of  Departments,  respectively,  might  be
appointed  by  the  President,  and  by  such  Courts  of  Law  and
Heads  of  Departments,  respectively.

6

Congress  could  not,  constitutionally,  devolve  upon  the  Judges
of the  Courts  of the  United  States  any  power,  or  duty,  which  did
not  properly  form  part  of  the judicial  power  of  the  United  States.

Const.,  Art.  3, sec.  1.

Supervisors  of  election  eannot  aid  the  Courts  of  the  United
States  in  the  execution  of  any  one  of  the  defined  judicial  powers
committed  to  their  charge;  Art.  3,  sec.  2;  and,  therefore,  Con-
gress  could  not,  constitutionally,  authorize  such  Courts  to  appoint
Supervisors  of election.

The  duties  which  are  devolved  upon  such  officers  by sections
2017,  2018,  2019  and  2022,  of the  Revised  Statutes,  are  executive
duties ; and,  if such  duties  can  be constitutionally  performed,  the
officers  entrusted  with  their  performance  ought  to  be appointed  by
the  executive  department  of  the  government.

It  would  seem,  therefore,  that  Supervisors  of  election,  as  now
appointed,  are  not  officers  properly  appointed  ;  and  that  all  pro-
visions  of the  Revised  Statutes,  which  relate  to  them,  are  nugatory
and  void.

There  are  other  objections  to  the  constitutionality  of the  sections
of the  Revised  Statutes,  upon  which  the  indictments  in  these  cases

were  founded.

Those  sections,  and,  all  the  sections,  it  is believed,  contained  in
Title  26  of said  Statutes,  relating  to  the  “  Elective  Franchise,”  are
based  upon  an  erroneous  understanding  of  the  true  lines  of  divi-
sion  between  Federal  and  State  powers,  and  of  the  proper  theory
upon  which  Federal  legislation  ought  to  be  framed.

The  people  of  the  United  States  ordained  and  established  the
Federal  Constitution  for  the  government  of the  United  States  as  a

7

governmental  whole,  and  not  for  the  government  of the  individual
States.
Each  State  established  a  Constitution  for  itself,  and  in
that  Constitution  provided  such  limitations  and  restrictions  on  the
its  judgment  dictated.
powers  of  its  particular  government  as
Barron  vs.  Mayor  and  City  Council  of Baltimore,  7  Peters,

247.

Under  the  Constitution  of  the  United  States,  certain  exclusive
powers  are  vested  in  the  United  States;  certain  exclusive  powers
remain  in  the  respective  States;  and  certain  other  powers  may  be
exercised,  concurrently,  by the  United  States  and  by  the  several
States,  so  long  as  their  use  by the  several  States  is not  inconsistent
with  their  use  by  the  United  States.

Sturges  vs.  Crowninshield,  4 Wheat.,  195,  196.
Houston  vs.  Moore,  5 Wheat.,  24.
Gilman  vs.  Philadelphia,  3 Wallace,  730.

It  was  the  purpose  of  that  Constitution  that  the  exclusive
powers,  conferred  upon  the  Government  of  the  United  States,
should  be  exercised  exclusively  by  that  Government;  that  the
powers,  reserved  to  the  States,  should  be  exercised  exclusively  by
the  people  of  such  States;  and  that  those  powers  which  were  con-
current  in  the  United  States  and  in  the  respective  States  should  be
exercised  by the  Federal  and  State  Governments,  if  they  could  be
properly  concurrently  exercised,  in  the  same  complete  manner  as
if each  were  the  sole  possessor  of such  powers.

U.S.  vs.  Cruikshank,  92  U.S.,  549,  541.
U.  S.  vs.  Reese,  92  U.S.,  221.

But  it would  seem  to  be an  elemental  principle  of  the  Constitu-
tion  of the  United  States  that  the  legislative  powers  of the  Federal
and  State  Governments  are  wholly  distinct;  that  the  State  Gov-
ernments  have  not  the  constitutional  power  to  enforce,  by  penal
legislation,  the  observance  of  Federal  laws;  that  the  Federal

legislation,  the  observance  of  State  laws;  that  the  State  Govern-

-—  On

If  these  propositions  be true,  Congress  could  not  constitutionally
confer  upon  a  United  States  Marshal,  and  his  general  deputies,
and  upon  the  special  deputies  appointed  by  him,  the  power  to
keep  the  peace  at  the  polls  in  any  State,  and  to  preserve  order  at
the  places  of  registration  of  voters  and  at  such  polls.

Revised  Statutes  U.S.,  sec.  2022.

9

The  keeping  of  the  public  peace  within  the  limits  of  a  State,
upon  such  occasions,  is  a common  law  duty  and  power,  belonging
to  such  State  Government,  which  Congress  cannot  assume  or  de-
volve  upon  Federal  agencies.

Congress  could  not,  constitutionally,  empower  the  Marshal  of the
United  States,  and  his  general  and  special  deputies,  to  prevent
fraudulent  registration  or  fraudulent  voting  at  the  polls  in  any
State  at  a  Congressional  election.

Rev.  Stat.  U.  S., sec.  2022.
Slaughter  House  Cases,  16  Wallace,  77.

If  any  registration  or  voting  at  such  polls  was  fraudulent,  it
was  fraudulent  because  it was  in  contravention  of  some  provision:
Congress  has  not  the
of  the  Constitution  or  laws  of  such  State.
power  to  make  the  violation  of  the  Constitution  or  laws  of  any
State  an  offence  against  the  United  “States;  nor  can  it  devolve
upon  a  Federal  officer  the  power  of enforcing  the  laws  of a  State.

Slaughter  House  Cases,  16  Wallace,  77.

Congress  could  not,  constitutionally,  provide  a  punishment  for
voting  at  a  place  within  a  State,  where  the  offender  was  not  law-
fully  entitled  to  vote,  when  the  unlawfulness  of  his  vote  depended
wholly  upon  the  law  of  the  State  within  which  his  vote  was  cast.

Revised  Stat.  U.S.,  sec.  5511.

Congress  could  not,  constitutionally,  provide  a  punishment  for
voting,  without  having  a  lawful  right  to  vote  in  a  particular  State,
when  the  unlawfulness  of  the  act  depended  entirely  on  the  law  of
the  State  in  which  it was  committed.

I bid.

Congress  could  not,  constitutionally,  provide  for  the  punishment
of  persons  within  a  State  who  did,  in such  State,  some  act  forbid-

2

10

den  by the  laws  of  such  State,  to  secure  to  himself,  or  another,  an
improper  opportunity  to  vote.

Lbid.

Congress  could  not,  constitutionally,  punish  an  offender  for  vio-
lating  the  laws  of  the  State  in  which  he  resided  by  preventing
another  from  freely  exercising  the  right  of  suffrage.

Ibid,

Congress  could  not,  constitutionally,  punish  an  offender  for  in-
terfering  with  a  State  officer  of  election  in  the  discharge  of  his
duties.

J bid.

Congress  could  not,  constitutionally,  punish  a  State  officer  for
receiving  the  vote  of  a  person,  not  entitled  to  vote  under  the  law
of  a  State,  or  for  refusing  to  receive  the  vote  of  a  person  entitled
to  vote  under  the  laws  of  a  State.

Ibid.

Congress  could  not,  constitutionally,  by adopting  a  registration,
made  under  the  laws  of  any  State,  or  territory,  for  a  State,  or
other  election,  at  which  a  Representative  in  Congress  might  be
chosen,  acquire  the  power  to  control  the  manner  of  making  of
such  registration,  or  the  power  to  punish  any  frauds,  or  offences,
committed  against  State,  or  Territorial  laws,  in  the  making  up  of
such  registration.

Rev.  Stat.  U.  S., sec.  5513.

Passing  from  these  sections,  which  illustrate  the  erroneous
theory  upon  which  every  portion  of  the  Revised  Statutes  of  the
United  States,  relating  to  the  “  Elective  Franchise,”  is grounded,

~et

11

we  may  certainly  assume  that  sections  5514  and  5515  are  invalid.
Congress  could  not  constitutionally  provide  that  the  law  of  apy
State,  or  territory,  should  create  prima  facie  evidence  that  any
person  was  guilty  of a  violation  of  a  Statute  of  the  United  States.

Revised  Stat.  U.  S., see.  5514.  Nor  could  Congress  constitutionally
provide  for  the  punishment  of  a State  officer,  who  might  violate  the

requirements  of the  eleetion  laws  of  such  State.

If  Congress  had  no  power  to  devolve  the appointment  of Super-
visors  of  election  upon  the  Judges  of  the  Civeuit  Courts  of  the
United  States,  it could  not  provide  for  the  punishment  of those  who
interfered  with  such  officers  in  the  performance  of  their  assumed
If  Congress  had  no  right  to  share  with  the  particular
duties.

States  the  duty  of preserving  peace  at  the  polls,  it  had  no  power  to
provide  for  the  punishment  of  those  who  obstructed  Federal

officers  in  the  execution  of  illegal  powers,  or  who  refused  to  assist
such  officers  in  their  attempts  to  execute  such  illegal  powers.

Revised  Stat.  U.S,  see.  5522.

If  Congress  has  a  complete,  “  ultimate”  diseretionary  power
over  the  elections  of  members  of  the  House  of  Representatives,

and  may  make  all  the  regulations  on  this  subject,  which  a  State
can  make,  and  ean  alter  the  regulations  of  a  State  as  to  such  elec-
g¢  them  could  alter  them,—
tions  as  completely  as  the  State  enactin

it  must  certainly  exercise  these  powers  in  a  proper  way.
If  the
whole  power  is  exercised  by  Congress,  it  must  enact  a  system

of  election  laws,  regulating  the  election  of  members  of  the  House

of  Representatives,  which  will  be  complete  and  operative  in

itself.

If  the  partial  power  is  exercised,  it  must,  nevertheless,  be

exercised  by enacting  a  law  which,  while  it  alters  the  laws  of  the

States  regulating  elections  for  members  of  the  House  of  Repre-
sentatives  in  some  particular,  will  nevertheless  be, in  itself,  in  such

particular,  complete  and  operative  as  a  Federal  law.

12

It  is  respectfully  submitted,  that  the  sections  of  the  Revised
Statutes,  upon  which  these  indictments  were  founded,  not  having
been  passed  in  pursuance  of  a  constitutional  power,  and  not  being
constitutional  in form,  substance  and  effect,  are  void,  and  that  the
petitioners,  who  have  been  deprived  of  their  liberty  under  such
unconstitutional  provisions,  ought  to  be  discharged  from  custody.

CHARLES  J.  M.  GWINN,

Attorney-General  of Maryland

appearing for the  Petitioners.

Petition  for  Habeas  Ccrpus.

SUPREME COUR

UNITED  STATES.
—
OCTOBER  TERM,  1879.

SUMMARY  OF  THE  ARGUMEYT.

This  is  the  case  of  five  election  judges,  appointed  under
the  Jaws  of  Maryland,  and  convicted  in  the  Circuit  Court  of
the  United  States  for  the  District  of  Maryland,  under  those
sections  of  the  Revised  Statutes  which  provide  regulations
of  the  manner  of holding  elections  for  members  of Congress.

Siebold  is  convicted  of  doing  an  act  unauthorized  by
(Record,

the  laws  of  Maryland  or  of  the  United  States.
page  12.)

Tucker  is  convicted  of  preventing  and  hindering  a
Special  Deputy  Marshal  in his  free  attendance  at  the  polls,
(page  15);  and  also  of  doing  an  act  unauthorized  by  the
laws  of  Maryland  or  the  United  States.

(Pages  16-17.)

Burns  is convicted  of hindering  a  Supervisor  of Elections
in  his  duty  of  inspecting  and  scrutinizing  the  manner  in
which  the  voting  was.  done,  and  of  keeping  him  out  of  the
room  where  the  poll  of  election  was  held;  and  also  of  re-
sisting  a  Special  Deputy  Marshal  and  the  United  States

[ 2 |

Marshal  in  their  arrest  of  him  for  his  offence  of  hindering
the  said  Supervisor  of  Elections.

(Pages  20,  21,  22.)

Coleman  is  convicted  of  hindering  a  Special  Deputy
Marshal  in  his  free  attendance  at  the  polls,  and  of  support-
ing and  protecting  a  Supervisor  of Elections  in  the  perform-
ance  of  his  (the  Supervisor’s)  duties  at  the  election ; and
also  of  doing  an  act  unauthorized  by the  laws  of  Maryland
or  the  United  States.

(Pages  24,  25.)  And

Bowers  is  convicted  of  hindering  a  Supervisor  in  the
performance  of  his  duty—of  personally  inspecting  and  scru-
tinizing  the  manner  in  which  the  voting  was  done  at  the
poll  of  election.

(Page  28.)

Under  these  convictions  they  are  now  confined  in  the
custody  of  the  Marshal  of  the  United  States  for  the  District
of  Maryland  and  the  Warden  of  the  Jail  of  the  City  of Bal-
timore,  from  which  imprisonment  they  pray  to  be  relieved
by this  Court  on  Habeas  Corpus.

I.

The  Act  of  Congress,  under  which  they  have  been
convicted,  and-are  now  deprived  of  their  liberty,  is set  forth
in  Title  XXVI  of  the  Revised  Statutes,  in  sections  2011,
2012,  2016,  2017,  and  in  other  sections  under  the  title
‘*  Crimes,’’  being  sections  5514,  5515  and  5522.

These  sections  are  the  regulations  prescribed  by law  of
the  manner  of  holding  elections  for  Representatives  in  Con-
gress,  which  law  has  been  passed  under  the  power  granted
to  Congress  by article  1, section  4 of  the  Constitution  of the
United  States.

They  provide  that  the  Circuit  Courts  shall  appoint  Super-
visors  of  Elections,  whose  duty  it  shall  be  to  guard  and

scrutinize  the  manner  in  which  elections  at  which  Represen-
tatives  in  Congress  are  voted  for,  are  held  by  the  officers  ap-
pointed  under  State  laws  to  conduct  the  same,  and  to  inspect

the  registration  of  voters,  the  voting  and  the  counting  of

[3 |

the  ballots,  and  watch  generally  that  the  election  be  fairly
conducted,

They  further  provide  that  the  Marshals  of  the  United
States  shall  appoint  Special  Deputy  Marshals,  whose  duty  it
shall  be,  to  attend  the  places  of registration,  and  voting,  sup-
port  and  protect  the  Supervisors  of Elections  in  the  perform-
ance  of  their  duties,  to  prevent  fraud  on  elections,  and  to
keep  the  peace  at  the  polls.

The  Supervisors  are  the  superintendents  of  the  manner
in  which  State  officers  perform  their  duties,  and  the  Mar-
shals  are  to  protect  the  Supervisors,  and  prevent  the  State
officers  from  committing  frauds,  and  to  preserve  order.

~The  punitory  part  of  the  law  then  denounces  penalties
for  preventing  Supervisors  and  Special
on  every  person
Deputy  Marshals  from  performing  these  duties  ; and  further-
more,  particularly  provides  punishments  for  officers  of  elec-

tions,  appointed  under  State  laws,  for  neglecting  to  perform

their  duties  under  such  State  laws;  or  who  violate  their
duties  imposed  on  tiem  by  such  laws;  or  who  do  any  act
not  authorized  by  any  law  of  the  State,  or  of  the  United
States,  with  intent  to  affect  the  election.

These  are  the  regulations  of  the  manner  of  holding  the
elections  for  Representives  in  Congress,  made  by law  of Con-
gress,  under  the  article  and
section  of  the  Constitution  re-
ferred  to.

It  is  claimed  that  they  are  not  a  proper  execution  of the
power  conferred  on  Congress  in  that  section,  and  are,  there-
fre,  unconstitutional  and  void.

And  that  the  petitioners  having  been  convicted,  and

being  now  held  under  a  void  law,  must  be  discharged.

II.

Article  1,  section  4  of  the  Constitution  is  the  only
It

source  from  which  power  is claimed  to  enact  such  laws.
is as  follows:

a4

‘(Tne  TIMES,  PLACES  AND  MANNER  OF  HOLDING  ELECTIONS
FOR  SENATORS  AND  REPRESENTATIVES  SHALL  BE  PRESCRIBED  IN
EACH  STATE  BY  THE  LEGISLATURE  THEREOF ; BUT  THE  CONGRESS
MAY  AT  ANY  TIME,  BY  LAW,  MAKE  OR  ALTER  SUCH  REGULA-
TIONS,  EXCEPT  AS  TO  THE  PLACFS  OF  CHOOSING  SENATORS.  ’’

The  intent  and  meaning,  the  purpose  and  object  of  this
‘lause  of  the  Constitution  are  plain  and  manifest  from  the
historical  vesume  (in  the  brief,  pages  57,  48)  of  its  origin,
development,  adoption  and  contemporaneous  construction.
It  was  part  of  the  compromise  upon  which  the  Constitution
was  founded,  between  the  friends  of  a  National  government
of  the  people,  led  by Edmund  Randolph,  and  the adherents
of  the  scheme  of  a  confederacy  of  States,  whose  views  were
presented  by  William  Patterson.

They  atl,  at  last,  united,  in  support  of  a  plan  of  gov-
ernment,  in  which  States  should  be  represented  in  the
Senate,  and  the  people  in  the  House  ; the  one  body  composed
of  equal  representatives  of  the  State  ;  the  other  of  those  of
One  preserved  the  equality  of  the  States,  the
the  people.
other  the  power  of  the  people.
The  Representatives  of  the
latter  were  to  be  elected  by  electors,  whose  qualifications
were  fixed  by  the  States,  but  they  represented  the  whole
They  were  the  popular  part  of  the
body  of  the  people.

government.

The  time,  place  and  manner  of  holding  the  elections  for
these  democratic  representatives  were  directed  and  required
It  was  considered  more  expedient,
to  be  fixed  by the  States.
in  the  language  of  Mr.  Madison,  that  this  duty  should  be
But  then  it  might  happen
first  committed  to  the  States.

that  States  might  neglect  or  refuse,  as  Rhode  Island  had
done  ;  or  might  be  disabled  by  invasion,  as  South  Carolina
had  been,  from  performiog  this  Federal  duty  ;  or  it  might
happen  that  the  State  Legislatures,  already  represented  in
the  Senate,  would  so  use  their  power  as  to  be  also  repre-
sented  in  the  House,  and  thus  the  compromise  be  defeated,
the  people  deprived  of  their  democratic  representation,  and

[8]

they  disobey  ;  but  a  Federal  Supervisor  may  consider  a  vote
legal  which  a  State  Judge  believes  to  be  illegal  ; and  a  Fed-
eral  Court  may  construe  a  vote  to  be  legal  which  a  State
Court  decides  to  be  illegal.
In  either  event  the  State  Judge
is  liable  to  suffer;  he  is  being  .operated  on,  at  the  same
time,  by two  distinct  wills  in  relation  to  the  same  subject-
matter,  to  wit:  the  manner  of  holding  the  election—which
this  Court  has  said  cannot  be  done.
Besides  this,  these
Federal  officers,  without  warrant,  can  arrest  any  officer  of
the  State;  while  no  officer  of the  State  with  warrant  can,  on
the  day  of election,  arrest  any  one  of  these  Supervisors  or
Special  Deputy  Marshals  for  any  offence,  including  treason
and  felony.

The  cases  at  bar  are  those  of Judges  of Election  who  have
been  convicted  under  this  law.
In  another  case,  a  Gov-
ernor  of  a  State  was.  haled  to  the  bar  of  a  Federal  Court,
aud  put  upon  his  trial  for  not  performing  his  duty  in  regard
to  elections,  under  the  laws  of  the  State  whose  Executive  he
was,  aud  whose  laws  de,  and  not  the  Federal  Court,  was

sworn  to  execute.

U.S.  vs.  Clayton,  2  Dillon,  219.

A  law  which  of  necessity  and  unavoidably  brings  on
such  conflicts  between  Federal  and  State  power,  caused
solely  and  alone  by  the  attempt  to  regulate  only  part  of
and  by  leaving  the  res-
‘‘the  manner  of holding  elections,’’
idue  of  the  manner  to  be  regulated  by  the  State,  is  uneon-
stitutional ; and  if,  in  order  to  prevent  this  collision,  the
States  withdraw  the  regulations  ‘*  of  the  manner  of holding
the  elections  ’’  made  by them,  then  the  whole  syvetem  fails,
and  the  very  evil  which  the  power  was  granted  to  Congress
to  prevent,  will  occur.

No  elections  can  be  held.

3?

It  was  given  as  a  power  of  self-preservation  ;  it  may  be
made  by the  fiat  of the  States-—the  power  of self-destruction.

[9]

V.

Second.  But  if  this  power  is  not  conclusive  and  entire,
and  may  be  exercised  on  a  part  of  the  subject,  then  such
part  as  it  does  regulate,  must  be  beyond  the  control  of  the
States.

Any  regulation  that  is  made  must  be  by law  of  Con-
cress,  enacted  by  it,  and  subject  to  repeal  or  modification
only  by it.
The  regulations  must  be  made  by law,  and  the
power  to  make  them  cannot  be  delegated.

Congress  could  not  provide  that  the  Attornev  General
or  the  President  should,  from  time  to  trme,  make  or  alter
such  regulations  of  the  manner  of  holding  elections  as  he
might  consider  necessary  and  proper.

The  regulations,  therefore,  must  be  beyond  the  control
But  especially  must  they  be  be-

of any  body  but  Congress.
yond  the  interference  or  control  of the  States.

The  very  evil,  to  remedy  or  prevent  which  this  power
was  created  in  Congress,  was  the  evil  of  State  intervention
or  non-action  ; and  when  a  remedy  is  applied  which  leaves
the  evil  untouched,  then  such  remedy  cannot  be  a  constitu-
Now  the  regulations  made  by this
tional  exercise  of  power.
They  leave  the  whole
law  of  Congress  do  this  precise  thing.
manner  of  holding  the  election  absolutely  and  entirely
within  the  control  of the  States;  for  the  election  itself  may
be  dispensed  with,  or  the  law  authorizing  the  election  be
repealed,  and  thus  the  law  making  the  regulations  rendered
inoperative.

.

New  the  law  of  Congress  making  these  regulations

either—

A.  Adopts  and  thereby  re-enacts  all  State  election  laws

in  force  at  the  time  of its  enactment.

B.  Or  it does  not  adopt  them.

:
C.  Or  it adopts  in  advance  each  modification  and  alter-
amon  of  the  State  law-as  may  be  thereafter  made  by  the

State.

[10]

A.  If  these  Acts  of  1870  and  1871,  re-enacted  by the
revision  of  June  24,  1874,  adopted  all  the  State  election
laws  then  in  force,  those  State  election  laws  became  thereby
Federal  laws  and  regulations  just  as  much  as  if  they  had
been  re-enacted  and  set  out  totidem  verbis  by the  Congress.
They  are  in  force  now  as  Federal  laws.

But  any  State,  since  1874,  may  have  repealed  her  elec-
tion  laws,  and  enacted  other  different  regulations  of  the
TheState  of Maryland,  whose
manner  of holding  elections.
Election  Judges  have  been  convicted,  and  who  now  pray  to
be  released,  has  actually  done  so.
The  regulations  made
by Law  of  Congress  adopted  and  re-enacted  the  regulations
of  the  manner  of  holding  elections  in  existence  in  Maryland
in  June,  1874.
Those  regulations  provided  that  Judges  of
Elections  for  the  city  of  Baltimore,  should  be  appointed  by
the  Board  of Police  Commissioners,  who  were  elected  by  the
Legislature.

The  congressional  regulations,  therefore,  in  Baltimore
applied  to  Judges  of  Election,  appointed  by  the  Board  of
Police  Commissioners,  and  to  no  other  Judges  of  Election,
created  in  another  mode.
But  the  General  Assembly  of
Maryland,  by  Act  of  1876,  chapter  223,  passed  April  3,

1876,  repealed.  those  sections  of  the  Code  of  Public  Local
Laws  of  Maryland,  which  thus  provided  for  the  appointment
of  those  Judges,  and  created  instead  ‘‘a  Board  of  Super-

visors  of Llecticns jor the  City  of  Baltimore,  who  shall  be  ap-
potted  by the  Governor,  and  who  shall  appoint  three  Judges
of  Llection  for  each  Lilection  Precinct  of said  Cily,  one  of
whom  shall  be  of a  political  party  different  from  that  with
which  the  other  two  are  connected.”’

The  Judges  under  the  Maryland  Act  of  1876—that  is,
the  Judges  who  were  convicted—are  different  Judges,  ap-

pointed  by a  different  department  of  Government,  with  en-
tirely  different  requisites  and  qualifications,  from  the  Judges
contemplated  by the  Act  of  Congress,  appointed  under  the
adopted  Federal-State  or  State-Federal  law.

[11]

Therefore,  if  the  Law  of Congress  adopts  the  State  reg-
ulations  in  force  at  the  time  of  its  passage,  it can  be,  and
has  been  in  Maryland,  actually  repealed  and  modified.

Or  the  State  regulations  adopted  by Act  of  Congress  in
1574  may  be  entirely  repealed,  and  instead  of  them,  others
might  be  substituted  which  merely  provided  for  the  appoint-
ment  of Judges  of Election,  with  general  directions  to hold  the
election  in  such  manner  as  in  their  discretion  they  consid-
ered  most  proper  and  reasonable.
What  then  would  become
of the  congressional  regulations  by adoption  of  1874?  Upon
what  could  they  operate?
The  only  duty  imposed  by State
regulations  would  be  to  hold  the  election  in  such  manner  as
they,  in  their  judgment,  deemed  reasonable  and  proper.
Could  the  Judges  be  held  responsible,  under  the  Law  of
Congress,  for  an  unreasonable  exercise  of  this  discretion  ?

But  if, on  the  other  hand,  the  regulations  adopted  by
the  Law  of  Congress  be  considered  the  supreme  law  and
regulations ; and  that  all  regulations  subsequently  made  by
States  inconsistent  with  them  be  considered  invalid,  so  that
the  State  law  altering  or  repealing  them  would  be  void,  still
if  such  State  law  be  passed,  so  altering  or  repealing  the
regulations  before  then  adopted  by  Law  of  Congress,  and
the  election  be  held  under  such  void  law,  by  Judges  ap-
pointed  under  such  void  law,  those  Judges  so  appointed
could  not  be  held  responsible  for  malfeasance  or  non-feas-
ance  under  such  void  law.
If  the  subsequent  State  law  be
void,  all  proceedings  under  it  are  void.

B.  Or  the  Law.of  Congress  does  not  adopt  the  regula-
tious  of  the  manner  of  holding  elections  prescribed  by  the
States.

If  this  is so,  then  such  regulations  remain  subject  to
modification,  repeal,  and  entire  control  of  the  States;  and
this  Law  of Congress  does  not  make  regulations  of the  man-
ner  of  holding  the  elections,  and  is, therefore,  no  appro-
priate  exercise  of  the  power  granted.

C.  Or  the  Law  of Congress  adopts  each  modification  of
the  regulations  prescribed  by the  States  as  soon  as  they  are
made,  which  then  become  regulations  made  by Law  of  Con-
eTess.

But  this  will  be  a  delegation  of  the  power  granted  to
The  legislative
Congress  to  make  regulations  by  law.
power  cannot  be  delegated.
‘‘  Where  the  sovereign  power
of  the  State  has  located  the  power  to  make  laws,  there  it
must  remain;  and  by the  ccnstitutional  agency  alone  must
the  laws  be  made.’’

(Cooley  on  Const.  Lim.  117.)

In  Martin  vs.  Hunter,  it  was  held  that  no  part  of  the
judicial  power  of the  United  States  can  be  conferred  on  State
officers  ;  a  fortiori  is  it that  no  part  of the  legislative  power
of  the  United  States  can  be  delegated  to  the  States.

Vi.

Third.  And  this  power  must  be  exercised  by  appro-
priate  and  constitutional  laws,  which  laws  must  contain  and
set  furth  the  regulations  made,  and  must  not  delegate  the
power  of making  them  to  any  other  authority  whatever.

The  power  conferred  is  to  make  regulations  ‘‘  by law.’’
It  is  not  to  regulate  or  to  provide  for  making  regulations,
but  the  regulations  must  be  contained  and  specified  in,  and
set  furth  by the  Jaw  which  makes  them.

The  object  of  the  power,  the  reason  for  which  it  was
conferred,  was  to  secure  the  elections  of  the  Representatives
of the  people  in  the  Lower  House  free  from  the  cabals  of  re-
fractory  States;  above  the  misfortunes  of  the  unfortunate ;
beyond  the  carelessness  of  the  negligent;  tu  preserve  and
perpetuate  the  Government  for  all  time,  by providing  it with
the  means  by  which  it could  communicate  with  and  operate
directly  upon  the  people,  free  from  the  interference  or  con-
trol  of  the  States,  and  thus  secure  an  unbroken  succession
of  Representatives  in  the  House,

But  the  regulations  made  by  this  Act  of  Cougress  are,
as  we  have  shown,  entirely  subject  to  the  power  of  the

[18]

States,  anl  are  not  appropriate  to  the  end  in  view,-and  do
not  tend  to  accomplish  the  object  for  which  the  pewer  was
granted,  to  wit:  the  election  of Representatives  in  Congress
free  from  the  control  or  interference  of the  States  ; and  these
regulations  thus  made  by  this  law,  are  not  made  by a  law
appropriate  to  the  object.

They  are  to  be  regulations  of the  manner  of  holding  the
Regulations  imply  rule,  and  as  Mr.  Madison
elections.
said,  ought  to  be  uniform  throughout  the  Continent.
They
denounce  penalties  on  State  Judges  for  doing  acts  forbidden
or  not  authorized  by State  laws.
Under  these  regulations
it  may  become  the  duty  ot  the  Chief  Justice  to  sentence  a
Stute  Judge  in  Maryland  for  opening  a  ballot  when  it  was
handed  to  him  by  the  voter:  the  next  week  he  may  be
obliged  to  punish  a  State  Judge  in  Virginia  for  failing  to
open  a  ballot  when  delivered  to  him;  the  week  after  he
may  be  obliged,  under  these  regulations,  to  imprison  State
Judges  in  North  Carolina  for  receiving  and  recording  a  viva
voce  vote  ;  and  two  days  after  that  to  send  State  Judges  in
South  Carolina  to  the  same  prison  for  refusing  to  receive
such  viva  voce  vote.
And  worse  than  this,  when  two  years
after  he  again  goes  his  circuit,  he  may  find  all  the  laws
changed,  and  those  things  which  were  unlawful  in  Mary-
land  rendered  lawful,  and  those  things  which  were  lawiul
made  unlawful;  and  so  in  each  of  the  other  States.

So  that  it  may  happen,  and  it  can  happen,  that  two
citizens  of  Maryland  may  be  confined  in  the  same  prison—

the  one  for  doing  a  thing,  and  the  other  for  not  doing  the
self-same  thing—each  punished  under  the  same  regulations
made  by  Law  of  Congress  unchanged  by  Congress,  and  sen-
tenced  by the  same  Court  speaking  by  the  same  Chief  Jus-
tice.

It  cannot  be  that  these  regulations  made  by law  can  be
such  regulations  of  the  manner  of  holding  elections  made
by such  law  as  is  required  by  the  whole  spirit  of  the  Con-
stitution,  and  authorized  by the  clause  in  the  Constitution

under  consideration.

(14 |

That  this  power  cannot  be  delegated  has  been  shown
That  this  exercise  of  it  by  this  ]aw  is  unconstitu-

above.

tiona!  will  be  demonstrated  hereafter.

VII.

Fourth,  The  regulations  made  by  law  of  Congress  of
the  manner  of  holding  elections  must  be  enforced  by  Fed-

eral  power,  acting  through  Federal  officers,  Constitutionally

appointed,

A.  The  scheme  of  the  American  Constitution  is  a  sys-
tem  of  co  ordinate  Governments  and  co-ordinate  depart-
nents.
Kkach  Government  and  every  department  must
operate  within  its own  sphere,  and  neither  can  interfere  with
nor  impose  duties  on  the  other.  The  Federal  Government  can
no  more  impose  part  of its  duties  on  the  State  Governments
than  the  Legislative  Department  can  impose  part  of  its
duties  on  the  Executive ; nor  require  the  Judiciary  to  per-
form  the  functions  of  the  Executive.
The  Federal  Govern-
ment  is  provided  with  all  the  powers  necessary  for  the  full
and  perfect  execution  of  its  functions.
It  is  a Government
in  part  of  States,  as  was  said  by the  Chief  Justice  in  Cruik-
shank’s  case,
It  is also,  for  certain  purposes,  a  Government
of  the  people.

The  Constitution  imposes  obligations  on  States,  but  it
provides  no  means  by which  States  can  be  compelled  to  per-
form  them.

This  Court,  speaking  by Chief  Justice  Taney,  in  Ken-

tucky  vs.  Dennison,  24  Howard,  107,  says:

‘*  NOR  IS  THERE  ANY  CLAUSE  OR  PROVISION  IN  THE  CONSTITU-
TION  WHICH  ARMS  THE  GOVERNMENT  OF  THE  UNITED  STATES  WITH
INDEED  SUCH  A  FOWER  WOULD  PLACE  EVERY  STATE
THIS  POWER.

UNDER  THE  CONTROL  AND  DOMINION  OF  THE  GENERAL  GOVERN-

MENT,  EVEN  IN  THE  ADMINISTRATION  O°  ITS  INTERNAL  AFFAIRS
AND  RESERVED  RIGHTS.’

BE

Licncud

And  the  present  Court,  by Mr.  Justice  Nelson;  in  The
Collector  vs.  Day,  11  Wallace,  declares  that  ‘‘  any  Govern-
ment,  whose  means  employed  in  conducting  its  operations,  if
subject  to  the  control  of another  and  distinct  Government,  can
exist  only  at  the  mercy  of that  Government.’’

And  if  the  Congress  can  impose  duties  on  State  Judges
of  Election  in  regard  to  elections,  and  provide  penalties  for
non-performance  of  them,  they  can  as  well  require  other
things  in  relation  thereto  to  be  done  by the  Governors,  the
Judiciary  and  the  Legislative  Departments,  and  thus,  in
fact,  the  State  Governments  would  exist  only  at  the  mercy
of  the  General  Government.

While,  therefore,  it  1s  clear  that  the  Congress  cannot
impose  duties  on  State  officers,  as  was  decided  by  this  Court
in  Prigg  vs.
Penn.,  it  is  equally  clear  that  it  cannot
punish  them  for  non-performance  of  their  duties  under
State  laws.

If  the  Constitution

provides  no  means  -by  which  the
(reneral  Government  can  compel  a  State  to  perform  a  duty
required  of  the  State  by  the  Constitution,  and  if  the  Con-
gress  cannot  compel  a  State  officer  to  perform  a  duty  imposed
by  itself,  a  fortiori  is  it  that  it  can  provide  no  means  by

which  State  officers  can  be compelled  by the  Federal  author-
ity  to  perform  their  duties  to  their  States,  under  State  laws.
These  regulations,  made  by  this  law  of  Congress,  de-
nounce  punishments  against  State  Officers  of  Election  for
doing  what  they  are  torbidden  to  do;  for  not  doing  what

they  are  commanded  to  do;  and  even  further  than  that,  for
doing  things  unauthorized  by State  laws.

If  these  State  laws  are  to  be  considered  State  regula-
tions  of  the  manner  of  holding  the  elections,  then  it  is clear
that  the  law  of Congress,  punishing  State  Judges  for  violation
of State  duties,  as  State  officers  under  State  laws,  is contrary
tothe  Constitution,  and  void  ; orif  these  State  regulations  are
to  be  considered  as  having  been  adopted  by  Congress, and
thereby  to  have  become  Federal  regulations,  then  this  law,

[ 16  |

requiring  these  Federal  regulations  to  be  enforced  by State
Federal  laws  must  be  enforced  by Federal
officers,  is void.
officers,  and  can  be  enforced  by none  other.

B.  The  State  officers  cannot  become  Federal  officers  by

adoption,  and  thereby  become  amenable  to  Federal  law.

Under  the  Constitution,  article  2, section  2, officers  of
the  United  States  can  only  be  appointed  in  the  manner
therein  provided.

The  manuer  of  appointment  is—

Ist.  Under  the  Constitution.

2d.  By  the  President,  with  the  alvice  and  consent  ot
the  Senate  ; and  when  Congress  has,  by law,  vested  the  ap-
pointment  of  inferior  officers.

3d.  By  the  President  alone.

4th.

By the  Courts  of  law.

And

5th.  By the  heads  of  departments.

And  by article  2, section  3, the  President  shall  commis-

sion  all  officers  of  the  United  States.

These  State  Judges  of  Election,  therefore,  cannot  be-
come  Federal  officers  by adoption,  because  they  are  neither
appointed  nor  commissioned  in  the  manner  prescribed  by
the  Constitution.
‘They  are  State  officers,  and  cannot  be com-
pelled  to  exeeute  Federal  laws;  nor  be  punished  under  this
Act  of  Congress  for  not  executing  State  laws.

As  a  Governmeat  of  States  the  Constitution  imposes
certain  duties  on  the  States.
But  when  they  fail,  neglect  or
refuse  to  perform  those  duties,  there  is  no  power  to  compel
them  as  States.
The  force  of  the  Government  of  the people
is-then  applied,  and  the  Federal  power,.  operating  directly
on  the:  people:  by its  own:  laws,  exeeated  by its:own  officers,
secures:  the  performanee  of  those  duties:  which  the  States
ought  to,  but  have  faaled  to  perform.

|

*

[17]

CU’.  And  the  officers,  to  enforce  the  regulations  made  by
law  of  the  manner  of  holding  elections,  must  be  Constitu-
tionally  appointed.

The  regulations  made  by  law  of  Congress  provide  for

the  appointment  of—

Ist.  Supervisors  of  Llections,  by  the  Circuit  Courts,
whose  dutv  it shall  be  to  scrutinize  and  to  guard  the  elec-
tion,  to  attend  the  polls,  inspect  the  count,  and  watch  gener-
ally  the  manuer  in  which  the  State  Judges  perform  their

duties.

2d.  Special  Deputy  Marshals,  who  shall  protect  the

Supervisors  in  the  discharge  of  their  duties,  and  likewise
watch, guard  and  scrutinize  the  manner  of holding  the  elec-
tions  provided  by  the  States.

The  Supervisors  of Elections  are  Executive  officers,  and

their  appointment  cannot  be  vested  in  the  Courts.

ws

[ 18 |

The  Supervisors  of  Elections;  therefore,  have  no  legal
existence ; and  the  fact  of their  appointment  by the  Courts,
and  the  acceptance  of  the  duties  of  the  office,  will  make  no
difference,  as  will  be  seen  by the  decision  of  this  Court  in
Yale  Todd’s  case,  reported  in  the  note  to  Ferreira’s  case.

The  Supervisors’  acts  are  void,  and  no  man  can  be  pun-
ished,  under  these  regulations  made  by Congress,  for  resist-
ing  them  in  the  performance  of  their  so-called  duties.
But
the  duties  of the  Special  Deputy  Marshals  are  to  assist  and
protect  the  Supervisors,  and  also  to  watch  the  State  Judges
and  to  preserve  order  at  the  polls.
All  these  duties  are  so
inextricably  mingled  and  mixed  that  it is  utterly  impossible
to  separate  them ; and  when  a  man  resists  a  Marshal  in  the
performance  of  his  duties—part  of  which  are  unconstitu-
tional,  and  part  legal—no  one  can  tell  where  the  void  part
ends,  and  where  the  valid  part  begins;  therefore,  the  law,
so  far  as  it  imposes  these  mixed  duties  on  the  Marshais,  is
unconstitutional  also.

VIl.

It  fullows,  therefore—

Ist.  That  the  Act  of  Congress  is  unconstitutional  in  so
far  as  it  imposes  penalties  on  State  Officers  of  Election  for
violation  of  duty  under  State  laws.

2d.  That  it  is  unconstitutional  in  so  far  as  it  imposes
on  Circuit  Courts  the appointment  of Supervisors  of Election.

3d.  That  it  is  unconstitutional  in  so  far  as  it requires
Deputy  Marshals  to  assist  and  protect  Supervisors  of  Elec-
tion  in  the  discharge  of  their  duty,  and  to  keep  order  at  the
elections,  when  such  order  consists  in  part  of  protecting  the
Supervisors.

4th.  That  it  is  unconstitutional  in  so  far  as  it  mixes
and  mingles  the  duty  of  the  Deputy  Marshals  and  Marshals
to  assist  Supervisors  of  Election,  to  maintain  order  in  pro-

er ee -<—- w

| 19)

‘tecting  them,  together  with  the  duty  of  preventing  fraud  at
said  election,  for  if  the  last  duty  be  valid,  the  others  are,
beyond  doubt,  void,  and  the  mixture  vitiates  the  good.

IX.

We.  therefore,  claim  that—
Ist  Siebold  shall  be  released  because  he  has  been  con.
victed  in  that  being  a  State  Judge  of  Election  he  did  things
unauthorized  by  the  laws  of  the  State  or  of  the  United
States  ;

2d.  That  Bowers  shall  be  released  because  he  stands
convicted  of  resisting  a  Supervisor  of  Election  in  the  dis-
charge  of  his  duties,  when  such  Supervisor  and  his  duties
have  no  legal  existence  ;

3d.  That  Coleman  shall  be  discharged  because  he  is
convicted  of  resisting  a  Special  Deputy  Marshal  in  the  per-
formance  of  his  duty  of  being  present  at  and  freely  attend-
ing  the  polls,  and  of supporting  and  defending  a  Supervisor
of  Elections  thereat ; and,  also,  that  being  a  State  Judge
of  Election,  he  did  an  act  unauthorized  by any  law  of  the
State  or  of the  United  States  ;

4th.  That  Tucker  shall  be  released  because  he  is con-
fined  in  that  he  hindered  and  prevented  a  Special  Deputy
Marshal  in  his  free  attendance  at  the  polls,  and  his  free
access  thereto;  and,  also,  that  being  a  State  Judge  of Elec-
tion,  he  did  an  act  not  authorized  by any  State  or  Federal
law;  and

5th.  That  Burns  shall  be  discharged  by this  Court  be-
cause  he  stands  convicted  and  confined  on  account  of having
hindered  and  prevented  a  Supervisor  in  the  discharge  of  his
duty,  and  then  resisting  a  Special  Deputy  Marshal  and  the
Marshal  himself  in  their  attempt  to  arrest  him  for  his
offence  against  the  Supervisor.

Ex  PARTE  HENRY  BOWERS  AND  FOUR  OTHERS.

AP-

PLICATION  FOR  WRIT  OF  HABEAS  CORPUS.

. 2

BRIEF  FOR  THE  UNITED  STATES.

This  is  an  application  for  the  writ  of  habeas  corpus,
made  to  the  Chief  Justice  of  the  Supreme  Court  in  his
capacity  as  circuit  justice  of the  fourth  circuit,  by Henry
Bowers,  Martin  C. Burns,  Walter  Tucker,  Lewis  Coleman,
and  Albert  Siebold,  upon  which  application  an  order  to
show  cause  was  granted  by the  Chief  Justice,  returnable
before  him  in  the  city  of  Washington  on  the  14th  of
October,  1879.
The  petition  of  each  of  the  applicants
sets  forth  substantially  the  following  facts:

That  on  the  5th  of  November,  1878,  an  election  was
held  in  the  State  of  Maryland  for  members  of Congress
of the  United  States,  and  that  the  time,  place,  and  man-
ner  of holding  such  election  had  been  prescribed  by the
legislature  of that  State;

That  the  petitioner  was  one  of  the judges  of said  elec-
tion,  duly  appointed  under  the  laws  of  said  State,  and

1*

A

|

.

that  he  discharged  his  duty  as  such  judge  under  said
laws  at  said  election  ;

That  he was  indicted  and  convicted  at  the  November
term,  1878,  of  the  circuit  court  of  the  United  States  for the
district  of  Maryland,  on  an  indictment  charging  that
the  petitioner,  being  a  judge  of  election  as  aforesaid,
did,  by  the  exercise  of  lis  power  and  authority  as  such
judge,  hinder  and  prevent  a  certain  United  States  deputy
marshal  and  a  certain  Unjted  States  supervisor  of  elec-
tions  (duly  appointed  under  sections  2012  and  2021  of
the  Revised  Statutes  of  the  United  States)  from  dis-
charging  thei  duties  of  inspecting,  superintending,
and  supervising  the  said  election,  and  also  that  the
petitioner  did  an  act  unauthorized  by any  law  of  said
State  or  of  the  United  States  [with  intent  thereby  to
affect  such  election  and  the  result  thereof],  contrary  to
sections  5515  and  5522  of  the  Revised  Statutes.

And  that,  upon  conviction, the petitioner  was  sentenced
to  be  imprisoned  for  twelve  months  in  the  jail of the  city
of  Baltimore,  and  was  by  said  court  committed  to  the
custody  of  the  marshal  of  said  district,  who  now  holds
him  by virtue  of  the  judgment  and  commitment  of  said
court,  Xe.

The  ground  of  the  application  is, the  alleged  uncon-
stitutionality  of  the  statutes  under  which  the  parties
were  indicted.

It appearing  by their  respective  petitions  that  each
of  the  petitioners  Is  in  prison  pursuant  to  a  judgment
of  the  circuit  court  of  the  United  States  for  the  district  of
Maryland,  rendered  in  a  criminal  prosecution  against
him  under  certain  statutes  of  the  United  States,  the  in-

">
eo

quiry  arises  at  the  outset,  whether  the  petitioner  can  be
relieved  by  means  of  the  writ  of  habeas  corpus  on  the
ground  upon  which  the  application  therefor  is  made.

In  regard  to  this  preliminary  question  we  submit—

1.  The  circuit  court,  having  *  cognizance  of all  erimes
andl  offenses  cognizable  under  the  authority  of  the  United
States”  (R.S.,  see.  629),  had  jurisdiction  of  the  cause,
and  its  judgment  therein  cannot  be  re-examined  by

habeas  Corpus.

The  jurisdiction  of  the  eireuit  courts  of  the  United
States,  in  criminal  cases,  is  by  virtue  of  section  629,
Revised  Statutes,  so  broad,  that  it  is  co-extensive  with
the  judicial  power  of  the  United  States  In  regard  to
Extending  to  “all  crimes  and  offenses  cog-
such  cases.
nizable  under  the  authority  of  the  United  States,”  it
necessarily  embraces  all  cases  of  that  character  which
arise  “under  the  Constitution  and  laws  of  the  United
States.”  And  as  the  judicial  power  covers  every  legis-
lative  act  of  Congress,  whether  it  be  made  within  the
limits  of  its  delegated  powers  or  be  an  assumption  of
power  beyond  the  grants  of  the  Constitution  (Ableman
vs.  Booth,  21  How.,  520;  Mayor  vs.  Cooper,  6 Wall,  252),
so  the  jurisdiction  of  the  circuit  courts  extends  to  all
crimes  and  offenses  created  by  any  legislative  act  of
Congress.
Hence,  in  a  criminal  proceeding  under  an  act
of  Congress,  jurisdiction  of the  cause—that  is,  the  right
to  take  cognizance  thereof  and  to  decide  all  questions
of law  occurring  therein,  even  the  question  of  the  valid-
ity of  the  act  under  which  the  proceeding  is  had—is
acquired  by the  cireuit  court  simply  by  the  fact  of  the

7

3

existence  of  such  an  act  and  the  allegation  of an  offense
under  it.

The  power  to  hear  and  determine

a  cause  is  jurisdic-
tion;  it  is  coram  judice  whenever  a  case  is  presented
which  brings  this  power  into  action.

United  States  vs.  Arredendo,  6  Pet.  709.
See  also  Cent.  Pac.  R.  R.  Co.  vs.  Placer  Co.,  43  Cal.,

5608-9.

Ex  parte  Bennett,  44  Cal.,  5s.
Kx  parte  Tracy,  25  Vt.,  95,  96.
Ex  parte  Greenough,  51  Vt.,  279,  255.
Ex  parte  O'Connor,  6  Wis.,  288.
King  vs.  Poole,  36  Barb.,  242.
Sheldon  vs.  Newton,  3 Ohio  St.,  499.
Kx  parte  Kellogg,  6  Vt.,  509,  511.

The  writ  of  habeas  corpus  las  not  the  scope  nor  does
it perform  the  otlice  of a  writ  of error  or  appeal.  When
it is alleged  that  a  prisoner  is  in  custody  by  virtue  ot
legal  process,  the  existence  of  the  process  and  its  valid-
ity  upon  its  face  become  a  legitimate  subject  of  inquiry
So  matters  which  en-
under  the  writ  of  habeas  corpus.
title  a  prisoner  to  his  discharge,  such  as  the  expiration
of the  period  of time  for  which  he was  sentenced,  a  par-
don,  or  payment  of the  fine  imposed  as  punishment,  are
subjects  upon  which  the  judge  issuing  the  writ  iInay
hear  proof,  and  when  established  constitute  good  cause
for  the  prisoner’s  discharge.
But  there  can  be  no  re-
examination  of  any  matter  that  goes  to  the  grounds  of
the judgment  upon  which  the  commitment  was  made.

People  vs.  Cavanagh,  2  Parker’s  C.  C.,  650.

Thus,  in  Lr  parte  Fisher  (6  Neb.  R.,  309),  where  on
habeas  corpus  it appeared  that  the  prisoner  was  detained

by the  judgment  and  commitment  of  a  justice  of  the
peace  in  a  criminal  action  under  a  license  law  of  the
State—the  justice  having  jurisdiction  thereunder  where
the  penalty  did  not  exceed  a  certain  sum—the  court  was
asked  to  discharge  the  prisoner  on  the  ground  of the  in-
But  the  court  dismissed  the  writ
validity  of  the  law.
and  remanded  the  prisoner,  holding  that  habeas  corpus
is  not  a  proper  proceeding  to  review  the  judgment  under
which  Ife  was  detained.

,

In  the  opinion  of  the  court  in  that  case  (which  is ex-
actly  in  point  here)  it is remarked  that,  ‘after  judgment
and  commitment  in  a  criminal  action  by an  inferior  court
having  jurisdiction  of the  offense  charged,  we  think  that
habeas  corpus  is not  the  proper  mode  of procedure  to bring
the cause  into  this  court  for  review  upon  alleged  errors
of  law;  for  it  seems  to  us  that,  when  the  validity  of  a
statute  is  controverted,  the  controversy  raises  a  legal
question,  which,  like  all  other  legal  questions  raised  on
the  trial  of  a  cause  in  an  inferior  court,  can  be  reviewed
only  by  the  mode  prescribed  by  law.
To  entertain  ju-
risdiction  in  such  case  upon  a  writ  of  habeas  corpus,  it
would  be  necessary  to  look  beyond  the  judgment  and:
re-examine  the  charges  upon  which  it  was  rendered,  as
well  as  to  review  the  questions  of  law  raised  on  the  trial
and  decided  by the  inferior  court.
If such  practice  were
to  obtain,  then,  indeed,  every  conviction  for  a  criminal
offense  might  be  brought  here  for  review  on  a  writ  of
habeas  corpus.  We  think  it  is  not  within  the  province  of
this  court  to  open  the  door  to  such  a  system  of  practice.
And  we  are  not  prepared  to  say  that,  upon  a  writ  of
habeas  corpus,  we  can  look  beyond  the  judgment  and  re-
examine  the  charges  on  which  it  was  rendered,  or  pro-
nounce  the  judgment  an  absolute  nullity  on  the  ground

6

that  the  constitutionality  of  the  statute  relative  to  the
If  the  validity  of  a  statute
license  law  is controverted.
is brought  in  question  in  an  inferior  court  on  the  trial  of
a  cause,  that  question  must  finally  be  determined  in  the
same  mode  as  other  legal  questions  arising  on  the  trial
of  causes,in  such  court—that  is,  by proceedings  in  error
or  appeal,  as  may  be  most  appropriate  and  allowable  by
law.”

So,  in  the  matter  of  J.  A.  Harris  (47  Mo.,  164¥,  which
was  a  petition  for  habeas  corpus,  it  was  held  that  where
one  has  been  arrested  on  legal  process  by a  court  having
jurisdiction  of  the  person  and  the  offense,  and  is  in  cus-
tody  of  the  proper  officer,  the  constitutionality  of  the
law  under  which  he  was  arrested  will  not  be  inquired
into  on  a  writ  of habeas  corpus.

Agreeably  to  this  doctrine  it  has  — recently  held
by the  Supreme  Court  of  the  United  States  that  where
an  inferior  court  has  jurisdiction  of  the  cause  and  the
person  in  a  criminal  suit,  and  no  writ  of  error  lies  from
the  Supreme  Court,  the  latter  court  will  not  on  habeas
corpus  review  the  legality  of the  proceedings.

Ex  parte  Parks,  93  U.S.,  18.

The  court  there  observes:  ‘To  review  the  decision  of
that  court  (a district  court  of  the  United  States)  by means
of the  writ  of  habeas  corpus  would  be  to  convert  that
writ  into  a  mere  writ  of error,  and  to assume  an  appellate
power  which  has  never  been  conferred  upon  this  court.”
If  a  judgment  is  absolutely  void,  then  habeas  corpus
would  lie  to  release  one  from  imprisonment  under  it;  if
good  until  reversed,  as  is  the  case  here,  habeas  corpus
will  not  prevent  its  execution.
(See  3  Peters,  202;  Ex
p.  Bushnell,  9  Ohio  St.,  183;  Ex  p.  Williamson,  26  Penn.
St.,  9;  People  v.  Orser,  12  Howd.  Pr.  (N.  Y.),  550;  Ex

p.  Baker,  11  Howd.  Pr.,  425-6;  Ex  p.  Prime,  1  Barb.,
349,  350;  People  v.  Cavanagh,  2 Abb.  Pr., 85;  People  v.
McCormack,  4  Parker’s  Crim.  Cas.,  9;  Ex  p.  Van  Aer-
nam,  3  Blatch.,  160.)

An  examination  of  the  earlier  cases  decided  in  the
Supreme  Court  upon  habeas  corpus  will  show  that  the
court,  while  it has  examined  the  regularity  of  proceed-
ings  in  cases  thus  brought  before  it, so  far  as  to  ascer-
tain  whether  the  lower  court  had  jurisdiction  or  has  ex-
ceeded  its  authority,  it has  not  passed  upon  questions
of law,  constitutional  or  other,  which  were  properly  be-
fore  the  judge  at  the  trial.

See  Lx parte  Watkins,  3  Peters,  193;  in  this  case  the
court,  after  reviewing  the  earlier  cases  of Hv parte  Ham-
ilton,  3  Dall.,  17;  Ec  parte  Burtord,  3 Cranch,  477;  and
Hex parte  Bollman  and  Swartwout,  4 Cranch,  75, observes
that  “In  no  one  of these  cases  was  the  prisoner  confined
under  the  judgment  of a  court.”

See  also  same  case,  7 Pet.,  568;  Hx  parte  Kearney,  7
Wheat.,  38;  and  Lv parte  Metzger,  5 How.,  176, in which.
the  court  says:  ** However  erroneous  the  judgment  of
the  court  may  be, either  in  a  civil  or  criminal  case,  if  it
had jurisdiction,  and  the  defendant  has  been  duly  com-
mitted,  under  an  execution  or  sentence,  he  cannot  be
discharged  by  this  writ”  (habeas  corpus).
Ex  parte
Lange,  18  Wall.,  163.

:

In  a  later  case  (Ex  parte  Jackson,  96  U.  S.,  727)  the
court  did,  upon  an  application  for  a  habeas  corpus,  pass
upon  the  constitutionality  of  the  act  under  which  the
petitioner  was  imprisoned,  and  decided  that  it  was  con-
It  is obvious  that  if  such  was  the  opinion
stitutional.
of  the  court,  the  question  whether,  if  unconstitutional,
the  court  could  or  would  have  discharged  the  prisoner

S

when  he  had  been  convicted  by  a  court  of  competent
jurisdiction, did not  necessarily  arise.  The  last  sentence
of  the  opinion  is  apparently  a  waiver  of  that  question.

2.  It  is not  within  the  competency  of a  circuit  justice
or  judge,  on  habeas  corpus,  to  review  a  judgment  of  the
circuit  court  in  a  criminal  proceeding  under  an  act  of
Congress.

In  re  Callicott,  8 Blateh.  C.  C.,  89.

The  case  just  cited  was  an  application  for  a  writ  ot
habeas  corpus,  made  to  the  circuit  judge  of  the  second
circuit,  alleging  that  the  petitioner  was  in  prison  under
sentence  of the  circuit  court  of  the  United  States,  pro-
nounced  in  the  eastern  district  of  New  York  in  a  erim-
inal  prosecution  under  a  statute  of  the  United  States,
&e.  The  judge  (Woodruff),  upon  examination  of author-
ities,  held  that  he  had  no  jurisdiction  to  review  the  judg-
ment  of  the  circuit  court.
‘That  court,”  he  observed,
‘had  jurisdiction  of  the  matters  charged  in  the  indict-
ment,  and  to  determine  whether  the  acts  therein  alleged
constituted  an  offense  against  the  laws  of  the  United
States,  and,  by the  aid  of  a jury,  to  try  and  determine
whether  the  petitioner  was  guilty  of  those  acts.
From
the  judgment  of  that  tribunal  no  appeal  lies  to  me  as
judge.  No  writ  of error  lies  to  me;  and,  if  my  opinion
was  that  the  learned  judges  by whom  the  court  was  held
when  the judgment  was  pronounced  committed  an  error,
I have  no  power  to  revise  or  to  reverse  the  judgment.”
Further  on  he  adds:  ‘‘  No  case  is  cited  to  me  in  which
the  Supreme  Court,  or  any  court  of  the  United  States,
has  countenanced  the  idea  that  a  judge  can,  under  or
by virtue  of the  writ  of habeas  corpus,  practically  reverse
a judgment  of the  circuit  court,  for  error,  and  discharge
a  prisoner  from  its  sentence.”
