10

any  reason  why  all  the  law  of  the  trial  should  not  be
here  investigated  (if  if  may  be  investigated  at  all)  as
that  which  is  strictly  constitutional  in  its  character.
The  same  reason  exists  in  the  one  case  that  exists  in
the  other.
The  meaning  of  the  sentence  would  seem  to
be  that  the  court  must  inquire  whether  or  not  the  pris-
oner  is  detained  in  violation  of  the  forms  provided  by
the  Constitution  and  laws,  as  he  would  be  if  detained
by  a  court  not  having  jurisdiction;  but  whether  the
court  having  jurisdiction  of  the  cause  and  the  prisoner
has  made  an  error  in  its construction  of the  Constitution
or  law  cannot  be  here  re-examined.
Unless  this  con-
struction  be  adopted,  as  the  power  to determine  whether
the  prisoner  is detained  in  violation  of  the  Constitution
or  laws  of  the  United  States  rests  not  alone  with  this
court,  but  with  every justice  of  this  or  of the  district  or
circuit  court,  it would  follow  that  after  a  prisoner  had
been  convicted  by  the  circuit  court  it  would  be  in  the
power  of  the  district  court  to  release  him  because  he
had  been  convicted  in  violation  of  the  Constitution  and
laws,  notwithstanding  such  court  had  jurisdiction  of the
cause,  and  when,  in  point  of  fact,  there  was  simply  a
difference  of  opinion  between  the  judge  of  the  district
court  and  the  judge  of  the  cireuit  court.
And  it  is  not
easy  to  see  either  why,  if  this  construction  be  adopted,
and  this  court  now  holds  that  a  prisoner  is rightfully
detained  by  virtue  of  the  Constitution  and  laws,  any
one  of the  magistrates  named  in  the  habeas  corpus  act
may  not  afterwards  release  him  because  he  is  of opinion
that  the  Constitution  and  laws  have  been  violated.
That  man  cannot  be  said  to  be  convicted  in  violation  of
the  Constitution  or  the  law  who  is  convicted  before  a
tribunal  which  has  general  jurisdiction  of offenses  cogni-

11

zable  under  such  Constitution  and  law.  An  imprison-
ment  under  a  judgment  cannot  be  unlawful  unless  that
judgment  be  an  absolute  nullity;  and  it is  not  a  nullity
if  the  court  has  general  jurisdiction  of  ‘the  subject,
although  it should  be  erroneous.

Ex  parte  Watkins,  3  Pet.,  202.

But  assuming  that  the  constitutionality  of  the  statu-
tory  provisions,  under  which  the  petitioners  were  tried
and  convicted  in  the  circuit  court,  may  be  inquired  into
in  &@  proceeding  on  habeas  corpus,  we  contend  that  those
provisions  are  valid.

The  statutory  provisions  under  which  the  applicants
were  convicted  are  contained  in  the  following  sections
of  the  Revised  Statutes:

“Src.  5515.  Every  ofticer  of an  election  at  which  any
Representative  or  Delegate  in  Congress  is  voted  for,
whether  such  officer  of  election  be  appointed  or  created
by or  under  any  law  or  authority  of  the  United  States,
or  by  or  under  any  State,  Territorial,  district,  or  mu-
nicipal  law  or  authority,  who  neglects  or  refuses  to  per-
form  any  duty  in  regard  to  such  election  required  of
him  by any  law  of  the  United  States,  or  of  any  State
or  Territory  thereof;  or  who  violates  any  duty  so  im-
posed;  or  who  knowingly  does  any  acts  thereby  unau-
thorized,  with  intent  to  affect  any  such  election,  or  the
result  thereof;  or  who  fraudulently  makes  any  false  cer-
tificate  of  the  result  of  such  election  in  regard  to  such
Representative  or  Delegate;  or  who  withholds,  conceals,
or  destroys  any  certificate  of  record  so  required  by law
respecting  the  election  of  any  such  Representative  or
Delegate;  or  who  neglects  or  refuses  to  make  and  return
such  certificate  as  required  by  law;  or  who  aids,  coun-
sels,  procures,  or  advises  any  voter,  person,  or  officer  to

do  any  act  by  this  or  any  of  the  preceding  sections
made  a  crime,  or  to  emit  to  do  any  duty  the  omission  of
whieh  is  by  this  or  any  of  such  sections  made  a  crime,
or  attempts  to  do  so,  shall  be  punished  as  preseribed  in
section  fifty-five  hundred  and  eleven.”

“SEC.  5022,  Every  person,  whether  with  or  without
any  authority,  power.  or  process,  or  pretended  author-
ity,  power,  or  process,  of  any  State,  Territory,  or  muni-
cipality,  who  obstructs,  hinders,  assaults,  or  by  bribes,
solicitation,  or  otherwise,  interferes  with  or  prevents  the
supervisors  of election,  or  either  of them,  or  the  marshal
or  his  general  or  special  deputies,  or  either  of  them,  in
the  performance  of  any  duty  required  of them,  or  either
of them,  or  which  he  or  they,  or  either  of  them,  may  be
authorized  to  perform  by  any  law  of  the  United  States,
in  the  execution  of  process  or  otherwise,  or  who,  by any
of the  means  before  mentioned,  hinders  or  prevents  the
free  attendance  and  presence  at  such  places  of registra-
tion,  or  at  such  polls  of  election,  or  full  and  free  access
and  egress  to  and  from  any  such  place  of registration  or
poll  of election,  or  in  going  to  and  from  any  sueh  place
of  registration  or  poll  of  election,  or  to  and  from  any
room  where  any  such  registration  or  election  or  canvass
oft  votes,  or  of  making  any  returns  or  certificates  thereof,
may  be  had,  or  who  molests,  interferes  with,  removes,
or  ejects  from  any  such  place  of  registration  or  poll  of
election,  or  of  Canvassing  votes  cast  thereat,  or  of  mak-
ing  returns  or  certificates  thereof,  any  supervisor . of
election,  the  marshal,  or  his  general  or  special  deputies,
or  either  of  them;  or  who  threatens,  or  attempts,  or
offers  so  to  do,  or  refuses  or  neglects  to  aid  and  assist
any  supervisor  of election,  or  the  marshal  or  his  general
or  special  deputies,  or  either  of  them,  in  the  performance
of  his  or  their  duties,  when  required  by  him  or  them,  or
either  of them,  to  give  such  aid  and  assistance,  shall  be
hable  to  instant  arrest  without  process,  and  shall  be
punished  by  imprisonment  not  more  than  two  years,  or
by a  fine  of  not  more  than  three  thousand  dollars,  or  by
both  such  fine  and  imprisonment,  and  shall  pay  the
cost  of  the  prosecution.”

LS

In  support  of  the  validity  of  the  provisions  under  which
the  convictions  were  had,  the  following  points  are  sub-
mitted  :

I.  Congress  has  full  and  unqualified  power  to  regulate

the  manner  of  holding  elections  for  members  thereof.

This  power  is  derived  from  section  4, article  1,  of  the
Constitution,  which  provides:  “The  times,  places,  and
manner
of holding  elections  for  Senators  and  Representa-
tives  shall  be preseribed  in each  State  by the  legislature
thereof;  but  the  Congress  may  at  any  time  by law  make
or  alter  such  regulations,  except  as  to  the  places  of
choosing  Senators.”

Irom  this  provision  it  is  that  the  States  themselves
derive  all  their  power  to  enact  laws  prescribing  the  time,
place,  and  manner  of  holding  elections  for  Representa-
tives  in  Congress;  and  it  will  be  observed  that  the
power  given  to Congress  touching  this  subject  is as  broad
as  that  given  to  the  States.
In  the  grant  to  the  States
the  language  employed  would  seem  to  make  it  impera-
tive  upon  them  to  exercise  the  power  conferred;  while
in  the  grant  to  Congress  the  exercise of the  power  is  left
Yet  this  discretion  is
to  the  diseretion  of  that  body.
subject  to  no  restriction  whatever.
According  to  the
terms  of  the grant,  Congress  may  “  at  any  time,”  by law,
make  regulations  prescribing  the  time,  place,  and  man-
ner  of  holding  such  elections,  or  may  alter  any  regula-
tions  of  that  character  which  have  been  enacted  by  the
legislatures  of  the  States;  and  regulations  enacted  by
Congress  pursuant  to  this  grant  become  by  the  Consti-
Therefore  should
tution  the  supreme  law  of  the  land.
Congress  make  regulations  covering  the  whole  subject-
matter  of  the  grant,  all  State  laws  on  the  same  subject-

matter  would  thereby  be  rendered  inoperative  and  void.
So  if Congress  alter  the  regulations of the  States,  bv  the
enactment  of  regulations  supplemental  thereto  or  other-
wise,  only  such  part  of  the  State  regulations  would
remain  valid  as  were  unaftected  by  the  legislation  of
Congress.

+

.

The  scope  and  effect  of  the  provision  of  the  Coustitu-

tion  above  quoted  are  very  forcibly  presented  in  a  re-
port  from  the  minority  of  the  Committee  of  Elections  of
the  House,  in  the  Thirty-eighth  Congress.
(See  Reports
of  Committees  of  the  House,  first  session  Thirity-eighth
Respecting that  provision  it  is  there
Congress,  No.  60.)
sald:  It  confers  all  necessary  authority  Ower  the  sub-
ject  by  the  terms  “to  regulate  the  times,  places,  and
manner of holding  elections  for  Senators  and  Represen-

It  gives  to  the  legislatures  of  the  States  and

tatives.”
to  Congress,  both,  the  power  to  make  these  regulations ;
and  to  Congress,  also,  the  further  distinct  and  important
power  to  alter  at  any  time  such  regulations  as  the  States
may  make.
It  commands  the  legislatures  of  the  States
to  execute  the  power  with  which  they  areinvested:  but
it  confides  to  Congress  the  discretion  to  exercise,  or  not,
elther  of  the  powers  intrusted  to  it  at  all  times.
No
legislation  of  the  States  can  prevent  Congress  from  act-
ing  on  this  subject,  either  in  the  form  of  making  these
regulations  or  altering  those  of  the  State  legislatures ;
nor  can  the  States  qualify  or  cireumscribe  that  action  in
the  one  mode  or  the  other,  whatever  it  may  be.
But
Congress,  by executing  its  power  to  make  these  regula-
tions,  may  exclude,  and*wholly  put  an  end  to,  the  juris-
diction  of  the  State  legislatures;  or,
in  exercising  its
other  power  to  alter  the  State  regulations  to  any  extent,
it  may  allow  that  jurisdiction  to  operate  within  as  large

a ee ey

lo

or  small  limits  as  it  may  adjudge  to  be  proper:  or  it may
leave  to  these  legislatures  the  exercise  of all  power  over
the  subject  by  itself  abstaining  from  all  legislation  re-
lating  toit.
But  when  Congress  does  legislate,  either  to
make  these  regulations  or  to  alter  such  as  the  State  legis-
latures  may  have  prescribed,  just  so  far  as  it  does  act
its  authority  is  absolute,  paramount,  and  exclusive.
This  character  would  be  imparted  to  its  action  by  the
second  clause  of  the  sixth  article  of  the  Constitution.
In  addition,  the  discretionary  power  with  which  Con-
egress  is  invested  to  make  these  regulations,  or  to  super-
rise  and  alter  those  of  the  State  legislatures  at  a/l  times,
stamps  its  legislation  in  the  premises  with  an  overruling
supremacy.

This  power  of  Congress  to  alter  the  laws  of  the  States
is  isolated.
It  is  confined  to  their  regulations  of  the
times,  places,  and  manner  of  holding  Congressional  elec-
tions,  and  extends  to  no  other  class  of  their  legislation.
But  the  authority  of  the  States  over  this  complex  mat-
ter  the  Constitution  makes  subject  at  all  times  to  the

freest  exercise  on  the  part  ot  Congress  of  its  power  to
When  Congress  acts  there  are
alter  these  State  laws.
not  two  distinct  and  independent  systems of legislation ;
but  by  force  of  the  Constitution  it  becomes  one  both  of
blended  jurisdiction  and  legislation:  and  any  alteration
it  may  make  of  the  State  laws  has  the  identical  effect  it
The  sys-
would  have  if  made  by  the  State  legislature.
tem  is  simple  and  harmonious.
If  Congress  pass  a  law
which,  either  in  terms  and  effect,  amounts  to  an  altera-
tion  of  the  State  regulations,  to  its  full  extent  the  latter,
by operation  of  the  Constitution,  give  place,  and  the  law
of  Congress  is  substituted.

In  volume  2  of  Curtis’s  History  of  the  Constitution.

referring  to  the  above-quoted  section  of  the  Constitu-
tion,  it  is observed:  *“  This  provision  originated  with  the
committee  of  detail;  but,  as  it  was  reported  by  them,
there  was  no  other  authority  reserved  to Congress  itself
than  that  of  altering  the  regulations  of  the  States;  and
this  authority  extended  as  well  to  the  place  of  choosing
the  Senators  as  to all  the  other  circumstances  ot  the  elee-
tion.
In  the  convention,  however,  the  authority of Con-
eress  was  extended  beyond  the  alteration  of State  regu-
lations,  so  as  to  embrace  a  power  to  make  rules  as  well
as  to  alter  those  made  by  the  States.
But  the  place  ot
choosing  Senators  was  excepted  altogether  from  this
restraining  authority  and  left  to  the  States.
Mr.  Madi-
son,  in  his  minutes,  adds  the  explanation  that  the  power
of  Congress  to  make  regulations  was  supplied  in  order
to  enable  them  to  regulate  the  elections,  if  the  States
But  the  text  of  the  Con-
should  fail  or  refuse  todo  so.
stitution,  as  finally  settled,  gives  authority  to  Congress
at  ‘any  time’  to  ‘make  or  alter  such  regulations;’  and
this  would  seem  to  confer  a  power  which,  when  exer-
cised,  must  be  paramount.  whether  a  State  regulation
exists  at  the  time  or  not.”

See  also  volume  1, Story  on  Const.,  section  515  et  seq.,
wherein  are  presented  the  leading  considerations  for
leaving  it  to  the  discretion  of  Congress  to  determine
when  and  to  what  extent  the  power  referred  to  should
be  exercised.

Also  the  Federalist,  Nos.  58  and  59.

II.  The  power  of Congress  to  regulate  the  manner  of
holding  elections  for  its  members  includes  authority  to
adopt  whatever  measures  may  be  deemed  necessary  or
expedient  to  conducting  such  elections  and  determining
the  result  thereof.

|

wr

This  proposition  covers  the  enactment  of  laws  con-
cerning  the  registration  of  voters,  the  casting  and  can-
vassing  of  votes,  the  declaration  of  the  result,  &e.,  and
also  providing  for  the  execution  of  these  laws  through
officers  charged  with  this  duty.

It  is submitted  that  if a State  should  neglect  or  refuse
to make  any  regulations  on  the  subject  of  such  elections,
and  Congress  should  in  consequence  supply  regulations
of its  own,  the  latter  regulations,  in  order  to  effect  their
purpose,  must  of  necessity  embrace  the  whole  process
of  election;  and,  in  this  view,  as  well  might  their  re-
quirements  extend  to  the  registration  of  voters  as  to  the
casting  and  canvassing  of  votes.
But  in  the  contingen-
cies  adverted  to  (the  neglect  or  refusal  of  the  State)  the
power  of  Congress  over  this  subject  would  be  no  greater
than  it  woulda  be  where  such  contingencies  did  not  exist,
i.  €..  Where  regulations  were  already  provided  by  the
State.
As  these  regulations  of  the  State  might  be
wholly  superseded  by others  enacted  by Congress  in  the

exercise  of  that  power,  SO  Congress  might  alter  them
by  making  additions  thereto  or  otherwise,  in  the  exer-
cise  of  the  same  power;  and  alterations  of  State  regu-
lations  so  made  by  Congress  might  be  limited  to  any
one  of  the  subjects  to  which  such  regulations  applied
(whether  it  be  the  registration  of  voters,  the  casting  or
canvassing  of  votes,  the  declaration  of  the  result,  &ce.),
or  they  might  be  extended  to  the  whole  of  those  sub-
jects.

When  the  laws  of  the  State  as  to  the  election  of mem-
bers  of Congress  are  altered  by  Congress,  these  altera-
tions  are,  as  it  were,  engrafted  upon  the  State  laws,  so
that  together  they  form  a  complete  whole.

 *

1s

Unquestionably,  Congress  has  the  right  to  provide
officers  for  carrying  into  effect  such  regulations  as  it
may  adopt,  in  the  exercise  of the  power  referred  to.
In
United  States  vs.  Clayton  (2  Dill,  227)  it  is said  by
the  court:  *  Under  the  Constitution  (art.  1, sec.  4), Con-
cress  has  the  undoubted  power  to  provide  its  own  offi-
cers  for  the  holding  and  conduct  of  Congressional  elee-

=

tions.

III.  Congress  can  punish  any  violation  of  the  laws
enacted  under  the  aforesaid  power,  and  also  any  inter-
ference  with  the  officers  charged  with  the  execution  of
such  laws.

Power  is  expressly  given  to  Congress  by  the  Consti-
tution  to  make  all  laws  which  shall  be  necessary  and
proper  for  carrying  into  execution  all  other  powers
Nearly  all  the  criminal  legislation  of
eranted  thereto.
Congress  rests  upon  that  power.

Thus  Congress  has  power  to  levy  and  collect  taxes,
duties,  imports,  and  excises;  and,  as  necessary  and
proper  for  carrying  into  execution  this  power,  the
numerous  punishments  found  in  the  revenue  laws  were
enacted.
Again,  Congress  has  power  to  regulate  com-
merece;  and,  as  necessary  and  proper  for  carrying  this
power  into  execution,  the  various  penal  provisions  in
the  regulations  established  thereunder  were  enacted.
Again,  Congress  has  the  power  to  establish  post-offices
and  post-roads;  and  the  statutes  which  punish  the many
offenses  relating  to the  postal  service  were  enacted,  as  nec~
essary  and  proper  for  carrying  into  execution  the  last-
mentioned  power.

United  States  vs.  Coombs,  12  Pet.,  78.
United  States  vs.  Fox,  95  U.  S.,  670.

19

Kix  parte  Jackson,  supra.
So  Congress,  being  Invested  with  power  to  regulate
the  manner  of  holding  elections  for  members  thereof,
can  enforce  the  regulations  which  it  may  prescribe
thereunder  by punishing  those  who  violate  them,  or  who
interfere  with  the  officers  while  engaged  in  executing
them.

U.  States  vs.  Johnson,  2 Saw.,  482.
U.  States  vs.  Souders,  2 Abbott’s  U.S.  Rep.,  £56.

[V.  The  statutory  provisions  under  which  the  convic-
tions  were  had  are  appropriate  for  carrying  into  effect
the  power  granted  to  Congress  to  regulate  the  manner
of holding  elections  for  Representatives.

What  those  provisions  are  is  to  be  ascertained  by  an
examination  of  the  specific  charges  on  which  the  peti-
tioners  were  convicted.
Of  these  charges  (as  shown  by
the  indictments  and  proceedings  thereon)  asynopsis  will
now  be  given.

The  petitioner  Bowers  was  convicted  on  the  second
count  in the  indictment  against  him,  which  charged  that
he—

‘Unlawfully  did  obstruct,  hinder,  and  by  the  use  of
his  power  and  authority  as  such  judge  as  atoresaid
(which  judge  he  then  and  there  was)  interfere  with  and
prevent  the  said  supervisor  of  election  in  the  perform-
ance  of a  certain  duty  in  respect  to  said  election  required
of  him,  and  which  he  was  then  and  there  authorized  to

section  35515  or  on  section  5522  of the  Revised  Statutes.

as  is  made  the  foundation  of the  counts  framed  upon  it:

cess,  of any  State,  *

With  regard  to  section  5515,  the  only  question  that
arises  here  is  this:  Can  State  officers  of  an  election  at
which  members  of  Congress  are  chosen  be  subjected  to
punishment  by Congress  for  the  commission  of  acts  wn-
authorized  by any  law  either  of the  State  or  of the  United

~

ee ee ee

i
Blase
gery.
ON Rett

~~
20

States,  where  the  acts  directly  relate  to  such  election,
and  are  committed  with  wrongful  intent  to  affect  the  same
or  the  result  thereof?

We  submit  in  answer,  that  the  matter  to  which  the
unauthorized  acts  relate  (a Congressional  election)  being
one  in  respect  to  which  Congress  has  power  to legislate,
and  therefore  within  the  jurisdiction  of  the  United
States,  such  acts  may  be  punished  by Congress—unless
the  officers  of election  can  claim  exemption  on  the  ground
(not  that  they  acted  pursuant  to  a  law  of  the  State,  for
the  case  supposes  the  contrary,  but)  that  they  derived
their  appointments  from  the  State!

In  United  States  vs.  Fox  (95  U.S.,  672)  the  principle  .
is  fully  recognized  that  an  act  committed  within  a  State,
which  has  relation  to  any  matter  that  concerns  the
United  States  and  is  subject  to  the  control  of  Congress,
‘an  be  made  an  offense  against  the  United  States.  And
as  to the  claim  of exemption  by the election  officers  on  the
ground  mentioned,  the  principle  upon  which  this  rests
would  give  immunity  from  punishment  to  every  State
official,  high  or  low,  who  might  violate  a  United  States
It  will  hardly  be  contended  that  officers  or  em-
law.
ployés  of  a  State  are,  by their  position,  made  irrespon-
sible  to  the  criminal  laws  of  the  United  States.

Passing  now  to  section  5522,  the  question  which  arises
thereon  is,  Whether  a  State  officer  of  an  election  at
which  members  of Congress  are  chosen  can  be  punished
by  Congress,  (1)  for  obstructing,  hindering,  or  interfering
with  (by the  use  of his  power  and  authority  as such  State
officer)  certain  United  States  officers  (viz,  supervisors  of
election,  the  marshal,  and  his  general  and  special  depu-
ties)  in  the  performance  of any  duty  required  of  those

24

officers,  or  which  they  may  be  authorized  to  perform,  re-
specting  such  election,  by a  law  of the  United  States,  or
(2)  for  hindering  or  preventing  (by  the  use  of  his  power
and  authority  as  such  State  officer)  the  free  attendance
and  presence  of  those  ofiicers  at  the  poll  of such  election,
or  free  access  and  egress  to  and  from  any  room  where
such  election  is  had,  in  the  performance  of  any  duty
thereat  devolved  upon  them  by  a  law  of  the  United
states.

The  answer  to  this  is  obvious.

If  Congress,  in  the
exercise  of  the  power  to  regulate  the  manner  of holding
elections  for  Representatives,  can  create  officers  to  su-
pervise  the  voting  and  canvassing  of  the  votes  at  such
elections  under  regulations  of  its  own  (a  proposition
which  we  think  is  incontrovertible),  it  can  enforce  these
regulations  by means  of  penalties  or  punishments.  Au-
thority  to  make  law  impliedly  confers  authority  to  im-
pose  penalties  for  violation  of  the  law.  And  the  penal
provisions  enacted  to  the  end  of  enforcing  such  regula-
tions,  it is  submitted,  may  be  made  to  embrace  all  per-
sons  alike—those  acting  with  equally  as  well  as  those
acting  without  authority  or  power  from  any  State.
In
this  connection  it  is to  be  observed  that  the  authority
or  power  derived  from  the  State  is  apparent  only,  not
real,  in  so  far  as  it comes  in conflict  with  the  regulations
of  Congress  and  their  enforcement;  for  these  regula-
tions,  being  paramount,  to  that  extent  annul  the  author-
ity or  power  given  by the  State.

See,  in  reference  to  this  subject,  the  latter  part  of the
opinion of Justice  Strong  in  the  case  of  United  States
vs.  Given,  17  Int.  Revenue  Record,  p. 190;  also  the  cases
cited  supra  under  point  III.

25

Upon  the  foregoing  considerations  it is submitted  that
the  application  of  the  petitioners  for  the  writ  of  habeas
corpus  should  be  denied.

CHARLES  DEVENS,

Attorney-General.
